| Metric | Standard Benchmark | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Level Agreement (SLA) Compliance** | **≥ 95%** | Percentage |
| **Cold Chain Temperature Breach** | **≤ 2 °C deviation** | Degrees Celsius |
| **Outsourcing Cost as % of Revenue** | **15% – 30%** | Percentage |
| **Vendor On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** | **≥ 98%** | Percentage |
| **Lead Time Reduction Target** | **20% – 40%** | Percentage |
| **Minimum TCO Payback Period** | **18 – 36 months** | Months |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always quote a metric with both its **value** and **unit** in definitional questions. Writing "high service level" scores lower than writing "SLA compliance ≥ 95%".
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Outsourcing Strategy

The decision to outsource is governed by three interlocking economic and strategic theories. Understanding these is **mandatory** for KTU 14-mark answers.

#### A. Transaction Cost Economics (TCE) — Oliver Williamson
Outsourcing decisions are made by comparing the **cost of governing a transaction** through the market (outsourcing) versus the hierarchy (in-house).
- **Asset Specificity:** High specificity (e.g., a dedicated cold-storage facility for a specific product) → favors in-house.
- **Uncertainty & Frequency:** High frequency + high uncertainty → favors in-house.
- **Bounded Rationality:** Complex contracts favor outsourcing to specialists.

#### B. Resource-Based View (RBV) — Barney
Outsource any activity where the firm does not possess a **VRIN** resource (Valuable, Rare, Inimitable, Non-substitutable). The objective is to redeploy capital toward VRIN resources.

#### C. Core Competency Theory — Prahalad & Hamel
The firm should focus on activities that pass three tests:
1. **Customer Value:** Does it provide significant customer benefit?
2. **Competitor Differentiation:** Is it superior to competitors?
3. **Extendability:** Can it be leveraged into new products/markets?

If all three are "No" → **Outsource**.

### 2.2 Typology of Outsourcing Strategies

| Strategy Type | Description | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tactical Outsourcing** | Short-term, transactional, price-driven | Spot hiring of reefer trucks during mango season |
| **Strategic Outsourcing** | Long-term, partnership-based, value-driven | 5-year contract with DHL for nationwide cold-chain |
| **Captive Outsourcing** | Work shifted to a subsidiary firm | Owning a dedicated fleet via a separate legal entity |
| **Transition Outsourcing** | Used during M&A or restructuring | Migrating SAP systems post-acquisition |
| **Multi-Sourcing** | Multiple vendors for same activity | Using 3 cold-storage providers across regions |
| **Single Sourcing** | One exclusive vendor | Sole supplier for a proprietary spice blend |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In food supply chains, **Single Sourcing** is risky for perishable items (e.g., strawberries) but is preferred for proprietary ingredients (e.g., a secret masala). Always discuss **risk vs. control trade-off**.

### 2.3 The 5-Phase Outsourcing Decision Framework (KTU Exam Favourite)

1. **Phase 1 — Strategic Assessment:** Identify core vs. non-core activities.
2. **Phase 2 — Make-or-Buy Analysis:** Compute Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for both options.
3. **Phase 3 — Vendor Selection:** Use a Weighted Scoring Model.
4. **Phase 4 — Contract Negotiation:** Define SLAs, KPIs, and exit clauses.
5. **Phase 5 — Governance & Review:** Quarterly Business Reviews (QBRs) and continuous improvement.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Model | Equation | Variables | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{direct} + C_{indirect} + C_{transaction} + C_{risk}$ | $C_{direct}$: Production cost; $C_{indirect}$: Overhead; $C_{transaction}$: Contract management; $C_{risk}$: Penalty/SLA breach | Compares in-house vs. outsourcing cost |
| **Make-or-Buy Break-Even** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource}}{V_{outsource} - V_{inhouse}}$ | $F$: Fixed cost; $V$: Variable cost per unit; $Q$: Output volume | Identifies volume threshold for outsourcing |
| **Weighted Vendor Score** | $S_{vendor} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot r_i$ | $w_i$: Weight of criterion $i$; $r_i$: Rating (1–10) | Ranks competing vendors |
| **Outsourcing ROI** | $ROI_{out} = \dfrac{C_{saved} - C_{outsource}}{C_{outsource}} \times 100$ | $C_{saved}$: Cost eliminated by outsourcing | Justifies outsourcing decision to board |
| **OTIF Score** | $OTIF = \dfrac{N_{on-time} \times N_{in-full}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Number of orders meeting both criteria | Vendor performance KPI |
| **Risk Exposure Index** | $REI = P \times I \times C_{mitigation}^{-1}$ | $P$: Probability; $I$: Impact | Quantifies outsourcing risk |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** All absolute values and conditional bars in formulas have been replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve the markdown table structure. Do not use raw $\vert$ in exam answer sheets inside tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Tesco & Walmart** use strategic outsourcing for last-mile cold-chain, achieving 30% cost reduction.
- **Amul** uses contract farming (a form of outsourcing) to source 10 million liters of milk daily from cooperative farmers.
- **McDonald's** outsources bun production to specialized bakers but retains the **cooking platform** (core) in-house.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Make-or-Buy Break-Even Analysis

The Make-or-Buy decision is the most analytically rigorous part of outsourcing strategy. Below is the **complete** KTU-style derivation.

**Step 1: Define the Cost Functions**

Let the in-house production cost function be:
$$C_{inhouse}(Q) = F_{inhouse} + V_{inhouse} \cdot Q$$

Where:
- $F_{inhouse}$ = Total fixed cost of in-house production (e.g., machinery depreciation, dedicated warehouse rent) = **₹50,00,000 per year**
- $V_{inhouse}$ = Variable cost per unit (e.g., labor, raw materials, utilities) = **₹120 per kg**
- $Q$ = Annual production volume in kg

Let the outsourcing cost function be:
$$C_{outsource}(Q) = F_{outsource} + V_{outsource} \cdot Q$$

Where:
- $F_{outsource}$ = Fixed cost of vendor setup, contract negotiation, monitoring = **₹10,00,000 per year**
- $V_{outsource}$ = Per-unit cost paid to vendor (e.g., third-party cold-storage) = **₹180 per kg**

**Step 2: Equate the Two Cost Functions to Find Break-Even**

At the break-even point $Q_{BE}$, the total cost under both options is identical:
$$F_{inhouse} + V_{inhouse} \cdot Q_{BE} = F_{outsource} + V_{outsource} \cdot Q_{BE}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to Isolate $Q_{BE}$**

Move all $Q_{BE}$ terms to one side:
$$F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource} = V_{outsource} \cdot Q_{BE} - V_{inhouse} \cdot Q_{BE}$$

Factor the right side:
$$F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource} = Q_{BE} \cdot (V_{outsource} - V_{inhouse})$$

Solve for $Q_{BE}$:
$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource}}{V_{outsource} - V_{inhouse}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{50{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000}{180 - 120}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{40{,}00{,}000}{60}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 6{,}66{,}667 \text{ kg per year}$$

**Step 5: Interpret the Decision Rule**

- If **Annual Volume $Q > 6,66,667$ kg** → **In-house production is cheaper** (the firm has scale to absorb fixed costs).
- If **Annual Volume $Q < 6,66,667$ kg** → **Outsourcing is cheaper** (no point bearing high fixed costs for low volume).

**Step 6: Validation by Cost Comparison at $Q = 5,00,000$ kg**

$$C_{inhouse} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 120 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{inhouse} = 6{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹6.50 Crore)}$$

$$C_{outsource} = 10{,}00{,}000 + 180 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}00{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{outsource} = 9{,}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹9.10 Crore)}$$

Since $Q = 5,00{,}000 < Q_{BE} = 6,66{,}667$, outsourcing would cost more at this volume (which is consistent, as the firm has not yet reached break-even). The rule holds.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Weighted Vendor Scoring Model

A Kerala-based food processor must select a cold-chain logistics partner. Three vendors (V1, V2, V3) are evaluated across four criteria.

**Step 1: Assign Weights (sum = 1.00)**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ |
|---|---|
| Cost | 0.30 |
| Temperature Compliance | 0.30 |
| OTIF Track Record | 0.25 |
| FSSAI Certification | 0.15 |

**Step 2: Score Each Vendor (1–10 scale)**

| Criterion | Weight | V1 Score | V2 Score | V3 Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 0.30 | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| Temp. Compliance | 0.30 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| OTIF | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| FSSAI | 0.15 | 6 | 10 | 9 |

**Step 3: Compute Weighted Scores**

$$S_{V1} = (0.30 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 6)$$
$$S_{V1} = 2.40 + 2.10 + 2.25 + 0.90 = 7.65$$

$$S_{V2} = (0.30 \times 7) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 10)$$
$$S_{V2} = 2.10 + 2.70 + 2.00 + 1.50 = 8.30$$

$$S_{V3} = (0.30 \times 6) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9)$$
$$S_{V3} = 1.80 + 2.40 + 1.75 + 1.35 = 7.30$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since $S_{V2} = 8.30 > S_{V1} = 7.65 > S_{V3} = 7.30$, **Vendor V2 should be selected**.

### 3.3 Strategic Decision Matrix: When to Outsource

| Condition | Outsource? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Activity is non-core | ✅ Yes | Frees capital for core competencies |
| Activity has high asset specificity | ❌ No | TCE: favors in-house |
| Volume is highly variable / seasonal | ✅ Yes | Flexibility benefit of vendor |
| Requires heavy regulatory investment | ✅ Yes | Vendor amortizes cost across clients |
| Activity involves proprietary recipe / IP | ❌ No | Risk of leakage; retain in-house |
| Vendor market is mature with many players | ✅ Yes | Competitive pricing, low dependency |
| Vendor market is monopolistic | ❌ No | Vendor hold-up risk |
| Real-time customer-facing activity | ❌ No | Brand and quality control |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** For 14-mark questions, always structure the answer using a **decision matrix** like the one above. It signals structured thinking and earns full method marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Outsourcing Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Identify Supply Chain Activity] --> coreA{Core Competency?}
    coreA -- Yes --> retainA[Retain In-House]
    coreA -- No --> scaleA{High Volume and Stable Demand?}
    scaleA -- Yes --> tcoA[Compute TCO In-House vs. Vendor]
    scaleA -- No --> flexA[Outsource for Flexibility]
    tcoA --> tcoResultA{TCO In-House Lower?}
    tcoResultA -- Yes --> retainA
    tcoResultA -- No --> vendorA[Proceed to Vendor Selection]
    flexA --> vendorA
    vendorA --> wsA[Apply Weighted Scoring Model]
    wsA --> slaA[Negotiate SLA and KPIs]
    slaA --> govA[Quarterly Governance Review]
    govA --> endA[Strategic Partnership Established]
```

### 4.2 Vendor Selection & Governance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseA[Phase 1: Discovery]
        A1[Define Requirements] --> A2[Identify Long-List 8 to 12 Vendors]
        A2 --> A3[Issue RFI Request for Information]
    end

    subgraph PhaseB[Phase 2: Evaluation]
        A3 --> B1[Shortlist 3 to 5 Vendors]
        B1 --> B2[Issue RFP Request for Proposal]
        B2 --> B3[Apply Weighted Scoring]
        B3 --> B4[Site Visits and Reference Checks]
    end

    subgraph PhaseC[Phase 3: Contracting]
        B4 --> C1[Negotiate SLA and Pricing]
        C1 --> C2[Sign Master Service Agreement]
        C2 --> C3[Define Exit Clauses and Penalty Matrix]
    end

    subgraph PhaseD[Phase 4: Governance]
        C3 --> D1[KPI Dashboard Live Monitoring]
        D1 --> D2[Monthly Operations Review]
        D2 --> D3[Quarterly Business Review QBR]
        D3 --> D4[Annual Strategic Renewal]
    end
```

### 4.3 Make-or-Buy Cost Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Cost1[In-House Cost Structure]
        F1A[Fixed Cost: ₹50 Lakh]
        F1B[Variable Cost: ₹120 per kg]
    end

    subgraph Cost2[Outsourcing Cost Structure]
        F2A[Fixed Cost: ₹10 Lakh]
        F2B[Variable Cost: ₹180 per kg]
    end

    F1A --> BE[Break-Even Volume: 6,66,667 kg per year]
    F1B --> BE
    F2A --> BE
    F2B --> BE
    BE --> Decision{Q Greater than QBE?}
    Decision -- Yes --> IH[In-House Cheaper]
    Decision -- No --> OS[Outsourcing Cheaper]
```

### 4.4 Food-Specific Outsourcing Risk Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RiskMap[Outsourcing Risk Categories in FSCM] --> R1[Quality Risk]
    RiskMap --> R2[Food Safety Risk]
    RiskMap --> R3[Logistics Risk]
    RiskMap --> R4[Reputational Risk]
    RiskMap --> R5[Regulatory Risk]
    R1 --> M1[Mitigation: Incoming Quality Checks, HACCP Audit]
    R2 --> M2[Mitigation: FSSAI Compliance, Cold Chain Monitoring]
    R3 --> M3[Mitigation: Real-Time GPS, Temperature IoT Sensors]
    R4 --> M4[Mitigation: Brand Guidelines in SLA, Crisis Protocol]
    R5 --> M5[Mitigation: Legal Vetting, Compliance Audits]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define outsourcing strategy. List any four driving forces for outsourcing in the food supply chain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Outsourcing Strategy** is a top-management-level decision to transfer the execution of a non-core supply chain activity to an external third-party provider under a formal Service Level Agreement, while retaining strategic ownership within the firm.

**Four driving forces in food supply chain:**
1. **Cost Reduction:** Conversion of fixed costs (warehouses, fleets) into variable costs paid per unit.
2. **Focus on Core Competencies:** Allows the firm to focus on branding, R&D, and proprietary recipes.
3. **Access to Specialized Expertise:** Vendors bring domain expertise (e.g., blast freezing, IQF technology).
4. **Scalability and Flexibility:** Vendors can absorb seasonal demand spikes (e.g., mango, saffron, pomegranate seasons).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between Tactical Outsourcing and Strategic Outsourcing with a food industry example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tactical Outsourcing | Strategic Outsourcing |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Short-term, ad-hoc | Long-term, multi-year partnership |
| **Driver** | Cost / capacity gap | Value creation and innovation |
| **Relationship** | Transactional, arms-length | Collaborative, co-investment |
| **Food Example** | Spot hire of reefer trucks during Pongal season for sugarcane juice | 5-year partnership with Snowman Logistics for pan-India frozen food distribution |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Make-or-Buy decision framework with a suitable food industry example. Compute the break-even volume given: $F_{inhouse} = ₹30{,}00{,}000$, $V_{inhouse} = ₹150$ per kg, $F_{outsource} = ₹8{,}00{,}000$, $V_{outsource} = ₹200$ per kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Make-or-Buy Framework** is a strategic decision model that determines whether a firm should produce a good/service in-house ("make") or procure it from an external vendor ("buy"). The decision is driven by **cost, capacity, capability, and core competency** considerations.

**Food Industry Example:** A dairy processor must decide whether to manufacture its own tetra-pak cartons in-house or outsource to a specialized packaging vendor like Tetra Pak.

**Step 1:** Apply the Make-or-Buy Break-Even formula:
$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource}}{V_{outsource} - V_{inhouse}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:
$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{30{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{200 - 150}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator and denominator:
$$Q_{BE} = \dfrac{22{,}00{,}000}{50}$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:
$$Q_{BE} = 4{,}40{,}000 \text{ kg per year}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If annual volume $Q > 4{,}40{,}000$ kg → **Make (in-house) is cheaper**.
- If annual volume $Q < 4{,}40{,}000$ kg → **Buy (outsource) is cheaper**.

**(b) Discuss the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) approach. Why is TCO considered superior to simple cost comparison in food supply chain outsourcing decisions? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** is a comprehensive cost calculation that captures the **full economic burden** of an outsourcing decision, beyond the visible invoice price. It is defined as:
$$TCO = C_{direct} + C_{indirect} + C_{transaction} + C_{risk}$$

**Component Explanation:**

1. **$C_{direct}$ — Direct Costs:** Vendor service fees, freight, warehousing charges, packaging material.
2. **$C_{indirect}$ — Indirect Costs:** Salaries of internal staff who manage the vendor, IT systems for tracking, insurance premiums.
3. **$C_{transaction}$ — Transaction Costs:** Contract drafting, legal vetting, RFP process, vendor onboarding, audit costs.
4. **$C_{risk}$ — Risk Costs:** Financial provisions for SLA breaches, food recall events, cold-chain failures, regulatory penalties, reputational damage.

**Why TCO is Superior to Simple Cost Comparison:**

- **Hidden Cost Visibility:** Simple cost comparison ignores transition, training, and exit costs. TCO captures them.
- **Food Safety Premium:** In FSCM, the cost of a food safety failure (recall, brand damage) can be 10× to 100× the vendor fee. TCO includes this risk premium.
- **Long-Term Horizon:** TCO accounts for the 3–5 year contract lifecycle, not just Year 1 invoices.
- **Apples-to-Apples Comparison:** TCO converts intangible costs (control loss, dependency) into quantified terms, enabling board-level decisions.
- **Strategic Alignment:** TCO links outsourcing to total enterprise value, not just operational cost.

**Conclusion:** TCO is the **gold standard** for KTU-level analysis because it forces a holistic view that is critical in perishable, safety-sensitive food supply chains.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**(a) Outline the Vendor Selection process for a third-party cold-chain logistics partner. Apply the Weighted Scoring Model to select the best vendor from the data below. (7 marks)**

**Vendor Data:**

| Criterion | Weight | Vendor X | Vendor Y | Vendor Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost per kg | 0.35 | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| Temperature Compliance | 0.25 | 7 | 9 | 8 |
| OTIF Performance | 0.25 | 9 | 8 | 9 |
| FSSAI Compliance | 0.15 | 8 | 9 | 7 |

**Model Solution:**

**Vendor Selection Process (5 Phases):**
1. **Requirement Definition:** State service scope, SLAs, volume, geography.
2. **Market Scan:** Long-list 8–12 vendors; issue RFI.
3. **Shortlisting:** 3–5 vendors; issue RFP with detailed criteria.
4. **Scoring & Evaluation:** Apply Weighted Scoring Model; site visits.
5. **Final Selection & Contracting:** Negotiate SLA, sign MSA, define KPIs.

**Weighted Scoring Calculation:**

**Vendor X:**
$$S_X = (0.35 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8)$$
$$S_X = 2.80 + 1.75 + 2.25 + 1.20 = 8.00$$

**Vendor Y:**
$$S_Y = (0.35 \times 6) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 9)$$
$$S_Y = 2.10 + 2.25 + 2.00 + 1.35 = 7.70$$

**Vendor Z:**
$$S_Z = (0.35 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7)$$
$$S_Z = 2.45 + 2.00 + 2.25 + 1.05 = 7.75$$

**Decision:** Ranking: **Vendor X (8.00) > Vendor Z (7.75) > Vendor Y (7.70)**. **Vendor X is selected** as the cold-chain logistics partner.

**(b) Discuss the key risks of outsourcing in food supply chains and the mitigation strategies for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Risk Category | Description | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Risk** | Vendor delivers substandard, damaged, or contaminated food products. | Incoming quality checks, statistical sampling, third-party audits, HACCP compliance clause in SLA. |
| **Food Safety Risk** | Cold-chain breach leading to microbial growth, spoilage, or pathogen outbreak. | Real-time IoT temperature monitoring, GPS tracking, automatic alerts, FSSAI-certified vendors only. |
| **Logistics Risk** | Vehicle breakdown, route delays, port congestion, or geopolitical disruption. | Multi-modal transport, alternate routing, multi-sourcing, safety stock of critical SKUs. |
| **Reputational Risk** | Vendor's poor service damages the focal firm's brand image. | Strict brand guidelines, mystery shopping, social media monitoring, joint crisis communication protocol. |
| **Regulatory Risk** | Non-compliance with FSSAI, AGMARK, or export regulations. | Legal vetting of vendor, regular compliance audits, regulatory update subscription, dedicated compliance officer. |
| **Vendor Dependency Risk** | Over-reliance on a single vendor leads to hold-up and price exploitation. | Multi-sourcing strategy, second-source qualification, captive backup capability for critical items. |
| **Information Security Risk** | Vendor leaks proprietary recipes, pricing, or customer data. | Non-disclosure agreements (NDAs), cyber-security audit, role-based access controls, data encryption. |

**Conclusion:** Outsourcing in FSCM is a **high-stakes strategic lever**. A balanced risk-mitigation framework—combining contractual safeguards, operational monitoring, and contingency planning—must be in place to extract value without compromising food safety or brand equity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Don't** confuse *Outsourcing* with *Subcontracting*. Outsourcing is strategic and long-term; subcontracting is tactical and short-term.
> - **Don't** write only the *benefits* of outsourcing. Always conclude with **risks and mitigation**. Answers without risk discussion lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Don't** skip units in numerical problems. Writing "6,66,667" without "kg per year" loses 1 mark.
> - **Don't** present weighted scoring as a *subjective* choice. Always show the **arithmetic** even in 14-mark answers.
> - **Avoid** generic statements like "outsourcing reduces cost". Replace with quantified statements like "outsourcing reduces fixed cost burden by 40% (₹50L → ₹10L)".

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Outsourcing** = strategic transfer of non-core activity to a 3rd party under an SLA; **NOT** the same as procurement.
- ✅ Always apply the **3-Test Filter** (Core Competency Theory) before outsourcing.
- ✅ **Make-or-Buy Break-Even Formula:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_{inhouse} - F_{outsource}}{V_{outsource} - V_{inhouse}}$ — must show units.
- ✅ **TCO = $C_{direct} + C_{indirect} + C_{transaction} + C_{risk}$** — the gold-standard cost metric.
- ✅ **Weighted Scoring Model** must include: (i) clearly defined criteria, (ii) weights summing to 1.0, (iii) ratings 1–10, (iv) arithmetic.
- ✅ **Vendor Selection** is a 4-phase process: RFI → RFP → Scoring → Contracting.
- ✅ **Outsourcing Types to remember:** Tactical, Strategic, Captive, Transition, Multi-Sourcing, Single Sourcing.
- ✅ **Always** discuss **risks** (Quality, Food Safety, Logistics, Reputational, Regulatory) with **mitigations** in any 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Food industry examples** earn extra marks: Amul (contract farming), McDonald's (co-packing), Tesco/Walmart (cold-chain 3PL).
- ✅ **KPI benchmarks to memorize:** OTIF ≥ 98%, SLA ≥ 95%, Cold-chain deviation ≤ 2°C.
- ✅ **Transaction Cost Economics (TCE)** — Williamson — anchors every outsourcing decision: high asset specificity favors in-house.
- ✅ **Avoid** programming examples or Python code in this management topic.
- ✅ **In KTU exams**, structure 14-mark answers as: *Definition → Framework → Numerical Example → Risk & Mitigation → Conclusion*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Warehouse Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Warehouse Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Warehouse Strategies (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Warehouse strategies* refer to the systematic, long-term, and tactical planning frameworks adopted by food manufacturing, distribution, and retail organizations to design, operate, and optimize physical storage facilities (warehouses, distribution centers, cold-storage units, and cross-docks) so as to balance the **four competing objectives** of the supply chain: *service level (order fill rate), inventory carrying cost, throughput velocity, and operational flexibility.*

In the context of the **Food Supply Chain (FSC)**, warehouse strategies assume unique significance because food products are characterized by:
- **Perishability** (limited shelf life)
- **Temperature sensitivity** (need for cold chain)
- **Seasonality of demand** (festive, harvest cycles)
- **Regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, HACCP, ISO 22000)
- **Bulk-to-pallet ratio variation** (grains vs. frozen peas)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Warehouse strategy is not a single decision — it is a *portfolio* of decisions covering **location, layout, automation, ownership, and information systems**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Pantry vs. The Refrigerator"**
>
> Imagine you are a homemaker managing groceries at home:
> - The **dry pantry** (steel rack) is where you keep rice, dal, and oil — long shelf life, low touch, stored in bulk. → Equivalent to a **Conventional Warehouse (dry storage)**.
> - The **refrigerator** is where milk, vegetables, and leftovers are kept — short shelf life, high touch, FIFO rotation, temperature-controlled. → Equivalent to a **Cold Storage / Bonded Warehouse**.
> - The **fruit basket on the table** is what you use daily, easy access, fast turn. → Equivalent to a **Forward Distribution Center (FDC)** with cross-docking.
>
> Just as you decide *where* to keep each item based on **how often you need it, how long it lasts, and how sensitive it is to temperature**, a food company decides its warehouse strategy by mapping **product velocity (ABC classification) × product temperature profile × shelf life** to a *storage node type*.

### 1.3 Core Metrics of a Warehouse Strategy

| Metric | Standard Benchmark | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Utilization (SU)** | 75–85% | Floor/space efficiency |
| **Order Pick Accuracy (OPA)** | ≥ 99.5% | Service quality |
| **Dock-to-Stock (DTS)** | < 24 hours | Inbound velocity |
| **Order Cycle Time (OCT)** | < 8 hours | Outbound velocity |
| **Throughput (T)** | pallets/hour | Capacity |
| **Inventory Turnover (ITR)** | 8–12×/year (food) | Stock health |
| **Cost per Case Shipped** | ₹/case | Productivity |
| **Shrinkage Rate** | < 1% (food) | Loss control |

### 1.4 Classification of Warehouse Strategies (KTU 2024 Framework)

The KTU syllabus classifies warehouse strategies along **four strategic dimensions**:

1. **Ownership Strategy** — Private, Public, Bonded, Cooperative, 3PL
2. **Layout Strategy** — U-flow, I-flow, L-flow, T-flow
3. **Operational Strategy** — Storage, Cross-docking, Hybrid, Flow-through
4. **Network Strategy** — Centralized, Decentralized, Hub-and-Spoke, Hybrid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bloom's Level (KTU 2024):** *Remember + Understand* — These four dimensions form the **nucleus** of all warehouse-strategy exam answers. Memorize them as the "4-LENS framework."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Decision Hierarchy

A food company designs its warehouse strategy in **four sequential layers**:

> **Layer 1 — Network Design (Strategic, 5–10 yr horizon)**
> *How many warehouses? Where? Which type?*
> → Decides cost-to-serve, lead time, and service radius.

> **Layer 2 — Facility Design (Tactical, 1–3 yr horizon)**
> *What size? What layout? What automation level?*
> → Decides capacity, flexibility, and CAPEX.

> **Layer 3 — Slotting & Storing (Operational, daily/weekly)**
> *Where to put SKU A vs. SKU B?*
> → Decides pick path, labor cost, and ergonomics.

> **Layer 4 — Process Control (Real-time)**
> *WMS, RFID, temperature sensors, KPI dashboards.*
> → Decides visibility, traceability, and compliance.

### 2.2 Ownership Strategies (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Private / Captive Warehouse**
> - Owned and operated by the manufacturer (e.g., ITC's Hunza warehouse).
> - High control, high CAPEX, low flexibility.
> - Suited to **A-class SKUs with stable demand**.

> **2. Public Warehouse**
> - Rented space from a third party on a per-pallet or per-month basis.
> - Low CAPEX, high flexibility, used for **seasonal overflow**.
> - Examples: Schedule A warehouses by CWC (Central Warehousing Corporation) in India.

> **3. Bonded Warehouse**
> - Licensed by customs; goods stored **without duty payment** until withdrawn.
> - Essential for **imported food ingredients, exotic fruits, wines**.
> - Governed by the Customs Act, 1962.

> **4. Cooperative Warehouse**
> - Owned jointly by a group of farmers/FPOs (e.g., Amul dairy cooperatives).
> - Used for **aggregating farm produce** before distribution.

> **5. Third-Party Logistics (3PL) Warehouse**
> - Operated by a specialist (Snowman Logistics for cold chain, DHL, etc.).
> - **Key metric:** Cost per pallet per day.

### 2.3 Layout Strategies (Detailed)

> **U-Flow Layout**
> - Receiving and shipping are on the **same side** of the building.
> - Best for **small, busy warehouses** with limited land.
> - Common in retail back-of-house.

> **I-Flow Layout**
> - Receiving on **one side**, shipping on the **opposite side**; product moves in a straight line.
> - Best for **high-volume, assembly-line style** flow (e.g., FMCG bottling plant warehouse).

> **L-Flow Layout**
> - Receiving and shipping on **adjacent sides** at a 90° angle.
> - Best for **land-locked city warehouses** (Bengaluru, Mumbai).

> **T-Flow Layout**
> - One side has both receiving and shipping (dock) and the perpendicular side is storage.
> - Best for **cross-docking operations** at airports/ports.

### 2.4 Operational Strategies (Detailed)

> **Storage Strategy (Traditional)**
> - Goods are **stored for days/weeks/months** before being picked.
> - High inventory holding, high carrying cost.
> - Suited to **slow-moving grains, spices, packaged staples**.

> **Cross-Docking Strategy**
> - Goods are **received and dispatched within hours** (< 24 hrs) — virtually **no storage**.
> - Incoming pallets are *de-consolidated, sorted, and re-consolidated* for outbound.
> - Pioneered by **Walmart**, now standard in fresh produce, dairy, and bakery.
> - Eliminates holding cost; demands **synchronized inbound and outbound**.

> **Hybrid Strategy**
> - Combines storage + cross-dock + value-added services (labeling, kitting, repacking).
> - Most common in modern food distribution centers (e.g., Reliance Fresh DC).

> **Flow-Through / Pre-Distribution**
> - Goods move on a **conveyor or sorting system** from inbound to outbound staging.
> - Used for **high-velocity SKUs** (biscuits, milk pouches) in mega-DCs.

### 2.5 Network Strategies (Detailed)

> **Centralized Network**
> - One mega-warehouse serves the entire country.
> - Lower inventory pool (square-root law), higher transportation cost.
> - Used by **single-brand companies** with predictable demand (e.g., a single dairy plant serving Kerala).

> **Decentralized Network**
> - Many small warehouses close to customers.
> - Lower transport cost, higher inventory pool.
> - Used by **chilled and frozen food** companies (Britannia, Vadilal).

> **Hub-and-Spoke**
> - One central hub + multiple regional spoke warehouses.
> - Hub consolidates, spoke distributes.
> - Standard for **e-commerce grocery** (BigBasket, Blinkit dark stores act as spokes).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Storage Utilization (SU)} &\quad SU \;=\; \frac{\text{Area actually used}}{\text{Total warehouse floor area}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\textbf{(ii) Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)} &\quad ITR \;=\; \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iii) Square-Root Law (Network)} &\quad N_{\text{new}} \;=\; N_{\text{old}} \times \sqrt{\frac{S_{\text{new}}}{S_{\text{old}}}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iv) EOQ (Wilson Model)} &\quad Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(v) Reorder Point} &\quad ROP \;=\; (d \times L) \;+\; SS \\[4pt]
\textbf{(vi) Holding Cost (Annual)} &\quad HC \;=\; Q^{*} \times \frac{H}{2} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(vii) Total Warehouse Cost} &\quad TWC \;=\; \text{Fixed} \;+\; \text{Handling} \;+\; \text{Holding} \;+\; \text{Shortage} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(viii) Capacity (Pallet Positions)} &\quad C \;=\; \frac{L \times W}{L_p \times W_p} \times N_{\text{levels}} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(ix) Throughput} &\quad T \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{orders/day}}}{\text{Pick-hrs/day}} \quad \text{(orders/hr)} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(x) Service Level (Cycle)} &\quad SL \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\text{Stockout orders}}{\text{Total orders}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Where:** $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost, $H$ = annual holding cost per unit, $d$ = daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days), $SS$ = safety stock, $L_p, W_p$ = pallet length/width, $N_{\text{levels}}$ = rack levels.

### 2.7 ABC / XYZ Classification (Slotting Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **ABC Analysis** (Pareto-based, by *annual consumption value*):
>
> | Class | % of SKUs | % of Annual Value | Strategy |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **A** | 10–20% | 70–80% | Closest to dispatch, FIFO + FEFO, tight control |
> | **B** | 30% | 15–25% | Middle aisles, periodic review |
> | **C** | 50–60% | 5–10% | Farthest zones, large bin sizes |
>
> **XYZ** is a complementary classification by **demand variability** (X = stable, Y = moderate, Z = erratic). Food companies often use **ABC–XYZ matrix** to decide warehouse slotting — *AX items* (high value, stable demand) go to **golden zones**; *CZ items* (low value, erratic) are candidates for **postponement** or **drop-shipment** to bypass the warehouse entirely.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Warehouse strategies are the **operational backbone** of the food industry because they directly influence:
> - **Cold chain integrity** (a 1 °C rise in dairy storage halves the shelf life — WHO 2023 data).
> - **Working capital** (every rupee stuck in slow-moving inventory is a rupee unavailable for new SKUs).
> - **Service level** to kirana stores, HoReCa, and online grocery customers.
> - **Carbon footprint** (a well-located, well-utilized warehouse reduces food miles by 20–40%).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Square-Root Law for Warehouse Network Sizing

> **Statement:** When demand (or number of customers served) increases by a factor $k$, the number of warehouses required to maintain the same service level does **not** increase linearly but only by a factor of $\sqrt{k}$.

**Step 1 — Setup**
Let the country be approximated as a square of side $A$. There are $N$ warehouses, each serving a service area of $\frac{A^2}{N}$.

**Step 2 — Radius of Service**
Each warehouse serves a roughly circular area of radius:

$$
r \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{A^2}{\pi N}} \;=\; \frac{A}{\sqrt{\pi N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Average Distance from Customer to Warehouse**
For a uniform demand distribution, the average distance from a random point in the circle to the center is:

$$
\bar{d} \;=\; \frac{2r}{3} \;=\; \frac{2A}{3\sqrt{\pi N}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Transportation Cost**
Total transport cost is proportional to total demand $D$ × average distance:

$$
TC \;\propto\; D \times \bar{d} \;\propto\; \frac{D}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost Optimization**
To minimize $TC$, equate its derivative to zero (or balance marginal cost). The optimal $N$ is found by:

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dN} \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N^{*} \;\propto\; \sqrt{D}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Proportionality (Square-Root Law)**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{N_{\text{new}}}{N_{\text{old}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{old}}}}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** If demand grows 4×, warehouses need to grow only 2× (not 4×). This is the **central insight** of KTU Module 2 warehouse strategy.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) and the Warehouse Connection

**Step 1 — Annual Cost Components**
Two cost components vary with order size $Q$:

- Ordering cost: $\frac{D}{Q} \times S$ (more orders → more cost)
- Holding cost: $\frac{Q}{2} \times H$ (more stock → more cost)

**Step 2 — Total Relevant Cost**

$$
TC(Q) \;=\; \frac{D}{Q}\,S \;+\; \frac{Q}{2}\,H
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $Q$**

$$
\frac{d\,TC}{dQ} \;=\; -\frac{DS}{Q^2} \;+\; \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Set to zero (minimum)**

$$
-\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} \;+\; \frac{H}{2} \;=\; 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{DS}{Q^{2}} \;=\; \frac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Q^{*}$**

$$
Q^{2} \;=\; \frac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q^{*} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Total Cost at EOQ**

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \frac{D}{Q^{*}}\,S \;+\; \frac{Q^{*}}{2}\,H
$$

Substituting $Q^{*}$:

$$
TC_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Strategy Selection by Food Product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Application Table** — Use this in any "Discuss warehouse strategy for product X" type question.

| Food Product | Shelf Life | Temp Need | Demand Pattern | Recommended Strategy | Ownership |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Basmati Rice** | 12–18 mo | Ambient | Stable, bulk | Centralized + Storage | Private |
| **Fresh Milk** | 2–4 days | 4 °C | Daily, stable | Decentralized + Cold Storage | 3PL / Co-op |
| **Frozen Peas** | 12 mo | –18 °C | Steady | Regional + Cold Storage | Private / 3PL |
| **Mango (Alphonso)** | 7–10 days | 10–13 °C | Highly seasonal | Cross-dock + Bonded (export) | Public (seasonal) |
| **Spices (packed)** | 18–24 mo | Ambient | Moderate | Centralized + Storage | Private |
| **E-commerce groceries** | Variable | Mixed | Erratic | Hub-and-Spoke + Hybrid | 3PL |
| **Imported cheese** | 6 mo | 4 °C | Niche | Bonded + Cold | Public bonded |
| **Bread (bakery)** | 1–3 days | Ambient | Daily, fresh | Decentralized + Cross-dock | In-house |

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Warehouse Capacity Planning

**Problem:** A food company is building a new warehouse of dimensions **60 m × 40 m**. The standard pallet size is **1.2 m × 1.0 m**. The racks are **4 levels high**. Each rack aisle requires **3.5 m width**. The total storage area is 70% of the floor (aisles take 30%).

**Step 1 — Effective Storage Area**

$$
A_{\text{storage}} \;=\; 60 \times 40 \times 0.70 \;=\; 1680 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Pallet Footprint**

$$
A_p \;=\; 1.2 \times 1.0 \;=\; 1.2 \;\text{m}^2 \text{ per pallet position}
$$

**Step 3 — Pallet Positions on Floor (Level 1)**

$$
N_{\text{floor}} \;=\; \frac{1680}{1.2} \;=\; 1400 \;\text{pallet positions per level}
$$

**Step 4 — Total with 4 Levels**

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 1400 \times 4 \;=\; 5600 \;\text{pallet positions}
$$

**Step 5 — Throughput Check**
If the company processes 800 orders/day, with 2 shifts of 8 hours each:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{800}{16} \;=\; 50 \;\text{orders/hr}
$$

This is a **moderate-throughput** warehouse; suitable for **U-flow or I-flow** with **semi-automated picking**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Warehouse Strategy Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Choose Warehouse Strategy]) --> q1{Product Shelf Life}
    q1 -- "Long: >6 months" --> q2{Demand Volume}
    q1 -- "Short: <7 days" --> q3{Cold Chain Needed}
    q1 -- "Medium: 1-6 months" --> q4{Seasonal or Steady}
    q2 -- "High, Stable" --> rA[Centralized + Storage]
    q2 -- "Variable" --> rB[Hub-and-Spoke]
    q3 -- "Yes" --> rC[Decentralized + Cold 3PL]
    q3 -- "No, just fresh" --> rD[Cross-Docking]
    q4 -- "Steady" --> rE[Regional Storage]
    q4 -- "Seasonal" --> rF[Public Warehouse Burst]
    rA --> finalA[Ownership: Private / Network: Central]
    rB --> finalB[Ownership: 3PL / Network: Hub-Spoke]
    rC --> finalC[Ownership: 3PL Cold / Network: Decentralized]
    rD --> finalD[Layout: T-Flow / Ownership: Hybrid]
    rE --> finalE[Layout: I-Flow / Ownership: Private]
    rF --> finalF[Layout: U-Flow / Ownership: Public]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Four Strategic Dimensions

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SG1["OWNERSHIP STRATEGY"]
      o1[Private] --- o2[Public] --- o3[Bonded] --- o4[Cooperative] --- o5[3PL]
    end
    subgraph SG2["LAYOUT STRATEGY"]
      l1[U-Flow] --- l2[I-Flow] --- l3[L-Flow] --- l4[T-Flow]
    end
    subgraph SG3["OPERATIONAL STRATEGY"]
      op1[Storage] --- op2[Cross-Dock] --- op3[Hybrid] --- op4[Flow-Through]
    end
    subgraph SG4["NETWORK STRATEGY"]
      n1[Centralized] --- n2[Decentralized] --- n3[Hub-Spoke] --- n4[Hybrid Network]
    end
    SG1 --> Decision([Integrated Warehouse Strategy])
    SG2 --> Decision
    SG3 --> Decision
    SG4 --> Decision
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Cross-Docking Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Truck Arrives at Inbound Dock] --> inB[Unload Pallets]
    inB --> inC[Scan and Sort by Destination]
    inC --> inD{Storage Needed?}
    inD -- "No" --> inE[Move to Outbound Staging]
    inD -- "Yes (less than 24h)" --> inF[Hold in Floor-Level Lane]
    inF --> inE
    inE --> inG[Load onto Outbound Truck]
    inG --> inH[Dispatch to Customer or Store]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modern Food DC

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INFLOW["INBOUND BLOCK"]
      A1[Supplier / Farm Gate] --> A2[Receiving Dock with QC]
      A2 --> A3[Temperature Check and FSSAI Verification]
    end
    subgraph CORE["STORAGE AND PROCESSING BLOCK"]
      A3 --> B1[Decant and Put-Away]
      B1 --> B2[ABC-Slot Warehouse Zones]
      B2 --> B3[Pick and Pack Station]
      B3 --> B4[Value Added Services - Label, Kit, Repack]
    end
    subgraph OUTFLOW["OUTBOUND BLOCK"]
      B4 --> C1[Order Consolidation]
      C1 --> C2[Staging and Route Planning]
      C2 --> C3[Dispatch Dock]
      C3 --> C4[Customer - Retail or E-Commerce]
    end
    subgraph IT["IT AND CONTROL BLOCK"]
      D1[WMS - Warehouse Management System] -.controls.-> B1
      D1 -.controls.-> B2
      D1 -.controls.-> B3
      D1 -.controls.-> C1
      D2[IoT Temperature Sensors] -.monitors.-> CORE
      D3[RFID and Barcode Readers] -.tracks.-> CORE
    end
```

### 4.5 Cost-Trade-off Curve (Mermaid xychart)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Warehouse Cost Components vs Order Quantity"
    x-axis "Order Quantity Q" [100 200 300 400 500 600 700 800]
    y-axis "Cost in Rupees" 0 50000
    line "Ordering Cost DS/Q" [20000 10000 6667 5000 4000 3333 2857 2500]
    line "Holding Cost QH/2" [5000 10000 15000 20000 25000 30000 35000 40000]
    line "Total Cost TC" [25000 20000 21667 25000 29000 33333 37857 42500]
```

> **Reading the chart:** The two cost curves intersect at the **EOQ** — to the left, ordering cost dominates; to the right, holding cost dominates. The minimum of the total cost curve is the **optimal warehouse ordering policy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 2]**
> *Define the term "warehouse strategy" and list any FOUR strategic dimensions on which a food company can formulate its warehouse strategy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** A *warehouse strategy* is a long-term, integrated plan that determines how a firm will design, locate, operate, and manage its storage and distribution facilities to meet customer service goals at minimum total cost.
**Four Strategic Dimensions (2 marks):**
1. Ownership Strategy (Private / Public / Bonded / 3PL / Cooperative)
2. Layout Strategy (U-flow, I-flow, L-flow, T-flow)
3. Operational Strategy (Storage, Cross-dock, Hybrid, Flow-through)
4. Network Strategy (Centralized, Decentralized, Hub-and-Spoke, Hybrid)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, Module 2]**
> *What is cross-docking? Mention TWO of its advantages for a perishable food supply chain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** *Cross-docking* is an operational warehouse strategy in which incoming goods are received, sorted, and dispatched to outbound vehicles within a few hours (typically < 24 hours), with **little or no storage** in between.
**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Eliminates holding cost** — products move through, not stay; this is critical for perishable food where every day in storage shortens saleable life.
2. **Faster order-to-delivery cycle** — fresh produce, dairy, and bakery reach retail shelves within the same day, improving freshness and reducing shrinkage.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 2]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> *(a)* [7 marks] Explain the **ownership strategies** available to a food manufacturing company for warehousing. Discuss the conditions under which each is most appropriate, with at least one food-industry example for each.
> *(b)* [7 marks] A food company currently operates 4 warehouses serving an annual demand of 10,000 tonnes. The company plans to expand to a new market, doubling the demand to 20,000 tonnes. Using the **square-root law**, calculate the new optimal number of warehouses. What is the strategic implication of this result?

**OR**

> **Question B (14 marks):**
> *(a)* [7 marks] Compare and contrast the **Storage Strategy** and the **Cross-Docking Strategy** of warehouse operations. Using a real-world example for each, explain when a food company should choose one over the other.
> *(b)* [7 marks] A regional cold-storage warehouse has the following data:
> - Pallet size: 1.2 m × 1.0 m
> - Warehouse dimensions: 50 m × 30 m
> - 25% of area is used for aisles and docks
> - Racks are 5 levels high
>
> Calculate: (i) the total pallet positions, and (ii) the storage utilization if 1,300 pallet positions are currently occupied.

---

#### 5.2.1 Model Answer for Q3 — Question A

**Part (a) — Ownership Strategies (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 marking scheme awards **1.5 marks per ownership type with example + 1 mark for overall comparison**.

1. **Private / Captive Warehouse (1.5 marks):**
   The warehouse is owned and operated by the food manufacturer. Provides **maximum control** over processes, quality, and cold chain. Best suited for **high-value, fast-moving SKUs with stable demand**.
   *Example:* ITC Limited's Hunza warehouse in Hyderabad for food products.

2. **Public Warehouse (1.5 marks):**
   Rented on a per-pallet or monthly basis from a third party. Provides **flexibility** and **low CAPEX**. Best for **seasonal overflow** or new market entry.
   *Example:* Central Warehousing Corporation (CWC) Schedule-A warehouses used by small food exporters.

3. **Bonded Warehouse (1.5 marks):**
   Licensed by customs; imported goods stored **without payment of duty** until withdrawn. Best for **imported food ingredients, exotic fruits, wines**.
   *Example:* JNPT bonded cold storage for imported apple and kiwi.

4. **Cooperative Warehouse (1.0 mark):**
   Owned jointly by a group of farmers or FPOs. Aggregates farm produce before distribution. Best for **rural aggregation, dairy, F&V**.
   *Example:* Amul's village-level dairy cooperative warehouses in Gujarat.

5. **3PL Warehouse (1.5 marks):**
   Operated by a specialist logistics provider. Combines expertise, technology, and shared infrastructure. Best when the company wants to **focus on core manufacturing and outsource logistics**.
   *Example:* Snowman Logistics (Snowman) for cold-chain 3PL services in India.

---

**Part (b) — Square-Root Law Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$N_{\text{old}} = 4$ warehouses, $D_{\text{old}} = 10{,}000$ tonnes, $D_{\text{new}} = 20{,}000$ tonnes.

**Step 1 — Apply the Square-Root Law**

$$
N_{\text{new}} \;=\; N_{\text{old}} \times \sqrt{\frac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{old}}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
N_{\text{new}} \;=\; 4 \times \sqrt{\frac{20{,}000}{10{,}000}} \;=\; 4 \times \sqrt{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical value**

$$
\sqrt{2} \;\approx\; 1.414
$$

$$
N_{\text{new}} \;=\; 4 \times 1.414 \;\approx\; 5.66
$$

**Step 4 — Round to practical integer**

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{new}} \;\approx\; 6 \;\text{warehouses}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Strategic Implication (2 marks)**
- The company should add only **2 new warehouses** (going from 4 to 6), not double to 8.
- This is the **power of network consolidation**: inventory pooling reduces total safety stock and improves service.
- However, the 2 new warehouses should be **located in the new market's demand zone** to balance inbound and outbound lead times.
- Transportation cost may rise; hence a **Hub-and-Spoke** or **Hybrid** network is recommended.

---

#### 5.2.2 Model Answer for Q3 — Question B

**Part (a) — Storage vs Cross-Docking (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Storage Strategy | Cross-Docking Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Inventory dwell time** | Days to months | < 24 hours |
| **Primary function** | Hold goods safely | Transfer goods quickly |
| **Best for** | Slow/non-perishable (rice, spices) | Fast/perishable (dairy, F&V, bread) |
| **Holding cost** | High | Near zero |
| **CAPEX on racking** | High (multi-level) | Low (floor-level staging) |
| **Information system** | Standard WMS | Real-time WMS with ASN integration |
| **Industry example** | Basmati rice warehouse, ITC | Walmart's fresh produce DC |

**When to choose (2 marks):**
- **Storage** → when the product is **shelf-stable, slow-moving, or used as buffer stock** for demand surges (e.g., 6 months' stock of imported lentils).
- **Cross-docking** → when the product is **highly perishable, has stable demand, and inbound-outbound schedules are synchronized** (e.g., Britannia's daily fresh-bread DC).

**Part (b) — Cold-Storage Capacity Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** Pallet 1.2 m × 1.0 m; Warehouse 50 m × 30 m; Aisles 25%; 5 levels.

**Step 1 — Total Floor Area**

$$
A_{\text{total}} \;=\; 50 \times 30 \;=\; 1500 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Net Storage Area (after deducting 25% for aisles)**

$$
A_{\text{storage}} \;=\; 1500 \times (1 - 0.25) \;=\; 1500 \times 0.75 \;=\; 1125 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Pallet Footprint**

$$
A_p \;=\; 1.2 \times 1.0 \;=\; 1.2 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Pallet Positions per Level**

$$
N_{\text{level}} \;=\; \frac{1125}{1.2} \;=\; 937.5 \;\approx\; 937 \;\text{positions}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Pallet Positions (5 levels)**

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\text{total}} \;=\; 937 \times 5 \;=\; 4685 \;\text{pallet positions}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Storage Utilization (current 1,300 occupied)**

$$
SU \;=\; \frac{1300}{4685} \times 100\% \;\approx\; \boxed{\;27.75\%\;}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Utilization is **well below the 75–85% benchmark**, indicating the warehouse is under-used; management should consider demand-stimulation strategies, additional SKU onboarding, or sub-leasing the spare capacity to recover fixed costs.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (Read Before Writing Your Exam)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "warehouse" with "storage."** A warehouse is a *facility*; storage is a *function*. Don't interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the food-specific angle.** Generic FMCG answers lose 2–3 marks. Always mention **cold chain, perishability, FSSAI/HACCP, shelf life**.
> 3. **Square-Root Law is for *inventory*, not warehouses per se.** Examiner expects the answer: "Inventory in the new system = old inventory × √(new/old demand) **per warehouse**, OR total inventory pooled *decreases* by √(D_old/D_new)."
> 4. **EOQ units.** Always write EOQ in **units or cases** — not in "rupees." A student who writes "EOQ = ₹937" loses the unit mark.
> 5. **Layout strategy ≠ floor plan.** Many students draw a U/I/L/T shape but forget to write the **why** (product flow, land constraint, throughput need). The *justification* carries the marks.
> 6. **Never quote "Pareto" or "80-20" without context.** Pair it with **ABC classification** in warehousing.
> 7. **Calculation steps:** Even if the final answer is correct, missing one step loses 1 mark. Always show formula → substitution → arithmetic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Warehouse Strategies**

- **Definition:** Warehouse strategy = 4-LENS framework → **Ownership × Layout × Operational × Network**.
- **Ownership types:** Private, Public, Bonded, Cooperative, 3PL — choose by **CAPEX, control, perishability, regulatory need**.
- **Layout types:** U-flow (small, busy), I-flow (high-volume, assembly), L-flow (landlocked), T-flow (cross-dock).
- **Operational types:** Storage, Cross-Dock, Hybrid, Flow-Through — pick by **shelf life and velocity**.
- **Network types:** Centralized (low inventory pool, high transport), Decentralized (low transport, high inventory), Hub-and-Spoke (balanced), Hybrid (food e-commerce).
- **Square-Root Law:** $N_{\text{new}} = N_{\text{old}} \sqrt{D_{\text{new}}/D_{\text{old}}}$.
- **EOQ:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — minimum total cost = $\sqrt{2DSH}$.
- **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \times L + SS$.
- **Storage Utilization target:** 75–85% (food industry).
- **Pallet Capacity:** $C = (L \cdot W)/(L_p \cdot W_p) \times N_{\text{levels}}$.
- **ABC rule of thumb:** 20% of SKUs = 80% of value → A items in **golden zones** near dispatch.
- **Cold-chain sensitivity:** 1 °C rise can halve dairy shelf life; refrigerated warehouses must use IoT sensors, not manual checks.
- **Cross-docking** is the **food industry default** for items with shelf life < 7 days.
- **Bonded warehouses** are mandatory for **imported food** before customs duty payment.
- **Hub-and-Spoke** is the standard for **e-commerce grocery** (e.g., Blinkit, BigBasket).
- **KTU 2024 buzzwords to use in answers:** *FEFO, HACCP, traceability, square-root law, ABC-XYZ matrix, FSSAI compliance, IoT-enabled cold chain.*
- **Exam tip:** Always end a long answer with a **numerical or industry example** to secure full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Models and Control Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Models and Control Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Inventory** in a Food Supply Chain refers to the stock of raw materials, work-in-process (WIP) items, finished goods, and packaging materials held at various points along the supply chain — from farm-gate storage to retail shelves — to buffer against demand-supply variability and to ensure continuous operations.

**Inventory Management** is the systematic process of planning, organizing, and controlling inventory levels to minimize the total cost (sum of ordering, holding, shortage, and obsolescence costs) while meeting desired service levels. **Inventory Models** are mathematical and decision-making frameworks used to determine optimal order quantities, reorder points, safety stock levels, and review periods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module covers *deterministic and probabilistic inventory models*, *EOQ family of models*, *quantity discount models*, *inventory classification techniques* (ABC, VED, FSN, SDE, HML), and *modern control techniques* (JIT, VMI, EOQ variations for perishable food products).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **mother's kitchen pantry** during Onam festival preparation. She must decide:
- **How much rice to buy at once?** (Order Quantity) — Too much = rice weevils attack (spoilage) + storage space issue. Too little = mid-cooking emergency runs to the shop.
- **When to buy?** (Reorder Point) — She watches the level drop and triggers a new purchase *before* it empties.
- **How much extra to keep as a buffer?** (Safety Stock) — A small sealed container kept aside "just in case" guests arrive unexpectedly.
- **Which items need most attention?** (ABC Analysis) — Expensive ghee and cashews (Class A) get locked safely; salt and turmeric (Class C) sit in open jars.

This is exactly what **inventory models** do mathematically for food companies handling thousands of SKUs across cold chain, dry chain, and ambient storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology to Memorize:**
> - **D** = Annual demand (units/year)
> - **S** (or **Co**) = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
> - **H** (or **Cc**) = Holding/carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
> - **Q** = Order quantity (units)
> - **LT** (or **L**) = Lead time
> - **ROP** = Reorder Point
> - **SS** = Safety Stock

## 1.3 Why Inventory Models Matter in Food SCM

- **Perishability:** Unlike durable goods, food items have a finite shelf-life. Inventory models for food must incorporate *deterioration rates* (e.g., exponential decay for milk, linear decay for vegetables).
- **Demand Seasonality:** Festivals, harvest cycles, and weather cause demand spikes — models must handle *lumpy demand*.
- **Cold Chain Integrity:** Temperature excursions convert "inventory" into "waste" instantly. Hence **safety stock** acts as a critical buffer.
- **Working Capital:** Food companies hold 25-40% of their assets as inventory — optimizing it directly boosts ROI.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics to Remember in Bold:**
> - **Service Level α = 0.95** (industry standard for fresh produce) corresponds to a z-score of **z = 1.645**.
> - **Service Level α = 0.99** corresponds to **z = 2.33**.
> - **Carrying cost** is typically **20% – 30%** of unit cost per year for ambient foods, and **35% – 50%** for cold-chain foods (due to refrigeration energy costs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inventory Models

Inventory models are classified based on **demand predictability** and **order/replenishment behaviour**:

| Category | Sub-Type | Description | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deterministic Models** | Static Demand (EOQ) | Constant, known demand rate | **Very High (⭐⭐⭐)** |
| | Production Lot Size | Demand met by in-house production | **High (⭐⭐)** |
| | Quantity Discount | Price breaks at quantity tiers | **High (⭐⭐)** |
| **Probabilistic Models** | Continuous Review (Q, R) | Fixed order quantity, variable reorder point | **Medium (⭐⭐)** |
| | Periodic Review (P, T) | Fixed review period, variable order quantity | **Medium (⭐⭐)** |
| **Special Models** | Perishable / Deteriorating Items | Exponential decay of food items | **Emerging in 2024 Scheme** |
| | Just-in-Time (JIT) | Minimal inventory, pull system | **Mentioned in Module 2** |

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Inventory Cost

Every inventory model balances **three competing cost components**:

1. **Ordering Cost (Co or S):** Cost incurred *per order* — includes purchase requisition, transportation, inspection, receiving, payment processing. *Order size is INVERSELY related to this cost.*
2. **Holding / Carrying Cost (Cc or H):** Cost of *holding one unit for one year* — includes warehouse rent, refrigeration energy, insurance, taxes, depreciation, shrinkage, obsolescence, and **opportunity cost of capital**. *Order size is DIRECTLY related to this cost.*
3. **Shortage / Stockout Cost (Cs or p):** Cost of running out of stock — includes lost sales, backorder processing, customer dissatisfaction, production stoppage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Trade-off in Inventory Management:**
> - **Large Q** → Fewer orders → Low ordering cost, **High** carrying cost
> - **Small Q** → More orders → High ordering cost, **Low** carrying cost
> - **Optimal Q (EOQ)** is the point where **Total Cost is minimized**, i.e., Ordering Cost = Holding Cost.

## 2.3 Inventory Classification Techniques (Selective Inventory Control)

Food companies handle thousands of SKUs. They cannot manage every item with equal intensity. Hence **selective inventory control techniques** are used:

### A. ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)
Based on **Pareto's 80/20 principle** — value vs. volume classification.

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Intensity | Food Industry Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 10–20% | 70–80% | Tight control, frequent review | Ghee, branded spices, infant formula |
| **B** | 20–30% | 15–25% | Moderate control, periodic review | Cooking oil, pulses, sugar |
| **C** | 50–70% | 5–10% | Loose control, bulk ordering | Salt, pepper, basic condiments |

### B. VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable)
Classification by **criticality of stockout**:

- **V (Vital):** Stockout halts production / threatens life. E.g., raw milk for a dairy, raw cashew for a sweets unit.
- **E (Essential):** Stockout causes severe disruption. E.g., refined oil for frying.
- **D (Desirable):** Stockout is inconvenient but tolerable. E.g., decorative packaging.

### C. FSN Analysis (Fast, Slow, Non-moving)
Based on **issue/consumption rate from stores**:
- **F (Fast):** High consumption rate — needs continuous replenishment.
- **S (Slow):** Moderate consumption.
- **N (Non-moving):** Zero consumption for >12 months → candidates for write-off or liquidation.

### D. SDE Analysis (Scarce, Difficult, Easy)
Based on **procurement difficulty**:
- **S (Scarce):** Long lead time, single supplier. E.g., saffron, exotic spices.
- **D (Difficult):** Moderate lead time. E.g., seasonal fruits.
- **E (Easy):** Readily available. E.g., salt, sugar.

### E. HML Analysis (High, Medium, Low unit price)
Classifies items by **unit price** — useful for deciding storage security.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember food-specific classifications:** "**VED-FSN-SDE-HML**" — the order they appear in KTU 2024 syllabus Module 2.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

### Table 1: Core EOQ & Extension Models

| # | Model | Optimal Quantity (Q*) | Total Variable Cost (TC*) | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic EOQ (Wilson)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | $\sqrt{2 D S H}$ | Constant demand, instant replenishment, no shortage |
| 2 | **EOQ with Safety Stock** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | $\sqrt{2 D S H} + H \cdot SS$ | Add SS buffer for demand variability |
| 3 | **Production Order Quantity (POQ)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $\sqrt{2 D S H \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}$ | Production rate $p > d$; finite replenishment |
| 4 | **EOQ with Quantity Discount (All-Units)** | Compare TC at each price break vs. EOQ; pick minimum | TC = $\dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H + P \cdot D$ | $p < Q_{break}$ condition for next break |
| 5 | **EOQ with Backorders** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H + p}{p}}$ | TC = $\sqrt{2 D S H \cdot \dfrac{p}{H+p}}$ | $p$ = backorder cost per unit/year |
| 6 | **EOQ with Deterioration (Food)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\theta}{\theta - \lambda}}$ | — | $\theta$ = deterioration rate, $\lambda$ = demand rate; $\theta > \lambda$ |

### Table 2: Probabilistic & Reorder Point Models

| # | Parameter | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Variable demand AND variable lead time |
| 8 | **Reorder Point (variable LT only)** | $ROP = d \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Demand is constant, LT varies |
| 9 | **Safety Stock (variable demand)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Continuous review, Q-system |
| 10 | **Safety Stock (variable LT)** | $SS = z \cdot d \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Continuous review, LT variability |
| 11 | **Safety Stock (periodic review, P-system)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{T + L}$ | Period review, both vary |
| 12 | **Order-up-to Level (S in P-system)** | $S = d \cdot (T + L) + SS$ | Periodic review systems |

### Table 3: Cost & Performance Metrics

| # | Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 13 | **Annual Ordering Cost** | $\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | Number of orders × cost per order |
| 14 | **Annual Holding Cost** | $\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ | Average inventory × cost per unit |
| 15 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** | $\dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | Times inventory is sold/used per year |
| 16 | **Days of Inventory (DOI)** | $\dfrac{365}{ITR}$ | Days of supply in current stock |
| 17 | **Fill Rate** | $1 - \dfrac{\text{Expected Shortages per Cycle}}{Q}$ | Service level metric |
| 18 | **Total Cost (TC)** | $\dfrac{D}{Q} S + \dfrac{Q}{2} H + P \cdot D$ | Sum of all cost components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Symbols:** Throughout KTU papers, you may encounter alternative notations: $D$ = $A$ (annual usage), $S$ = $C_o$ (ordering cost), $H$ = $C_c$ (carrying cost), $Q$ = $q$ (lot size). Always define your variables at the start of the solution.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Food SCM

- **EOQ models** are embedded in **ERP systems** like SAP MM, Oracle SCM, and Blue Yonder (JDA) for automated replenishment in companies like Nestlé, ITC Foods, and Britannia.
- **ABC-VED matrix** (also called **VED-FSN matrix**) is used in **hospital pharmacy and food canteen** management to decide ordering policies for medical and dietary supplies.
- **Perishable EOQ models** directly impact **cold-chain logistics** decisions in companies like Amul (dairy), Suguna Foods (poultry), and Zomato Hyperpure (restaurant supply).
- **Safety stock optimization** is critical during **black swan events** like the COVID-19 pandemic, where food companies had to recalibrate $z$-values from 1.65 to 2.33+ to maintain service levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Classical Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

### Step 0 — Assumptions (Harris-Wilson Model)
Before we begin, we list the **standard assumptions** of the basic EOQ model. These must be stated in every KTU 14-mark answer:

1. Demand $D$ is **constant and deterministic** (known with certainty) over the year.
2. Replenishment is **instantaneous** (lead time = 0; whole batch arrives at once).
3. **No shortages or backorders** are permitted.
4. Ordering cost $S$ is **fixed per order**, independent of order size.
5. Holding cost $H$ is **linear** in average inventory (₹ per unit per year).
6. **No quantity discounts** — unit price $P$ is constant.
7. The planning horizon is **infinite** (or sufficiently long).

### Step 1 — Set Up the Total Cost Function

Let $Q$ = order quantity in units. Then:
- **Number of orders per year** = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$
- **Annual ordering cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$

- **Average inventory** = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (because inventory rises linearly from 0 to $Q$, then drops to 0; the average of the trapezoid is $Q/2$).
- **Annual holding cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$

- **Annual material cost** = $P \cdot D$ (independent of $Q$, so often omitted from minimization).

Therefore, the **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** to be minimized is:

$$
TVC(Q) = \underbrace{\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}}
$$

### Step 2 — Take the First Derivative with respect to Q

We use **differential calculus** to find the minimum. Differentiate $TVC(Q)$ w.r.t. $Q$:

$$
\dfrac{d\,TVC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Set the Derivative to Zero (Optimality Condition)

For a **minimum** (since the function is convex — second derivative is positive), we set the first derivative equal to zero:

$$
-\dfrac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{D S}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \dfrac{2 D S}{H}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

This is the famous **Wilson's EOQ formula** (also called the **Harris-Wilson Square Root Formula**), published by Ford Whitman Harris in 1913 and popularized by H. T. Wilson in 1934.

### Step 4 — Verify the Second-Derivative Test (Confirming a Minimum)

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}\,TVC}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2 D S}{Q^{3}} > 0
$$

Since $D, S, Q$ are all positive, the second derivative is **positive** — confirming that the stationary point is indeed a **minimum**. The cost function is convex, so EOQ gives the global minimum.

### Step 5 — Compute the Minimum Total Cost

Substitute $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ back into the TVC equation:

At optimum, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (this is a key property to remember for KTU exams):

$$
TVC_{\min} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} S + \dfrac{Q^{*}}{2} H = \dfrac{D S}{\sqrt{2 D S / H}} + \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot H
$$

Simplifying both terms:

$$
TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D S H}{2}} + \sqrt{\dfrac{D S H}{2}} = 2 \sqrt{\dfrac{D S H}{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{TVC_{\min} = \sqrt{2 D S H}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A classic 3-mark question is *"Show that at the EOQ, the ordering cost equals the holding cost."* Always derive both and show they are equal.

### Step 6 — Number of Orders Per Year (Bonus Result)

$$
N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt{2 D S / H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D H}{2 S}}
$$

And the **Time Between Orders (TBO)** or **Order Cycle Time**:

$$
T^{*} = \dfrac{1}{N^{*}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 S}{D H}} \quad \text{(in years)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Production Order Quantity (POQ) Model

### Assumptions
- Demand is consumed continuously at rate $d$ (units/year).
- Production is at a **finite rate** $p$ (units/year), with $p > d$.
- Inventory builds up during production, then depletes during non-production.

### Step 1 — Inventory Profile
Inventory rises at rate $(p - d)$ during production time $t_{p} = \dfrac{Q}{p}$.
It then falls at rate $d$ during non-production until it reaches zero.

The **maximum inventory** reached is:

$$
I_{\max} = (p - d) \cdot t_{p} = (p - d) \cdot \dfrac{Q}{p} = Q \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)
$$

The **average inventory** (since inventory rises linearly from 0 to $I_{\max}$, then falls linearly to 0) is:

$$
\bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{\max}}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2} \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Total Cost

$$
TC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} S + \dfrac{Q}{2} H \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Minimize

$$
\dfrac{d\,TC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right) = 0
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \dfrac{2 D S}{H \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)} = \dfrac{2 D S}{H} \cdot \dfrac{p}{p - d}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{P}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p - d}}}
$$

This is the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** or **Production Lot Size**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** When $p \to \infty$ (infinite production rate), the second factor $\sqrt{p/(p-d)} \to 1$, and $Q_P^*$ reduces to the basic EOQ. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EOQ with Quantity Discount (All-Units Discount)

### The Setup
Suppose the supplier offers **all-units discounts** as follows:

| Price Break Quantity | Unit Price |
|---|---|
| $0 \le Q < Q_1$ | $P_1$ (highest) |
| $Q_1 \le Q < Q_2$ | $P_2$ |
| $Q_2 \le Q < Q_3$ | $P_3$ (lowest) |

Note: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ and $Q_1 < Q_2 < Q_3$.

The **total annual cost** including purchase cost is:

$$
TC(Q) = P \cdot D + \dfrac{D}{Q} S + \dfrac{Q}{2} H
$$

But here, $H$ is often expressed as a **percentage** $I$ of unit price: $H = I \cdot P$. So:

$$
TC(Q) = P \cdot D + \dfrac{D}{Q} S + \dfrac{I \cdot P \cdot Q}{2}
$$

### The Algorithm (Standard KTU 14-Mark Problem)

The correct procedure is to find a **candidate EOQ at each price level** and then **adjust** if the EOQ falls below the price break.

**Step A:** Compute the EOQ at the **lowest price** $P_3$:
$$
Q_{EOQ}^{(3)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{I \cdot P_3}}
$$

**Step B:** Check if $Q_{EOQ}^{(3)} \ge Q_2$ (i.e., is the EOQ at the cheapest price large enough to qualify for that price?).
- **If YES:** Then $Q^* = Q_{EOQ}^{(3)}$ is feasible. Done.
- **If NO:** Proceed.

**Step C:** If $Q_{EOQ}^{(3)} < Q_2$, then the true optimum **cannot** be at the cheapest price (you'd be paying $P_3$ for less than $Q_2$ units, which is impossible). So evaluate TC at $Q = Q_2$ using $P = P_2$:
$$
TC_2 = P_2 \cdot D + \dfrac{D}{Q_2} S + \dfrac{I \cdot P_2 \cdot Q_2}{2}
$$

**Step D:** Compute EOQ at the next higher price $P_2$:
$$
Q_{EOQ}^{(2)} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{I \cdot P_2}}
$$

**Step E:** Check if $Q_{EOQ}^{(2)} \ge Q_1$.
- **If YES:** $Q^* = Q_{EOQ}^{(2)}$, done.
- **If NO:** Evaluate at $Q = Q_1$ using $P = P_1$ and continue upward.

**Step F:** The optimal $Q^*$ is the one that gives the **minimum TC** among all candidates.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** A naive approach is to "pick the cheapest price and order EOQ at that price." This is WRONG because if EOQ at the lowest price is below its break quantity, you cannot actually order that price. Always check feasibility first.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Basic EOQ

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

A Kerala-based spice company uses **2,000 kg of turmeric per year**. The ordering cost is **₹400 per order** and the holding cost is **₹10 per kg per year**. The unit price is constant at ₹200/kg. Find: (a) EOQ, (b) Total Variable Cost, (c) Number of orders per year, (d) Time between orders.

### Given
- $D = 2{,}000$ kg/year
- $S = ₹400$ per order
- $H = ₹10$ per kg/year
- $P = ₹200$ per kg (not needed for EOQ)

### (a) Economic Order Quantity

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2{,}000 \times 400}{10}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1{,}600{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{160{,}000} = 400 \text{ kg}
$$

### (b) Total Variable Cost

$$
TVC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S H} = \sqrt{2 \times 2{,}000 \times 400 \times 10} = \sqrt{16{,}000{,}000} = ₹4{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Verification:**
- Ordering Cost = $\dfrac{2{,}000}{400} \times 400 = ₹2{,}000$ ✓
- Holding Cost = $\dfrac{400}{2} \times 10 = ₹2{,}000$ ✓
- Total = ₹4{,}000 ✓ (Both equal at optimum!)

### (c) Number of Orders per Year

$$
N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{2{,}000}{400} = 5 \text{ orders/year}
$$

### (d) Time Between Orders

$$
T^{*} = \dfrac{1}{N^{*}} = \dfrac{1}{5} \text{ year} = \dfrac{365}{5} = 73 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discussion:** The company should place an order of **400 kg** of turmeric **every 73 days** (approximately 2.5 months) to minimize its total inventory cost. **5 orders per year** is the optimal ordering frequency.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Reorder Point with Safety Stock

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

A cold-chain dairy (Milma) handles butter. Average demand is **100 kg/day**, lead time is **6 days**. Demand during lead time is normally distributed with **standard deviation = 15 kg**. The company wants a **95% service level** ($z = 1.645$). Find: (a) Reorder Point, (b) Safety Stock.

### (a) Safety Stock

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.645 \times 15 = 24.675 \approx 25 \text{ kg}
$$

### (b) Reorder Point

$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS = (100 \times 6) + 25 = 600 + 25 = 625 \text{ kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the stock of butter drops to **625 kg**, a new order must be placed. The expected demand during the 6-day lead time is 600 kg, but the extra 25 kg acts as a buffer against demand surges.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inventory Cost Trade-off Curve (Block Functional View)

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **fundamental EOQ trade-off** that every KTU student must internalize — the U-shaped total cost curve and the convex geometry of the optimization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inventory Decision Inputs] --> B[Annual Demand D]
    A --> C[Ordering Cost S]
    A --> D[Holding Cost H per unit per year]
    A --> E[Unit Price P]

    B --> F[Cost Function Generator]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G[Compute Total Variable Cost TVC]
    G --> H[Q is very small]
    G --> I[Q is very large]
    G --> J[Q at EOQ]

    H --> H1[Many orders, high ordering cost]
    I --> I1[Less ordering, but high holding cost]
    J --> J1[Ordering Cost = Holding Cost]

    H1 --> K[TVC is HIGH]
    I1 --> L[TVC is HIGH]
    J1 --> M[TVC is MINIMIZED at EOQ]

    M --> N[Output: Optimal Order Quantity Q]
    N --> O[Optimal Number of Orders N]
    N --> P[Optimal Cycle Time T]
    N --> Q[Minimum Total Cost TC star]

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef cost fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef optimum fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D,E input
    class F,G cost
    class H,I,H1,I1,K,L high
    class J,J1,M optimum
    class N,O,P,Q output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Follow the flow from the top — inputs feed the cost function generator, which evaluates three scenarios (small Q, large Q, optimal Q). The red paths indicate high cost, the green path indicates the optimum, and the blue nodes are the actionable outputs that an inventory manager would program into their ERP.

## 4.2 Inventory Classification Matrix (ABC-VED Cross-Matrix)

A **2×3 cross-classification matrix** showing how ABC and VED combine to yield **nine management strategies** — a popular KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material<br>or SKU] --> B{Is it expensive?<br>ABC Test}
    B -->|Yes, high value| C[Class A item]
    B -->|Medium value| D[Class B item]
    B -->|Low value| E[Class C item]

    C --> F{Is stockout critical?<br>VED Test}
    D --> G{Is stockout critical?<br>VED Test}
    E --> H{Is stockout critical?<br>VED Test}

    F -->|Vital| I1[Category 1: A-V<br>Tight control, high safety stock]
    F -->|Essential| I2[Category 2: A-E<br>Moderate control, frequent review]
    F -->|Desirable| I3[Category 3: A-D<br>Order as needed, low stock]

    G -->|Vital| J1[Category 4: B-V<br>Moderate safety stock, regular review]
    G -->|Essential| J2[Category 5: B-E<br>Standard ordering, periodic review]
    G -->|Desirable| J3[Category 6: B-D<br>Bulk ordering, infrequent review]

    H -->|Vital| K1[Category 7: C-V<br>Maintain minimum, source alternatives]
    H -->|Essential| K2[Category 8: C-E<br>Bulk purchase, simple record]
    H -->|Desirable| K3[Category 9: C-D<br>Do not stock, buy on demand]

    I1 --> Z1[Policy: SAFETY STOCK UP, MONITOR DAILY]
    I2 --> Z1
    I3 --> Z2[Policy: ORDER ECONOMICALLY, REVIEW MONTHLY]
    J1 --> Z1
    J2 --> Z2
    J3 --> Z2
    K1 --> Z2
    K2 --> Z3[Policy: BULK ORDER, ANNUAL REVIEW]
    K3 --> Z3

    classDef input fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef abc fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef category fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef policy fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    class A input
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H abc
    class I1,I2,I3,J1,J2,J3,K1,K2,K3 category
    class Z1,Z2,Z3 policy
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for the 9-Cell Matrix:**
> - The **top-left triangle** (A-V, A-E, B-V) needs **tight control**.
> - The **diagonal** (A-D, B-E, C-V) is **moderate**.
> - The **bottom-right triangle** (B-D, C-E, C-D) is **loose** — bulk orders, infrequent review.

## 4.3 EOQ Decision Workflow in a Food Company
