This block diagram maps the **operational flow** of how an inventory manager uses these models on a daily basis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Stock Reaches Reorder Point]) --> Step1[Step 1: Check Current Stock Level]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Identify SKU and Lookup in Master Data]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Retrieve Demand D Ordering Cost S<br>Holding Cost H from ERP]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Check Item Classification]
    Step4 --> Step4A{Is it Class A or B-V?}
    Step4A -->|Yes| Step4B[Apply Tight Control: Reorder Immediately]
    Step4A -->|No| Step4C[Apply Loose Control: Batch with Other Items]

    Step4B --> Step5[Step 5: Compute EOQ = sqrt 2DS over H]
    Step4C --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Apply Adjustments]
    Step6 --> Step6A[Add Safety Stock for Variability]
    Step6 --> Step6B[Apply Quantity Discount Check]
    Step6 --> Step6C[Check Production Rate if In-house]

    Step6A --> Step7[Step 7: Finalize Order Quantity Q star]
    Step6B --> Step7
    Step6C --> Step7

    Step7 --> Step8[Step 8: Generate Purchase Order]
    Step8 --> Step9[Step 9: Track Lead Time and Incoming Shipment]
    Step9 --> Step10[Step 10: Receive Goods, Update Inventory]
    Step10 --> End([Cycle Complete: Next Reorder Cycle Begins])

    classDef start fill:#4CAF50,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#FFF
    classDef step fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    classDef adjust fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#FFF

    class Start,End start
    class Step1,Step2,Step3,Step5,Step7,Step8,Step9,Step10 step
    class Step4,Step4A decision
    class Step4B,Step4C,Step6,Step6A,Step6B,Step6C adjust
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEFTT636]**
*Define the term "Inventory" in the context of Food Supply Chain Management. List the four main types of inventory with one food industry example each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Inventory in a Food Supply Chain refers to the **stock of physical items** — raw materials, work-in-process, finished goods, and packaging materials — held at various nodes of the supply chain (farms, processing plants, warehouses, distributors, retailers) to **decouple supply from demand**, buffer against variability, and ensure uninterrupted operations.

**Four Types of Inventory with Food Industry Examples:**

| # | Type | Description | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Raw Materials Inventory** | Unprocessed inputs awaiting use | Wheat flour in a bakery's silo; raw cashew in a sweets factory |
| 2 | **Work-in-Process (WIP) Inventory** | Partially processed items | Fermenting dough in a bread plant; maturing cheese in a dairy |
| 3 | **Finished Goods Inventory** | Completed products ready for sale | Packaged biscuits in a Britannia warehouse; bottled milk at Milma depot |
| 4 | **MRO / Packaging Inventory** | Maintenance, Repair, Operations supplies | Bottle caps, corrugated cartons, shrink-wrap rolls |

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table (0.5 per type).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEFTT636]**
*State and briefly explain the "Square Root Formula" of Economic Order Quantity (EOQ). What are the assumptions of the model?*

**Model Answer:**

**The Square Root Formula (Wilson's EOQ Formula):**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year.

**Key Assumptions:**
1. Demand is **constant and deterministic**.
2. Lead time is **zero** (instantaneous replenishment).
3. **No shortages** or backorders are allowed.
4. Ordering cost and holding cost are **fixed and known**.
5. Unit price is **constant** (no quantity discounts).

> [!NOTE]
> The minimum total variable cost at EOQ is $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S H}$.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Students must answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Below are **TWO alternative question choices**, and students can attempt any one.

---

### 📘 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, PEFTT636 — Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula from first principles. Show that at the optimal order quantity, the annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A Kerala-based food processing unit uses **8,000 kg of jaggery per year**. The cost of placing an order is **₹250 per order**. The cost of holding one kg of jaggery for one year is **₹5 per kg**.
- (i) Calculate the Economic Order Quantity.
- (ii) Calculate the minimum total variable cost.
- (iii) Find the number of orders per year and the time between orders.

#### Model Solution to Q. (a)

**Step 1: Define variables and write the total cost function.**

Let $Q$ = order quantity in kg, $D$ = 8,000 kg/year, $S$ = ordering cost, $H$ = holding cost.

Total Variable Cost:

$$
TVC(Q) = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

**Step 2: Differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and equate to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{d\,TVC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D S}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $Q$.**

$$
Q^{2} = \dfrac{2 D S}{H} \implies \boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}}
$$

**Step 4: Show that Ordering Cost = Holding Cost at $Q^*$.**

Ordering Cost at $Q^*$:

$$
OC = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} \cdot S = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{D S H}{2}}
$$

Holding Cost at $Q^*$:

$$
HC = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{2} \cdot H = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}} \cdot H = \sqrt{\dfrac{D S H}{2}}
$$

Since both equal $\sqrt{DSH/2}$, they are equal. **Q.E.D.** ✓

**Step 5: Minimum total cost.**

$$
TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 D S H}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q. (b)

**Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ kg/year, $S = ₹250$/order, $H = ₹5$/(kg·year).

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 250}{5}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}000{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{800{,}000}
$$

$$
Q^{*} \approx 894.43 \approx 894 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Minimum Total Variable Cost:**

$$
TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 250 \times 5} = \sqrt{20{,}000{,}000} \approx ₹4{,}472.14/\text{year}
$$

**Verification:**
- $OC = (8{,}000/894) \times 250 \approx ₹2{,}236.07$ ✓
- $HC = (894/2) \times 5 = ₹2{,}235$ ✓
- Total ≈ ₹4,471 ✓ (matches our calculated TC*)

**(iii) Number of Orders and Time Between Orders:**

$$
N^{*} = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{894} \approx 8.95 \approx 9 \text{ orders/year}
$$

$$
T^{*} = \dfrac{365}{9} \approx 40.6 \text{ days between orders}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer. Always write "kg" or "₹/year" — marks deducted for unit omission.
> 2. **Rounding too early.** If you round $Q^*$ to 894 before computing TC*, your verification will *not* exactly match. The trick is to either keep $Q^*$ exact (as $400\sqrt{5}$) or to verify using the unrounded value.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** in part (a). KTU evaluators often allocate 1 mark for listing the assumptions of the EOQ model.

---

### 📗 Question Choice B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Option*

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEFTT636]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **ABC analysis** and **VED analysis** of inventory control. How does the **ABC-VED matrix** help in inventory decision-making for a food processing company? Illustrate with a suitable example.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A dairy cooperative handles a single SKU — **butter** — with the following demand characteristics:
- Average daily demand: 50 kg
- Standard deviation of daily demand: 8 kg
- Lead time: 4 days (deterministic)
- Service level: 97.5% ($z = 1.96$)

Calculate: (i) Safety Stock, (ii) Reorder Point, (iii) If the company wants 99% service level ($z = 2.33$), what will be the new reorder point?

#### Model Solution to Q. (a)

**ABC Analysis (Always Better Control):**
Based on **Pareto's 80/20 principle**. Items are classified by **annual consumption value** (price × annual usage):
- **A items (10–20% of SKUs, 70–80% of value):** Tight control, frequent counting, accurate records.
- **B items (20–30% of SKUs, 15–25% of value):** Moderate control, periodic review.
- **C items (50–70% of SKUs, 5–10% of value):** Loose control, bulk ordering, simple records.

**VED Analysis (Vital, Essential, Desirable):**
Based on **criticality of stockout**:
- **Vital (V):** Stockout halts production or endangers life. *Example:* raw milk in a dairy.
- **Essential (E):** Stockout causes severe disruption. *Example:* refined oil.
- **Desirable (D):** Stockout is inconvenient. *Example:* decorative packaging.

**ABC-VED Matrix:**

| ABC \\ VED | Vital (V) | Essential (E) | Desirable (D) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Highest priority — high safety stock, daily monitoring | High priority — strict EOQ, frequent review | Moderate — review monthly |
| **B** | High priority — adequate safety stock | Moderate — standard EOQ | Low — bulk ordering |
| **C** | Moderate — minimum stock, alternate sourcing | Low — bulk purchase | Do not stock |

**Food Industry Example:** For a sweets manufacturing unit (e.g., Krishna Sweets):
- **A-V:** Pure ghee, cashew (high value + vital) → locked storage, daily count, high safety stock.
- **A-D:** Premium gift box (high value + desirable) → ordered based on festival demand forecast.
- **C-V:** Raw milk (low unit price + vital) → bulk procurement with multiple suppliers.
- **C-D:** Decorative ribbon (low value + desirable) → do not stock; order on demand.

**Utility of the Matrix:** It enables **differential inventory policies** — managers don't waste effort on C-D items while risking stockouts on A-V items. It is the foundation of **selective inventory control**.

#### Model Solution to Q. (b)

**Given:** $d = 50$ kg/day, $\sigma_d = 8$ kg/day, $L = 4$ days, $z = 1.96$ (for 97.5%).

**(i) Safety Stock:**

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L} = 1.96 \times 8 \times \sqrt{4} = 1.96 \times 8 \times 2 = 31.36 \approx 32 \text{ kg}
$$

**(ii) Reorder Point:**

$$
ROP = d \cdot L + SS = (50 \times 4) + 32 = 200 + 32 = 232 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) New ROP at 99% service level ($z = 2.33$):**

$$
SS_{new} = 2.33 \times 8 \times 2 = 37.28 \approx 38 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
ROP_{new} = 200 + 38 = 238 \text{ kg}
$$

**Discussion:** Increasing service level from 97.5% to 99% raises the safety stock by ~6 kg and the ROP by 6 kg. The dairy must carry an additional buffer of 6 kg to achieve the higher service level — this is the **service-level vs. inventory cost trade-off**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Wrong formula for SS when demand is variable:** Students often use $z \cdot \sigma_d$ (which assumes lead time is 1 day). The correct formula is $z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $\sigma_d$ (daily std dev) with $\sigma_{LT}$ (LT std dev):** In this problem, LT is *deterministic* — so we use the daily demand variability scaled by $\sqrt{L}$.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the exam.**

### 🔑 Core Formulas to Memorize

- **EOQ:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$
- **Minimum TC:** $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$
- **Production Order Quantity:** $Q_P^* = \sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$
- **Reorder Point:** $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$
- **Safety Stock (variable demand):** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$

### 🔑 Key Properties to Remember

- At optimum EOQ: **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (always).
- EOQ model assumes: **constant demand, no shortage, zero lead time, no quantity discount**.
- Production model reduces to basic EOQ when $p \to \infty$.
- Lowering EOQ → more orders, less inventory, higher service risk.

### 🔑 Classification Techniques — Quick Facts

- **ABC:** Value-based (Pareto).
- **VED:** Criticality-based.
- **FSN:** Consumption rate-based.
- **SDE:** Procurement difficulty-based.
- **HML:** Unit price-based.
- **GOLF:** Government/Ordinary/Local/Foreign supplier-based.

### 🔑 z-Score Reference (Service Level)

| Service Level $\alpha$ | z-score |
|---|---|
| 90.0% | 1.28 |
| 95.0% | 1.645 |
| 97.5% | 1.96 |
| 99.0% | 2.33 |
| 99.9% | 3.09 |

### 🔑 Common KTU 14-Mark Question Templates

1. Derive EOQ + show OC = HC at optimum (7 marks) + numerical problem (7 marks).
2. ABC-VED matrix explanation with food industry example (7 marks) + ROP/SS calculation (7 marks).
3. EOQ with quantity discount (algorithm explanation) (7 marks) + numerical evaluation (7 marks).
4. Production Order Quantity derivation (7 marks) + comparison with basic EOQ (7 marks).
5. Perishable inventory model for food (deterioration) (7 marks) + numerical (7 marks).

### 🔑 Common Mistakes to Avoid

- ❌ Rounding EOQ too early (loses the OC = HC equality).
- ❌ Forgetting to check feasibility in quantity discount problems.
- ❌ Using $z \cdot \sigma_d$ instead of $z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ for safety stock.
- ❌ Confusing $H$ (₹/unit/year) with $I$ (% of unit price).
- ❌ Stating the EOQ formula without listing its assumptions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** Always begin a 14-mark answer with a **diagram or table** (for 1 mark), state **assumptions** (for 1 mark), show **all formula steps** (for 3 marks), perform **calculations with units** (for 2 marks), and conclude with a **practical interpretation in food industry context** (for 1 mark).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance and Challenges in Postharvest Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Importance and Challenges in Postharvest Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Postharvest Management (PHM)** refers to the integrated set of operations, technologies, and institutional interventions applied to agricultural, horticultural, animal, and aquatic produce from the moment of harvest (or slaughter/capture) until it reaches the final consumer, encompassing all stages of handling, cleaning, sorting, grading, packaging, pre-conditioning, storage, transportation, processing, marketing, and quality assurance.

According to the **Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO)** of the United Nations, postharvest loss is defined as the measurable quantitative and qualitative loss of edible food mass (or nutritional value) along the supply chain that was originally intended for human consumption, but which is unaccounted for at any of the sequential stages from harvest to retail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Postharvest Management is positioned in Module 3 as a **critical leverage point** in the Food Supply Chain (FSC). It is the bridge between agricultural production (farm gate) and the consumption stage, and inefficiencies here directly translate into food insecurity, economic loss, and carbon footprint inflation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just bought a beautiful bunch of fresh **jasmine flowers** (malli poo) from a local vendor in Kerala. If you leave them on a hot table without water for even **2 hours**, they wilt, brown at the edges, and lose fragrance. Postharvest management for food is exactly this principle — produce is "alive" after harvest (it respires, transpires, ripens, and decays), and the entire PHM system is essentially the **"climate-controlled life-support system"** that keeps that life-freshness intact from the farmer's field to your plate.

Think of PHM as a **temperature-controlled relay race**:
- The **baton** = the produce (banana, fish, milk, rice)
- The **runners** = farmers, aggregators, transporters, cold storage operators, retailers
- The **track conditions** = cold chain, humidity, hygiene
- **Dropping the baton** = quality loss, spoilage, rejection, waste

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Global Postharvest Loss (FAO estimate):** ~**13.2%** of total food produced (2011 baseline, still used as a benchmark)
> - **Developing countries (like India):** Up to **30–40%** loss in fruits and vegetables
> - **Developed countries:** Typically **5–15%** loss
> - **Cereals in India:** ~**5–10%** annual postharvest loss
> - **Perishables (F&V, fish, dairy) in India:** ~**20–40%** annual loss

### 1.3 Scope of Postharvest Management

The scope spans **four macro-pillars**, often summarized in KTU textbooks as the **"4-L Framework"**:

| Pillar | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Loss Reduction** | Quantitative minimization | Cold storage reducing wastage of tomato from 35% → 8% |
| **Life Extension** | Shelf-life prolongation | Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) of strawberries |
| **Linkage Strengthening** | Market connectivity | Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) linking to retailers |
| **Livelihood Uplift** | Income improvement for primary producers | Direct procurement, fair pricing |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Postharvest Management — A Layered Justification

Postharvest management is not a luxury — it is a **food security, economic, and sustainability imperative**. The depth of its importance can be unpacked across **six analytical layers**:

#### Layer 1: Quantitative Loss Reduction (Food Security)
- Every kilogram saved at the postharvest stage is a kilogram that does not need to be re-produced.
- This reduces pressure on land, water, fertilizer, and energy inputs.
- **Respiration & Transpiration rates** determine shelf-life; controlling them = saving food.

#### Layer 2: Qualitative Preservation (Nutrition & Safety)
- Vitamin C in leafy vegetables degrades **40–60%** within 24 hours of harvest at ambient temperature.
- Aflatoxin contamination in improperly stored grains can cause **liver cancer** and is regulated globally.
- PHM preserves **micronutrients, phytochemicals, and sensory attributes** (color, texture, aroma).

#### Layer 3: Economic Value Capture
- The "**farm-to-fork value chain**" typically multiplies produce value by **3x to 10x** through proper handling, grading, and branding.
- Example: A kilogram of **pineapple** sold raw at the farm may fetch ₹15, but as a minimally processed, cold-chain-tracked product, it can fetch ₹80–120.

#### Layer 4: Sustainability & Climate Impact
- Food loss = **wasted embedded resources**: ~**1.4 billion tonnes** of food lost globally = ~**3.3 Gt CO₂ equivalent** of avoidable GHG emissions (FAO).
- Reducing loss is ranked among the **top 3 highest-impact climate solutions** by Project Drawdown.

#### Layer 5: Trade & Export Competitiveness
- International buyers (EU, USA, Gulf) require **phytosanitary compliance**, cold-chain traceability, and grade standards.
- India's **APEDA-regulated mango exports** to Japan/USA depend entirely on postharvest vapor heat treatment (VHT) and irradiation facilities.

#### Layer 6: Social & Rural Livelihoods
- Postharvest infrastructure (cold stores, pack houses) creates **non-farm rural employment**, especially for women.
- Reduces distress sales by farmers — a known driver of rural indebtedness.

### 2.2 The "How" — Operational Sequence of PHM

Postharvest operations follow a **chronological and interdependent chain**. The KTU reference sequence (CO-aligned) is:

1. **Harvesting** (maturity indexing, time-of-day harvesting)
2. **Pre-cooling** (rapid removal of field heat)
3. **Cleaning & Washing** (sanitization)
4. **Sorting & Grading** (size, color, defect classification)
5. **Pre-treatment** (waxing, fungicide dip, curing)
6. **Packaging** (field packs, MAP, vacuum packs)
7. **Storage** (cold rooms, CA — Controlled Atmosphere, MA — Modified Atmosphere)
8. **Transportation** (reefer trucks, insulated vans)
9. **Distribution & Retailing**
10. **Processing** (if applicable)
11. **Consumer handling**

> [!NOTE]
> **Vital Concept — The Cold Chain:**
> A cold chain is a **temperature-controlled uninterrupted logistics network** maintaining produce within a defined temperature window (e.g., **0–4°C** for chilled foods, **-18°C** for frozen). A "break" in the chain — even for 30 minutes — can compromise entire shipments.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Below is the **exam-ready quantitative toolbox** for Postharvest Management calculations.

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $$\text{Postharvest Loss \%} = \frac{Q_h - Q_c}{Q_h} \times 100$$ | Quantitative loss percentage | $Q_h$ = quantity at harvest (kg), $Q_c$ = quantity reaching consumer (kg) |
| $$\text{Shelf Life Extension Factor} = \frac{SL_{cold}}{SL_{ambient}}$$ | Multiplier of shelf life due to cold chain | $SL_{cold}$ = shelf life under refrigeration (days), $SL_{ambient}$ = shelf life at ambient (days) |
| $$\text{Respiration Rate: } R = \frac{CO_2 \text{ produced (mL)}}{\text{kg} \cdot \text{hr}}$$ | Indicator of metabolic activity; higher R = shorter shelf life | Measured in mL/kg·hr at a given T |
| $$Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$ | Temperature quotient — rate doubles for every 10°C rise | $R_1, R_2$ = respiration rates at $T_1, T_2$ (°C) |
| $$\text{RH Factor: } a_w = \frac{p}{p_0}$$ | Water activity — microbial growth threshold typically $\geq 0.6$ | $p$ = partial vapor pressure, $p_0$ = vapor pressure of pure water |
| $$\text{Clausius-Clapeyron for storage RH: } \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{L}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ | Predicts vapor pressure — useful for humidity-controlled storage design | $L$ = latent heat of vaporization, $R$ = gas constant |
| $$\text{Value Addition \%} = \frac{V_{processed} - V_{raw}}{V_{raw}} \times 100$$ | Economic uplift from postharvest processing | $V$ = market price (₹/kg) |
| $$\text{Cold Storage Load (kW)} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot t^{-1}$$ | Heat load estimation for designing a cold store | $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature drop (K), $t$ = time (s) |
| $$\text{Storage Capacity Utilization \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Stock}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100$$ | Operational efficiency of a cold store | Unitless, expressed as percentage |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic — they are deployed in:
- **Cold chain logistics companies** (Snowman, ColdEx, Rivigo ColdChain) for truck-load sizing.
- **Pack-house design** under the **MIDH (Mission for Integrated Development of Horticulture)** scheme of the Government of India.
- **FPO (Farmer Producer Organization)** business plans for bank credit appraisal.
- **Export consignments** where the **$Q_{10}$** rule informs pre-cooling timelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — The $Q_{10}$ Rule and Its Cold-Chain Implications

The **temperature quotient ($Q_{10}$)** is the foundational biophysical relationship in PHM. It states that the rate of a biological/chemical reaction (such as respiration or microbial growth) typically **doubles or triples** for every **10°C rise in temperature**.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Assume the reaction rate $R$ follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence:

$$
R = A \cdot e^{-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T}}
$$

where $A$ is the pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

**Step 2:** Take the ratio of rates at two temperatures $T_2$ and $T_1$:

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1} = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}} = e^{\frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)}
$$

**Step 3:** The $Q_{10}$ is defined as the rate ratio when $T_2 - T_1 = 10$ K. Therefore:

$$
Q_{10} = e^{\frac{10 \cdot E_a}{R \cdot T_1 \cdot T_2}}
$$

**Step 4:** A common empirical simplification assumes $Q_{10} \approx 2$ to $3$ for produce respiration. Solving for shelf life reduction $SL_{ratio}$:

$$
SL_{ratio} = \left(\frac{1}{Q_{10}}\right)^{\frac{\Delta T}{10}}
$$

**Step 5:** For strawberries, with $Q_{10} = 2.5$ and a temperature drop of $\Delta T = 15$°C (from 25°C ambient to 10°C refrigerated):

$$
SL_{ratio} = \left(\frac{1}{2.5}\right)^{\frac{15}{10}} = (0.4)^{1.5} \approx 0.253
$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation — shelf life of strawberries at 10°C is approximately **~4 times longer** than at 25°C, justifying the entire cold-chain investment.

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — Cold Store Heat Load (Engineering Design)

A cold storage room's refrigeration capacity depends on the heat it must remove per unit time.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Sensible heat to be removed from the produce:

$$
Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2:** Latent heat from respiration and transpiration (approximated as a fraction of sensible heat, typically **5–10%** for stored fruits):

$$
Q_{latent} = 0.07 \cdot Q_{sensible}
$$

**Step 3:** Heat ingress through walls, floor, and ceiling (using $U$-value, area, and temperature difference):

$$
Q_{conduction} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 4:** Heat from infiltration (door opening, personnel):

$$
Q_{infiltration} = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot n
$$

**Step 5:** Heat from lights, fans, and personnel (typically 5–10 W/m² floor area):

$$
Q_{misc} = 8 \cdot A_{floor}
$$

**Step 6:** Total refrigeration load (kW):

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent} + Q_{conduction} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{misc}}{3600}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical:** For **5,000 kg of mangoes** pre-cooled from 30°C to 13°C over **8 hours** in a **50 m²** room ($c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K, $U \cdot A = 50$ W/K, $\Delta T_{room} = 15$ K, infiltration cycles $n = 4$):
>
> $Q_{sensible} = 5000 \times 3.5 \times 17 = 297,500$ kJ
>
> $Q_{conduction} = 50 \times 15 = 750$ W = 21,600 kJ over 8 h
>
> $Q_{infiltration} \approx 8,000$ kJ
>
> $Q_{misc} = 8 \times 50 = 400$ W = 11,520 kJ over 8 h
>
> **Total = ~339,000 kJ** → **~11.7 kW** refrigeration load required.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Importance vs. Challenges (KTU High-Yield Comparison)

This **two-axis matrix** is a frequently asked structure in KTU exams. Each "Importance" has a mirrored "Challenge."

| Dimension | Importance (Why PHM matters) | Corresponding Challenge (Why it is hard) |
|---|---|---|
| **Food Security** | Reduces loss, feeds more mouths | Fragmented farms, lack of cold chain at farm gate |
| **Nutrition** | Preserves vitamins and minerals | Vitamin C degrades rapidly without cold chain |
| **Economics** | Value addition multiplies farmer income | Capital-intensive infrastructure; small farmer unaffordability |
| **Sustainability** | Cuts embedded emissions of wasted food | Cold chain itself uses energy (refrigerant leakage) |
| **Trade** | Unlocks export markets | Stringent SPS (Sanitary and Phytosanitary) compliance |
| **Livelihoods** | Creates rural non-farm jobs | Skill gaps and migration of trained workforce |
| **Quality** | Maintains sensory + safety attributes | Microbial contamination in humid tropical climate |
| **Logistics** | Enables long-distance distribution | India's average cold chain gap of **~70%** (NCCD data) |

### 3.4 Major Categories of Postharvest Losses

| Category | Description | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical losses** | Spillage, breakage, weight loss | Torn sacks of grain, crushed tomatoes |
| **Physiological losses** | Sprouting, senescence, wilting | Onion sprouting, leafy vegetable wilting |
| **Pathological losses** | Microbial decay, fungal rots | Botrytis in grapes, anthracnose in mango |
| **Entomological losses** | Insect/pest damage | Weevils in pulses, fruit flies in citrus |
| **Biochemical losses** | Quality, flavor, nutrient change | Rancidity in oilseeds, browning in apples |
| **Mechanical losses** | Cuts, bruises during handling | Banana finger damage, apple bruising |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "classify postharvest losses," always present them in a **tabular 5- or 6-category classification** with one real-life crop example for each category. Examiners reward **concrete examples**.

### 3.5 The "5-S Framework" of PHM Challenges

A student-friendly mnemonic for KTU answers:

1. **S**tructure — Inadequate cold chain infrastructure (India has only ~8,000 cold stores vs. a need of ~70,000).
2. **S**kills — Low technical know-how among farmers and handlers.
3. **S**tandards — Poor adherence to grades, packaging norms, and traceability.
4. **S**ustainability — Energy cost, refrigerant emissions, plastic waste.
5. **S**ystems — Fragmented supply chains, multiple intermediaries, low digitalization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 PHM Stakeholder Responsibility Matrix

| Stakeholder | Primary PHM Responsibility | Common Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| Farmer | Harvest timing, field handling, primary cleaning | Inadequate tools, distress sale pressure |
| Aggregator | Collection, primary sorting | Low storage at aggregation points |
| Transporter | Cold chain integrity, on-time delivery | Old reefer fleet, fuel cost pass-through |
| Cold Store Operator | Temperature & RH control | Power supply unreliability, ammonia leaks |
| Processor | Quality grading, preservation | Limited capacity utilization |
| Retailer | Display, FIFO rotation | Wastage in retail backrooms |
| Consumer | Home storage, date awareness | Improper refrigerator use |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define postharvest management. List any four operations performed under it.

**Model Answer:**
Postharvest management refers to the set of techniques and operations applied to agricultural produce between harvest and consumption to preserve quality, reduce loss, and add value.
Four key operations are:
1. **Cleaning and washing** — removal of soil, microbes, pesticide residues.
2. **Sorting and grading** — classification by size, color, ripeness, defect.
3. **Packaging** — protective and MAP packaging for transport and shelf-life.
4. **Cold storage** — temperature-controlled storage to slow respiration.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for 4 operations. No need to elaborate.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the significance of the **cold chain** in postharvest management of perishables?

**Model Answer:**
The cold chain is a temperature-controlled, uninterrupted supply chain that maintains perishables (F&V, fish, dairy, meat) within their safe temperature range from harvest to retail. Its significance includes:
- Slows down **respiration and microbial growth** ($Q_{10}$ principle).
- Extends **shelf life** — e.g., strawberries from 1 day at 25°C to ~7 days at 0–2°C.
- Preserves **sensory and nutritional quality**.
- Enables **geographically distant markets**, reducing gluts and stabilizing farmer income.
- Reduces **post-harvest losses** by **30–60%** in fruits and vegetables.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for significance. Examiner looks for $Q_{10}$/shelf-life mention.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, aligned to KTU 2024 Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **importance of postharvest management** with reference to food security, economics, and sustainability. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **major categories of postharvest losses** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Importance of PHM — Food Security, Economics, Sustainability (7 marks):**

1. **Food Security Dimension (2 marks):**
   - The FAO estimates ~13.2% of global food is lost post-harvest; in India, perishables lose 20–40%.
   - Reducing this loss is equivalent to "producing more without farming more" — directly feeding the growing population.
   - Aligns with **UN SDG 2 (Zero Hunger)** and **SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production)**.

2. **Economic Dimension (2.5 marks):**
   - **Value addition:** A raw mango at ₹20/kg can become a value-added pulp at ₹120/kg — a 6x uplift.
   - **Cold chain multiplier:** Every ₹1 invested in cold chain infrastructure generates ₹3–4 of value (NCCD, GoI).
   - **Export earnings:** APEDA estimates India's horticulture export potential at $20+ billion if PHM standards are met.
   - **Distress-sale mitigation:** Farmers currently sell 60–70% of produce immediately post-harvest due to lack of storage, accepting low prices. PHM enables warehousing credit (e-NWR — electronic Negotiable Warehouse Receipts).

3. **Sustainability Dimension (2.5 marks):**
   - **Embedded resources saved:** ~25% of agricultural freshwater, ~20% of cropland, and ~3.3 Gt CO₂e emissions can be avoided by halving food loss.
   - **Waste reduction** in municipal solid waste (organic waste = ~50% of Indian urban waste).
   - **Circular economy:** Spoiled produce can become compost, biogas, or animal feed — PHM systems integrate this.

**(b) Major Categories of Postharvest Losses (7 marks):**

| # | Category | Mechanism | Example (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical** | Spillage, breakage, weight loss | Torn gunny bags of rice, broken eggs |
| 2 | **Physiological** | Sprouting, wilting, senescence | Onion sprouting in storage, lettuce wilting |
| 3 | **Pathological** | Fungal, bacterial decay | Botrytis rot in grapes, anthracnose in mango |
| 4 | **Entomological** | Insect/pest damage | Pulse beetle in stored dal, fruit fly in guava |
| 5 | **Biochemical** | Enzymatic degradation, rancidity | Polyphenol oxidase browning in apple, oil rancidity in groundnut |
| 6 | **Mechanical** | Bruising, cuts during handling | Banana finger damage, tomato impact bruising |

Each category explained with mechanism + crop example = full 7 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, aligned to KTU 2024 Pattern]**
**(a)** Describe the **major challenges in postharvest management** in developing countries like India, with reference to infrastructure, technology, policy, and market linkage. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A cold storage facility is designed to store **8,000 kg of apples**. The apples must be cooled from **25°C to 1°C** in **10 hours**. The specific heat of apples is **3.6 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the **sensible heat load (kW)** and estimate the **total refrigeration capacity** required, assuming a 25% safety margin for latent, conduction, and miscellaneous heat gains. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Challenges in PHM in India (7 marks):**

1. **Infrastructure Gap (2 marks):**
   - India has ~8,000 cold stores with ~32 million MT capacity against an estimated need of ~70 million MT (NCCD).
   - **70%+ gap** in reefer vehicles; rail reefer network is underused.
   - **Pack house deficit** at farm-gate clusters.

2. **Technology & Skill Deficit (1.5 marks):**
   - Low adoption of CA/MAP storage; pre-cooling missing in most farms.
   - Inadequate training of farmers in maturity indexing, harvest handling.

3. **Policy & Institutional Challenges (1.5 marks):**
   - Multiple ministries (Agriculture, Food Processing Industries, FSSAI, APEDA) — fragmented governance.
   - **APMC mandi** system sometimes restricts direct farmer-to-retailer supply chain models.
   - Subsidy delivery delays under MIDH/NHM schemes.

4. **Market Linkage & Capital (1 mark):**
   - High interest rates (12–14%) on cold chain CAPEX.
   - Multiple intermediaries (5–6) compressing farmer share to ~30–35% of consumer rupee.

5. **Climate & Energy (1 mark):**
   - Kerala/coastal regions have high humidity (RH 80–95%) — increases spoilage.
   - Power cuts and high diesel costs for backup generators increase OPEX.

**(b) Numerical — Sensible Heat Load Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 8{,}000$ kg
- $\Delta T = 25 - 1 = 24$ K
- $c_p = 3.6$ kJ/kg·K
- $t = 10$ hours $= 36{,}000$ seconds

**Step 1: Sensible Heat (kJ):**
$$
Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 8000 \times 3.6 \times 24
$$

$$
Q_{sensible} = 691{,}200 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to kW (dividing by time in seconds):**
$$
Q_{sensible, kW} = \frac{691{,}200}{36{,}000} = 19.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Add 25% safety margin for latent + conduction + miscellaneous gains:**
$$
Q_{total} = 19.2 \times 1.25 = 24.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Final Answer & Engineering Interpretation:**
- The refrigeration system should be specified at **24 kW (~6.5 TR – tonnes of refrigeration)** capacity.
- Selecting the next standard size up (~30 kW or 8.5 TR) is recommended for cyclic load flexibility.

**Answer:** Sensible heat load = **19.2 kW**; Total recommended capacity = **24 kW**.

*(Distribution: Step 1 = 1.5 marks, Step 2 = 1.5 marks, Step 3 = 2 marks, Step 4 = 1 mark, Final answer boxed = 1 mark.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget to convert hours to seconds** when computing kW from kJ — a common 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Always state the safety margin assumption** explicitly when adding conduction/latent/infiltration heat loads.
> 3. **Round up** to the next commercially available compressor size in design questions.
> 4. In **theory questions**, never list only "cold storage" as a challenge — examiners want **5–6 categories** with **Indian context** (NCCD data, NHM/MIDH scheme, APMC).
> 5. **Do not confuse** "post-harvest loss" with "food waste" — losses are pre-consumer, waste is at consumer stage. Examiners deduct marks for this interchange.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Postharvest Management Module**

- **Definition:** PHM = all interventions from harvest to consumer to minimize loss and maximize value.
- **FAO global PHL benchmark:** ~**13.2%**; **India perishables PHL:** ~**20–40%**.
- **Six categories of PHL:** Physical, Physiological, Pathological, Entomological, Biochemical, Mechanical.
- **Cold chain** = uninterrupted temperature-controlled logistics; critical for perishables.
- **$Q_{10}$ rule:** Respiration/deterioration rate **doubles per 10°C rise** — fundamental to all storage design.
- **Water activity ($a_w$):** Microbial growth threshold ~**0.6**; reducing $a_w$ extends shelf life.
- **Four pillars of PHM importance:** Food security, Nutrition, Economics, Sustainability.
- **Five "S" challenges in India:** Structure, Skills, Standards, Sustainability, Systems.
- **Key Indian schemes to cite in answers:** MIDH, PMKSY, e-NWR, APEDA, MoFPI's PMFME.
- **Engineering must-know formulas:** PHL %, $Q_{10}$, cold storage load (kW), value addition %.
- **Real-world examples to quote:** Alphonso mango export to Japan (VHT), Kerala banana to Gulf (reefer), pomegranate to EU (MAP).
- **Kerala/KTU-specific context:** High humidity tropical climate, spice value chain, coconut & banana as flagship perishables, FPO-led cluster models.
- **Exam mantra:** Always pair a **concept** with a **number** and an **example** in your answer — examiners reward specificity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Chain Technology** refers to the integrated, temperature-controlled system of equipment, processes, and monitoring instruments that ensure the unbroken preservation of perishable food products across the entire supply chain — from the point of harvest/slaughter/processing to the final consumer's table — by maintaining each product within its biologically and microbiologically safe temperature range.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Major Technologies in Maintaining Cold Chain** encompasses the **hardware engineering, thermodynamic processes, and digital monitoring platforms** that physically sustain the required low-temperature environment at each node (pre-cooling, cold storage, refrigerated transport, retail display, and last-mile delivery) of the perishable food pipeline.

### 1.1.1 Operational Hierarchy of a Cold Chain

| Stage | Typical Temperature Window | Primary Technology |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Pre-cooling** | Ambient $\to$ 4 °C | Hydro, Vacuum, Forced-Air |
| **Cold Storage** | $-25$ °C to $+4$ °C | VCR / VAR Cold Rooms, CA Chambers |
| **Reefer Transport** | $-25$ °C to $+8$ °C | Reefer Containers (ISO 1496), PCM Boxes |
| **Retail Display** | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Open/Closed Display Refrigerators |
| **Last-Mile / Home** | $-18$ °C to $+4$ °C | Domestic Fridge, Passive PCM Packs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The major technologies specifically asked are — (i) Refrigeration Systems (VCR, VAR, Cryogenic), (ii) Cold Storage Architecture, (iii) Refrigerated Transport Technologies, (iv) Modified/Controlled Atmosphere Packaging, (v) Pre-cooling Methods, (vi) Phase Change Materials (PCMs), and (vii) Temperature Monitoring & IoT-enabled Traceability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Biological Time Machine"**
> Imagine a fresh tomato as a tiny ticking clock. The enzymes inside it, plus aerobic bacteria and mould spores on its skin, are steadily winding the hands toward spoilage. Heat acts as the **accelerator pedal** of that clock. A cold chain is essentially a **biological time machine** that slows the clock down dramatically.
>
> Each technology — from the fan in a forced-air cooler to the compressor in a reefer truck — is a different tool designed to **pull heat OUT of the clock's vicinity** as quickly and continuously as possible. A *hydro-cooler* is like plunging the clock into ice water; a *vacuum cooler* is like putting it in a pressure chamber where evaporating water drags heat away; a *cryogenic freezer* is like flash-freezing the clock with liquid nitrogen so its hands literally cannot move.

### 1.2.1 Why a Cold Chain Fails Without These Technologies

- **Respiration heat** (in fresh produce): strawberries release $\approx 20\text{–}50$ mW/kg of metabolic heat — cumulatively heating a pallet of berries enough to spoil them in hours if uncooled.
- **Microbial doubling**: psychrotrophic spoilage bacteria double every $6\text{–}8$ h even at $4$ °C.
- **Lipid oxidation**: accelerates $2\text{–}4\times$ for every $10$ °C rise above recommended storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU must-knows):**
> - **Chilled zone**: $0$ °C to $+4$ °C — dairy, fresh meat, fish, leafy greens.
> - **Frozen zone**: $\le -18$ °C — long-term storage of meat, seafood, ice cream.
> - **Deep frozen**: $\le -25$ °C — premium seafood, pharma-grade foods.
> - **Specific heat of water**: $c_w = 4.186$ **kJ/(kg·K)**.
> - **Latent heat of fusion of water**: $L_f = 334$ **kJ/kg**.
> - **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations of Cold Chain Technology

Every cold-chain technology ultimately performs one fundamental operation: **heat extraction**. The effectiveness of that extraction is governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and quantified by the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.

### 2.1.1 The Vapor Compression Refrigeration (VCR) Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The workhorse of industrial and transport refrigeration. It moves heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold room / reefer container) to a high-temperature reservoir (ambient atmosphere) using a circulating **refrigerant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Logical Steps of VCR (Why & How):**
>
> 1. **Compression (Low-pressure vapor $\to$ High-pressure vapor)**
>    * *Why:* Compressing a gas raises its temperature (per $PV = nRT$), pushing it above ambient so it can dump heat.
>    * *How:* An electrically-driven screw/piston compressor raises refrigerant pressure typically from **$\sim 2$ bar (saturation $\sim -10$ °C)** to **$\sim 12\text{–}15$ bar (saturation $\sim 35\text{–}50$ °C)**.
>
> 2. **Condensation (High-pressure vapor $\to$ High-pressure liquid)**
>    * *Why:* The hot, high-pressure vapor rejects its latent + sensible heat to ambient air (or cooling water) through a finned condenser coil.
>    * *How:* At constant pressure, refrigerant temperature drops to saturation temperature and then condenses, releasing $L_{vap}$.
>
> 3. **Expansion (High-pressure liquid $\to$ Low-pressure two-phase mixture)**
>    * *Why:* Sudden pressure drop across a throttling (expansion) valve causes **flash evaporation** — a fraction of the liquid instantly vaporizes, absorbing $L_{vap}$ and plummeting the temperature of the remaining mixture.
>    * *How:* Enthalpy is conserved (isenthalpic process: $h_3 = h_4$).
>
> 4. **Evaporation (Low-pressure two-phase $\to$ Low-pressure vapor)**
>    * *Why:* The cold liquid refrigerant boils inside the evaporator coil immersed in the cold-room air. As it vaporizes, it absorbs latent heat $\Rightarrow$ **this is the useful cooling effect**.
>    * *How:* Fan-forced convection moves warm return air across the coil fins at temperature differential $\Delta T = T_{room} - T_{evap}$ (typically $8\text{–}12$ °C).

### 2.1.2 The Vapor Absorption Refrigeration (VAR) Cycle

Used where electricity is scarce or unreliable — ideal for **off-grid dairy cold rooms** in rural Kerala. Instead of a mechanical compressor, a **thermal compressor** (generator + absorber + pump) uses a heat source (LPG, kerosene, solar thermal, waste heat) to drive the refrigerant.

- **Common pair:** Ammonia–Water ($\text{NH}_3$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$) for industrial; **Lithium Bromide–Water** ($\text{LiBr}$–$\text{H}_2\text{O}$) for air-conditioning (above $0$ °C only).
- **Why chosen in food cold chain:** Very low electrical consumption (only the solution pump + fans), no moving compressor parts, can run on biomass.

### 2.1.3 Cryogenic Cooling Technology

Uses the **latent heat of vaporization** of ultra-cold liquefied gases:

| Cryogen | Boiling Point (1 atm) | Latent Heat of Vaporization |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Liquid Nitrogen ($\text{LN}_2$) | $-196$ °C | **199 kJ/kg** |
| Liquid $\text{CO}_2$ | $-78.5$ °C (sublimes) | **571 kJ/kg** (sublimation) |
| Liquid Helium (He) | $-269$ °C | 21 kJ/kg (used only in pharma/space) |

> Used in **individual quick freezing (IQF)** of shrimp, berries, and in long-duration **passive cold-chain containers** (e.g., pharma vaccine shipments).

### 2.1.4 Phase Change Materials (PCMs) — The "Thermal Battery"

PCMs absorb/release enormous amounts of heat at a **constant phase-transition temperature**. For food cold-chain:

- **Water-based PCMs** (with nucleating agents): phase change at $0$ °C, store **$\sim 334$ kJ/kg**.
- **Salt-hydrate PCMs** (e.g., Glauber's salt $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \cdot 10\text{H}_2\text{O}$): phase change at $+32$ °C (used in HTLT — High Temperature, Low Temperature processing).
- **Organic PCMs** (paraffins, fatty acids): phase change tunable between $-20$ °C and $+80$ °C.

PCM packs are sealed into insulated boxes for **last-mile delivery of vaccines, seafood, and ice cream**.

### 2.1.5 Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP) vs Controlled Atmosphere (CA) Storage

| Feature | MAP | CA Storage |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Gas composition** | Set **once** at packing | Continuously **monitored & adjusted** |
| **Typical gases** | $\text{O}_2$: $2\text{–}5\%$; $\text{CO}_2$: $5\text{–}20\%$; $\text{N}_2$: balance | $\text{O}_2$: $1\text{–}3\%$; $\text{CO}_2$: $1\text{–}5\%$; rest $\text{N}_2$ |
| **Application scale** | Retail packs | Bulk warehouse chambers |
| **Shelf-life extension** | $50\text{–}100\%$ | $200\text{–}400\%$ (apples: 6 mo $\to$ 12 mo) |
| **Common products** | Cut salads, fresh pasta, poultry | Apples, kiwifruit, pears |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MAP/CA Works (The 'How'):** Lower $\text{O}_2$ slows aerobic respiration and oxidation of lipids/pigments. Elevated $\text{CO}_2$ suppresses spoilage bacteria and mould growth. The combined effect **slows senescence** without freezing.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Oriented)

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are **assessable** in KTU Part B numericals. Memorize the symbols and units.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Thermal & Refrigeration Equations

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Quantity / Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{prod}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat to cool product | kJ |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{lat}} = m_{\text{freeze}} \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat to freeze water in food | kJ |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{resp}} = m \cdot R_h \cdot t$ | Respiration heat of fresh produce | kJ |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{trans}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{avg}} \cdot t / 1000$ | Transmission heat gain through walls/ceiling/floor | kJ |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{inf}} = V \cdot n \cdot \rho_{\text{air}} \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T \cdot t$ | Heat from door opening / air infiltration | kJ |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{equip}} = P_{\text{fan+light}} \cdot t$ | Heat from evaporator fans, lights, defrost | kJ |
| 7 | $\displaystyle \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{comp}}} = \frac{\text{Desired Cooling}}{\text{Work Input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible (ideal) COP (T in **K**) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ | Refrigerating Effect per kg refrigerant | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $m_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{load}}}{\text{RE}}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | kg/s |
| 11 | $W_{\text{comp}} = m_{\text{ref}} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor power input | kW |
| 12 | $\text{THR} = W_{\text{comp}} + Q_L$ | Total Heat Rejection at condenser | kW |
| 13 | $t_{\text{cool}} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)}{h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{lm}}}$ | Newton cooling time for pre-cooling | s or min |
| 14 | $\Delta T_{\text{lm}} = \dfrac{\Delta T_i - \Delta T_f}{\ln(\Delta T_i / \Delta T_f)}$ | Log-Mean Temperature Difference | K or °C |
| 15 | $P_{\text{evap}} = P_{\text{sat}}(T_{\text{evap}})$ | Saturation pressure at evaporator T | Pa or bar |
| 16 | $\eta_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}}$ | Relative (Second-Law) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 17 | $\text{SL} = \dfrac{\text{SLA with cooling}}{\text{SLA without cooling}}$ ratio expressed as factor | Shelf-life multiplier | dimensionless |
| 18 | $R = \frac{V}{nT} = 8.314$ **J/(mol·K)** | Universal gas constant (for psychrometrics) | J/(mol·K) |
| 19 | $C_{\text{PCM}} = m_{\text{PCM}} \cdot L_f$ | Cold energy stored in a PCM pack | kJ |
| 20 | $Q_{\text{refr,truck}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \cdot t$ | Simplified reefer-truck heat load | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $T_L$ and $T_H$ in Carnot COP **must be in Kelvin**.
> - For foods, $c_p \approx 3.5\text{–}4.0$ kJ/(kg·K) above freezing; $c_p \approx 1.9\text{–}2.1$ kJ/(kg·K) below freezing.
> - $\text{O}_2 < 1\%$ for $\ge 21$ days $\to$ anaerobic fermentation risk (alcohol/ethyl acetate off-flavours).
> - Typical U-values: insulated panel wall $\approx 0.25$ **W/(m²·K)**; older brick wall $\approx 1.0$ **W/(m²·K)**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility Across Food Industry Verticals

- **Fisheries (Kerala context):** Shrimp IQF lines use **cryogenic + spiral freezers**; export reefer containers run at **$-20$ °C**.
- **Dairy:** Bulk milk chilling centers use **direct expansion (DX) plate coolers** within **2$ h$** of milking.
- **Horticulture:** Banana ripening chambers use **ethylene-controlled cold rooms** at $13\text{–}15$ °C.
- **Pharma-food (probiotics, vaccines):** **Phase Change Material (PCM)** cold boxes validated to **$+2$ °C to $+8$ °C** for $96\text{+} h$ last-mile delivery.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Set 1 — Heat-Load Calculation for a Mango Cold Store**
> *A cold room in Palakkad stores 10,000 kg of 'Alphonso' mangoes. The mangoes are cooled from $28$ °C field temperature to $13$ °C storage temperature in $12$ hours. The cold room dimensions are $6$ m $\times 5$ m $\times 4$ m. The insulated wall, ceiling and floor have an average U-value of $0.30$ W/(m²·K). The door is opened 20 times per day, each time admitting $1.5$ m³ of warm ambient air at $32$ °C, $60\%$ RH. Evaporator fan motor is $1.5$ kW running $18$ h/day. Lighting is $0.4$ kW for $6$ h/day. Estimate the total refrigeration load in kW and the required compressor size.*

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the Sensible Heat Load on the Product ($Q_{\text{prod}}$)**

We use the formula $Q_{\text{prod}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.

Given:
- $m = 10{,}000$ kg
- $c_p = 3.78$ kJ/(kg·K) (mango, above-freezing, standard reference)
- $\Delta T = T_i - T_f = 28 - 13 = 15$ °C (= $15$ K, since the difference is what matters)

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{prod}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 10{,}000 \; \text{kg} \times 3.78 \; \tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \times 15 \; \text{K} \\
&= 567{,}000 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** Multiplying the three numbers yields $\text{kg} \cdot \tfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \cdot \text{K} = \text{kJ}$, the correct unit of heat energy.

---

**Step 2 — Convert Heat to Power (kW) Over 12 Hours**

Since $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$, we have $12 \text{ h} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 43{,}200 \text{ s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{prod}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{prod}}}{t_{\text{cool}}} = \frac{567{,}000 \; \text{kJ}}{12 \times 3600 \; \text{s}} \\
&= \frac{567{,}000}{43{,}200} \; \text{kJ/s} \\
&\approx 13.125 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** Energy $\div$ time gives the average **rate** of heat removal, which is the **refrigeration capacity** in kW. This is the value the compressor must provide on average during the pull-down phase.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Respiration Heat of Mangoes ($Q_{\text{resp}}$)**

Mango respiration rate at $13$ °C $\approx R_h = 35$ mW/kg = $0.035$ W/kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{resp}} &= m \cdot R_h = 10{,}000 \times 0.035 = 350 \; \text{W} = 0.35 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** Respiration is metabolic heat continuously released by the *living* fruit. Unlike $Q_{\text{prod}}$, it is **continuous** (not a one-time pull-down load) and therefore expressed as a rate directly in kW.

---

**Step 4 — Compute Transmission Heat Load ($Q_{\text{trans}}$)**

Room surface area (6 walls approximation):
- Two walls $6 \times 4 = 24$ m² each $\to 48$ m²
- Two walls $5 \times 4 = 20$ m² each $\to 40$ m²
- Ceiling $6 \times 5 = 30$ m²
- Floor $6 \times 5 = 30$ m²
- Total $A = 48 + 40 + 30 + 30 = 148$ m²

$\Delta T_{\text{ambient-cold}} = 32 - 13 = 19$ K. Operating $24$ h/day.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{trans}} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 0.30 \; \tfrac{\text{W}}{\text{m²·K}} \times 148 \; \text{m²} \times 19 \; \text{K} \\
&= 843.6 \; \text{W} \approx 0.844 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** This is heat constantly leaking *into* the cold room through walls/ceiling/floor. Multiply the three factors — conductivity (U) $\times$ area (A) $\times$ driving temperature difference (ΔT) — to get the steady-state heat inflow.

---

**Step 5 — Compute Infiltration Heat Load ($Q_{\text{inf}}$)**

Each door opening admits $1.5$ m³ of air at $32$ °C into a room at $13$ °C.

Using $\rho_{\text{air}} \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air,opening}} &= \rho \cdot V = 1.2 \times 1.5 = 1.8 \; \text{kg per opening} \\
Q_{\text{per open}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 1.8 \times 1.005 \times (32 - 13) \\
&= 1.8 \times 1.005 \times 19 \\
&\approx 34.37 \; \text{kJ per opening}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $20$ openings per day:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{inf,day}} &= 34.37 \times 20 = 687.4 \; \text{kJ/day} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{inf}} &= \frac{687.4}{24 \times 3600} = \frac{687.4}{86{,}400} \approx 0.00796 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** Door-opening infiltration is usually a small contributor (sub-1\%) compared to product + transmission, but is **critical** in tropical climates with frequent loading/unloading (Kerala ports, fish markets).

---

**Step 6 — Compute Equipment Heat ($Q_{\text{equip}}$)**

Fan: $1.5$ kW $\times 18$ h/day. Lighting: $0.4$ kW $\times 6$ h/day.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{fan}} &= 1.5 \times \frac{18}{24} = 1.125 \; \text{kW (time-weighted average)} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{light}} &= 0.4 \times \frac{6}{24} = 0.100 \; \text{kW} \\
\dot{Q}_{\text{equip}} &= 1.125 + 0.100 = 1.225 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** We time-average because the compressor may not run 24 h, but the average daily heat dissipation from electrical accessories is what the cooling system must remove.

---

**Step 7 — Sum All Loads to Get Total Refrigeration Requirement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{total}} &= \dot{Q}_{\text{prod}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{resp}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{trans}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{inf}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{equip}} \\
&= 13.125 + 0.350 + 0.844 + 0.008 + 1.225 \\
&= 15.552 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Apply a Safety Factor and Size the Compressor**

Industry practice: oversize by $15\text{–}25\%$ for pull-down margin and future load growth.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{\text{design}} &= \dot{Q}_{\text{total}} \times 1.20 \\
&= 15.552 \times 1.20 \approx 18.66 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Estimate Compressor Shaft Power Using COP**

Assume $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 2.8$ (typical for medium-temperature R-404A system):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{comp}} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{design}}}{\text{COP}} = \frac{18.66}{2.8} \\
&\approx 6.66 \; \text{kW (shaft power)} \\
&\approx 8.93 \; \text{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** $1$ HP (mechanical) $\approx 0.7457$ kW, so $6.66 / 0.7457 \approx 8.93$ HP.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Set 2 — PCM Cold-Pack Sizing for Vaccine Last-Mile Delivery

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A last-mile vaccine courier needs to keep a payload of $5$ kg of mRNA vaccines at $\le -70$ °C for $24$ h using dry-ice ($\text{CO}_2$ solid, $L_{\text{sub}} = 571$ kJ/kg, density $1.56$ kg/L) inside an insulated polystyrene box. The box has overall U-value equivalent to $1.2$ W/K and the ambient is $30$ °C. Estimate the minimum dry-ice mass required.

**Step 1 — Total Heat Gain to Resist Over 24 h**

The interior must stay at $-70$ °C, ambient is $30$ °C.

$\Delta T = 30 - (-70) = 100$ K. Time $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{gain}} &= U \cdot \Delta T \cdot t \\
&= 1.2 \times 100 \times 86{,}400 \\
&= 10{,}368{,}000 \; \text{J} = 10{,}368 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dry-Ice Mass Required**

Dry ice absorbs heat primarily via **sublimation** (solid $\to$ gas $\text{CO}_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{dry-ice}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{gain}}}{L_{\text{sub}}} = \frac{10{,}368 \; \text{kJ}}{571 \; \text{kJ/kg}} \\
&\approx 18.16 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{18.16}{1.56} \approx 11.6 \; \text{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical conversion note:** Sublimation requires 571 kJ per kg — far higher than water's freezing 334 kJ/kg — which is why dry ice is the gold standard for ultra-cold vaccine transport.

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technology | Best Use Case | Temperature Range | Energy Form | Major Limitation |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **VCR** | General cold rooms, reefers | $-40$ °C to $+10$ °C | Electrical | HFC refrigerant GWP |
| **VAR** | Off-grid dairy, rural | $0$ °C to $+10$ °C | Thermal (waste/LPG) | Bulky, slow response |
| **Cryogenic ($\text{LN}_2$/$\text{CO}_2$)** | IQF, pharma, premium seafood | $-196$ °C to $-78$ °C | Cryogen | High recurring cost |
| **Vaccum Cooling** | Leafy veg, mushrooms | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Electrical | Only for porous products |
| **Hydro Cooling** | Mangoes, sweet corn | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Electrical + water | Water use, washing damage |
| **Forced-Air Cooling** | Berries, stone fruit | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Electrical | Dehydration if RH low |
| **MAP** | Retail fresh cuts, salads | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Pre-mixed gas | Single-use |
| **CA Storage** | Bulk apples, kiwifruit | $0$ °C to $+5$ °C | Gas + scrubber | High capex |
| **PCM Pack** | Last-mile, vaccine, ice cream | Tunable | Passive | Finite duration |
| **IoT + RFID Logger** | All cold-chain stages | N/A (monitoring only) | Battery | Connectivity dead zones |
