```mermaid
        L10[GPS plus GSM Telemetry for Cold Chain Visibility]
    end

    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L1
    L3 --> L7
    L4 --> L7
    L7 --> L8
    L5 --> L10
    L7 --> L6
    L9 --> L7
```

> **Engineering Significance:** The **T-floor** (T-bar floor in rail cars, T-groove in trucks) is not decorative — it is the **plenum** that distributes chilled air uniformly. Without it, temperature stratification can be **±5 °C** between floor and ceiling, causing localized spoilage.

## 4.4 Decision Logic — Selecting MAP Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startMAP[Choose MAP Strategy] --> q1{Is product red meat?}
    q1 -->|Yes| mapHM[70 to 80 percent O2 plus 20 to 30 percent CO2 - Maintains Bloom]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is product fish or fresh produce?}
    q2 -->|Yes, fish| mapFish[20 to 40 percent CO2 plus 60 to 80 percent N2 - Inhibits Spoilage Bacteria]
    q2 -->|Yes, produce| q3{Is product leafy or cut vegetable?}
    q3 -->|Yes| mapLeafy[2 to 5 percent O2 plus 5 to 10 percent CO2 - Low O2 Inhibits Browning]
    q3 -->|No, whole fruit| mapFruit[3 to 8 percent O2 plus 3 to 10 percent CO2 - Balanced]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating RH and Moisture Content:** Many students write "fruits need moisture" instead of "RH 85–95 %." Lose **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting Chilling Injury:** When listing banana storage, students say "0 °C" — wrong. Banana suffers chilling injury below **13 °C**. Lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing Poultry & Red Meat Limits:** Poultry transit at 0–2 °C has **half** the shelf life of beef. Many students quote 28 days for chicken. Lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring Ethylene Segregation:** When asked "why not load banana and tomato together?" — students say "they spoil." Correct reason: **ethylene-induced ripening**. Lose **1 mark**.
> 5. **Misquoting K-value Limits:** Fresh fish K-value is **< 20 %** for sashimi-grade, **> 60 %** for spoilage. Do not mix up.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why must bananas not be refrigerated below 13 °C during transit? What is the consequence?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bananas are **chilling-sensitive** tropical fruits. When stored below **13 °C** for more than a few days, they develop **chilling injury** — symptoms include:
1. **Surface pitting and blackening** (due to polyphenol oxidase activation).
2. **Failure to ripen** (inhibition of ethylene-mediated ripening enzymes).
3. **Off-flavour development** (irreversible cellular damage).

The transit temperature for bananas is **13–15 °C** with **85–90 % RH**, ensuring normal climacteric ripening post-transit. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the K-value used in assessing marine product freshness. What is the threshold for rejection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **K-value** is a chemical freshness index expressed as a percentage of **ATP-related catabolites** (HxR + Hx) relative to the **total ATP breakdown products**:

$$
K = \frac{[\text{HxR}] + [\text{Hx}]}{[\text{ATP}] + [\text{ADP}] + [\text{AMP}] + [\text{IMP}] + [\text{HxR}] + [\text{Hx}]} \times 100
$$

**Thresholds:**
- **K < 20 %** → Sashimi-grade (very fresh)
- **K 20–40 %** → Fresh, edible
- **K 40–60 %** → Stale, limit of acceptability
- **K > 60 %** → **Spoiled — rejected for human consumption** **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE full question (14 marks)** with internal choice. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **storage requirements of fresh fruits and vegetables during transit**. Discuss temperature, humidity, ethylene management, and packaging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Temperature Requirements (2 marks):**
Fruits and vegetables are classified into three thermal groups:
- **Tropical produce** (banana, mango, papaya): **8–15 °C** — below this, chilling injury occurs.
- **Subtropical produce** (citrus, tomato): **5–10 °C**.
- **Temperate produce** (apple, grape, leafy veg): **0–2 °C**.

The transit temperature must align with the commodity's **lowest safe temperature** to minimize respiration while avoiding **chilling injury**. Pre-cooling before loading is mandatory to remove **field heat**.

**(ii) Humidity Requirements (2 marks):**
- Most F&V require **85–95 % RH** to prevent **transpiration losses**.
- **Onion, garlic, and dried products** require **low RH (60–70 %)** to prevent mould.
- **Water loss > 5–8 %** causes visible shrivelling and economic loss.

**(iii) Ethylene Management (2 marks):**
- **Climacteric fruits** (banana, mango, apple) **produce ethylene** and must be **well-ventilated**.
- **Non-climacteric produce** (cucumber, leafy greens) are **ethylene-sensitive** — they wilt, abscise, or develop disorders.
- **Compartmentalization** in transit trucks (separate bays) is a standard practice.

**(iv) Packaging (1 mark):**
- **Ventilated crates** for whole produce.
- **MAP** (2–5 % O₂, 5–10 % CO₂) for cut vegetables.
- **Plastic liners** to maintain humidity.

**(7 marks distributed as: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)**

---

**(b)** Discuss the **storage requirements of marine products during transit**. Include pre-treatment, temperature, ice-to-fish ratio, and quality indices. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Pre-Treatment at Harbour (2 marks):**
- Fish must be **chilled within minutes** of capture to slow autolysis and bacterial growth.
- Methods: **Icing in boxes (RSW — Refrigerated Sea Water)** or **CSW (Chilled Sea Water)**.
- Sorting, grading, washing, and **gutting** (for some species) occur pre-transit.

**(ii) Temperature & Ice (2 marks):**
- **Whole fresh fish: 0 ± 0.5 °C** maintained by **1:1 (or 1.5:1 in tropics) ice:fish ratio**.
- **Frozen fish: ≤ −18 °C**, ideally **−25 to −30 °C** for long-haul reefer vessels.
- **Live crustaceans:** 4–8 °C in aerated seawater.
- Ice must be **potable-water ice** to prevent *Vibrio* contamination.

**(iii) Quality Indices (2 marks):**
- **K-value** (< 20 % fresh; > 60 % spoiled).
- **TVB-N (Total Volatile Basic Nitrogen)** < 35 mg N/100 g.
- **TMA-N (Trimethylamine Nitrogen)** — proxy for bacterial spoilage.
- **Histamine** < 50 mg/kg (FDA limit) for scombroid fish.
- **Sensory**: shiny eyes, red gills, firm flesh, sea-fresh odour.

**(iv) Transit Mode & HACCP (1 mark):**
- Reefer trucks with **continuous temperature loggers**.
- **HACCP plan** mandatory; CCP at loading and unloading.
- Avoid **thaw-freeze cycles** which cause drip loss and protein denaturation.

**(7 marks distributed as: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the **transit storage requirements of milk and milk products** with those of **meat and meat products**. Highlight differences in temperature, humidity, microbial risk, and packaging. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Milk & Milk Products** | **Meat & Meat Products** |
|---|---|---|
| **Optimal Transit Temp** | **2 – 6 °C** (pasteurized); **−18 °C** (frozen dairy) | **0 – 2 °C** (chilled); **−18 to −25 °C** (frozen) |
| **Humidity** | **N/A** (sealed packaging) — surface condensation risk | **85 – 90 % RH** (prevents surface drying/freezer burn) |
| **Dominant Spoilage** | **Psychrotrophic bacteria** (*Pseudomonas*, *Bacillus*) | **Pseudomonas + Listeria + Salmonella**; lipid oxidation |
| **Shelf Life (Chilled)** | **2–7 days** (pasteurized); **14–21 days** (UHT once opened) | **7–14 days** (poultry); **28–42 days** (beef) |
| **Packaging** | **Aseptic cartons, HDPE bottles, insulated tankers** | **Vacuum packs, VSP, MAP (high-O₂ for red meat)** |
| **Transit Vessel** | **Stainless steel insulated tanker** with CIP system | **Reefer truck** with hanging rails for carcasses |
| **Key CCP** | **Cold chain integrity**, post-pasteurization contamination | **HACCP**, temperature control, avoid cross-contamination |

**(Distribution: 1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for synthesis/comparison statement = 7 marks)**

> **Synthesis (1 mark):** While both require **strict cold chain (< 4 °C)**, dairy's transit is dominated by **microbial doubling time in liquid medium**, while meat's transit is dominated by **surface microbial load and oxidative rancidity**. Dairy is **sealed-system transit**, while meat requires **humidity-controlled, MAP or vacuum packaging**.

---

**(b)** A **dairy processor in Kerala** needs to transport **15,000 L of pasteurized milk at 4 °C** over a **6-hour journey** in **35 °C ambient temperature**. The tanker is insulated with **120 mm PUF (k = 0.020 W/m·K)** with a **surface area of 55 m²**. Calculate:
1. Heat ingress through walls.
2. Total heat load in 6 hours.
3. Required compressor capacity (with 1.6 safety factor). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $V = 15{,}000 \text{ L}$, $T_{milk} = 4 \text{ °C}$, $T_{amb} = 35 \text{ °C}$
- $k = 0.020 \text{ W/m·K}$, $d = 0.120 \text{ m}$, $A = 55 \text{ m²}$
- $t = 6 \text{ h}$

**Step 1 — Heat Ingress Through Walls (2 marks)**

$$
Q_{wall} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d} = \frac{0.020 \times 55 \times (35 - 4)}{0.120}
$$

$$
Q_{wall} = \frac{0.020 \times 55 \times 31}{0.120} = \frac{34.10}{0.120} \approx 284.17 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q_{wall} \approx 284 \text{ W (0.284 kW)}\;}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Heat Load in 6 Hours (2.5 marks)**

$$
Q_{total,6h} = 0.284 \times 6 = 1.704 \text{ kWh}
$$

Converting to kJ:

$$
Q_{total,6h} = 1.704 \times 3600 = 6{,}134.4 \text{ kJ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q_{total} \approx 6{,}134 \text{ kJ over 6 hours}\;}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature Rise of Milk if No Cooling (1 mark)**

Mass of milk: $m = 15{,}000 \times 1.032 = 15{,}480 \text{ kg}$

Specific heat of milk: $c_p = 3.93 \text{ kJ/kg·°C}$

$$
\Delta T_{milk} = \frac{Q_{total}}{m \cdot c_p} = \frac{6{,}134.4}{15{,}480 \times 3.93} = \frac{6{,}134.4}{60{,}836.4} \approx 0.101 \text{ °C}
$$

So milk would warm only ~0.1 °C in 6 hours — insulation is excellent.

**Step 4 — Compressor Sizing (1.5 marks)**

$$
Q_{comp} = 1.6 \times Q_{wall} = 1.6 \times 0.284 = 0.454 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;Q_{comp} \approx 0.45 \text{ kW} \; (\approx 0.61 \text{ HP})\;}
$$

**(7 marks distributed as: 2 + 2.5 + 1 + 1.5)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

### 🥬 Fruits & Vegetables
- ✅ Climacteric vs Non-climacteric distinction is **mandatory**.
- ✅ Tropical produce: 8–15 °C; Temperate: 0–2 °C; Leafy: 0–2 °C with 95 % RH.
- ✅ Ethylene segregation: **Banana ≠ Lettuce co-loading**.
- ✅ MAP: 2–5 % O₂, 5–10 % CO₂ for cut vegetables.
- ✅ Chilling injury below **13 °C** for banana, **8 °C** for mango.
- ✅ Onion/garlic require **LOW humidity** (60–70 %).

### 🥛 Milk & Milk Products
- ✅ **Raw milk: ≤ 4 °C**, pasteurized: 2–6 °C.
- ✅ UHT: ambient shelf-stable.
- ✅ Frozen dairy (ice cream): **≤ −18 °C**, ideally −25 °C.
- ✅ Critical hazard: **Psychrotrophs at temperature abuse**.
- ✅ Tanker insulation: 75–100 mm PUF; k ≈ 0.022 W/m·K.

### 🥩 Meat & Meat Products
- ✅ Chilled: **0–2 °C**; Frozen: −18 to −25 °C.
- ✅ Poultry has **shortest shelf life** (~7 d chilled).
- ✅ Vacuum skin packaging inhibits *Pseudomonas*.
- ✅ High-O₂ MAP (70–80 %) maintains **red bloom** in beef.
- ✅ HACCP CCP at slaughter-to-refrigeration transition.

### 🐟 Marine Products
- ✅ **Most perishable** of all food classes.
- ✅ K-value: < 20 % fresh; > 60 % spoiled.
- ✅ TVB-N limit: 35 mg/100 g; Histamine limit: 50 mg/kg.
- ✅ Ice-to-fish ratio: **1:1 (temperate), 1.5:1 (tropical)**.
- ✅ Scombroid fish (tuna, mackerel) → histamine hazard.
- ✅ Frozen fish reefer: **−25 to −30 °C** for long-haul.

### 🔑 Universal Constants to Memorize
- ✅ **Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3** for most biological reactions.
- ✅ **FATTOM** acronym: **F**ood, **A**cidity, **T**ime, **T**emperature, **O**xygen, **M**oisture — microbial growth factors.
- ✅ **TCM (Time-Temperature Indicator)** on packaging shows cumulative chill-chain.
- ✅ **Cold chain break** = any excursion > 2 °C above setpoint for > 30 min.

### 📐 Key Equations to Remember
- ✅ $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}$ (Conduction heat ingress)
- ✅ $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{\Delta T}}$ (Biological rate ratio)
- ✅ $K = \dfrac{[\text{HxR}] + [\text{Hx}]}{\sum \text{ATP-catabolites}} \times 100$ (Freshness index)

### ⚠️ Examiner's Golden Rules
- ❌ Never write "fruits need moisture" — write "85–95 % RH."
- ❌ Never say "banana at 0 °C" — that is **chilling injury**.
- ❌ Never equate UHT and pasteurized milk storage.
- ❌ Never forget that **histamine is heat-stable** — cooking doesn't help.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Storage Requirements During Transit**
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme NEP-Aligned Standards | PEFTT636**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Packaging and Packaging Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Packaging and Packaging Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Packaging** in the context of food supply chain management is defined as the coordinated system of preparing goods for transport, distribution, storage, retailing, and end-use, such that the product reaches the consumer in a safe, hygienic, and quality-preserved condition. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEFTT636, packaging is recognized as the **fourth pillar of the food industry** — sitting alongside procurement, processing, and distribution — and serves as the **silent salesman, protector, and information carrier** for a food product.

From an engineering standpoint, packaging is the science, art, and technology of enclosing or protecting products for distribution, storage, sale, and use. It refers to the process of design, evaluation, and production of packages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Packaging is a coordinated system of enclosing products for handling, distribution, storage, retailing, and consumption, while satisfying consumer requirements regarding protection, identification, and communication at optimum cost.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Packaging by Function

Packaging in food supply chains is broadly classified into **three hierarchical tiers**:

| Level | Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Primary Packaging** | The layer that directly contacts the food product | Milk pouch, biscuit wrapper, tetra pack inner liner |
| **2** | **Secondary Packaging** | The outer wrapper that groups primary packages | Cardboard box containing 12 juice tetra packs, shrink wrap over cans |
| **3** | **Tertiary Packaging** | Used for bulk handling, warehousing, and transport | Wooden pallet, corrugated fiberboard shipping container, stretch-wrapped load |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** A single milk tetra pack is primary; the corrugated box holding 24 tetra packs is secondary; the wooden pallet carrying 50 such boxes is tertiary. This triad is a **favorite 3-mark KTU question**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Courier" Intuition

Imagine you are sending a fragile glass bottle of homemade honey to a relative 1,500 km away.

- **Primary Packaging** = the bubble wrap wrapped **directly around the bottle** (touches the product, prevents breakage, holds the honey).
- **Secondary Packaging** = the **cardboard gift box** you place the wrapped bottle in (groups it, carries the label, looks attractive).
- **Tertiary Packaging** = the **large wooden crate** the courier company loads onto the truck along with 200 other boxes (efficiency in transport, stackability).

Now imagine the **bubble wrap** (primary) is a *plastic film*, the **box** (secondary) is *paperboard*, and the **crate** (tertiary) is *wood*. The *type of material* matters just as much as the *level of packaging*. This is what we mean by **Types of Packaging and Packaging Materials**.

---

## 1.4 Modern Packaging Categories (KTU 2024 Emphasis)

The 2024 KTU syllabus places strong emphasis on the following contemporary packaging categories:

1. **Flexible Packaging** — Plastics, foils, paper laminates that can be easily bent or shaped.
2. **Rigid Packaging** — Glass, metal cans, rigid plastics that hold their shape.
3. **Aseptic Packaging** — Sterile product filled into sterile packaging under sterile conditions.
4. **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** — Air inside the package is replaced with a specific gas mix.
5. **Vacuum Packaging** — Air is evacuated before sealing.
6. **Active Packaging** — Interacts with the product (e.g., oxygen scavengers, ethylene absorbers).
7. **Intelligent / Smart Packaging** — Monitors and communicates product condition (e.g., time-temperature indicators, freshness sensors).
8. **Edible / Bio-based Packaging** — Made from biodegradable or edible polymers (e.g., starch films, chitosan coatings).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:** The global food packaging market is valued at approximately **USD 350 billion (2024)** and growing at a CAGR of **~5.5%**. Flexible packaging accounts for **> 40%** of this market by volume.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Food Package

Every food packaging system must satisfy the **"5 P's" framework** for the food it protects. KTU examiners frequently test the logic behind each:

- **P₁ — Preserve:** Inhibit microbiological, chemical, and physical deterioration.
- **P₂ — Protect:** Shield against mechanical shock, vibration, compression, light, oxygen, and moisture.
- **P₃ — Present:** Provide surface for branding, labeling, nutritional info, and barcodes.
- **P₄ — Portion:** Deliver the product in correct quantity and convenient unit size.
- **P₅ — Permit:** Allow traceability (batch codes, FSSAI marks, MRP, expiry date).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Packaging Materials — The Master Map

Below is the KTU-recommended classification tree for **packaging materials**, which students must internalize:

$$
\text{Packaging Materials} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Glass} \\[2pt]
\text{Metal} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Tinplate Steel} \\ \text{Aluminium} \\ \text{Stainless Steel} \end{cases} \\[4pt]
\text{Paper \& Paperboard} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Kraft paper} \\ \text{Sulphite paper} \\ \text{Corrugated Fiberboard} \\ \text{Glassine / Greaseproof} \end{cases} \\[4pt]
\text{Plastics} \Rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)} \\ \text{PE (LDPE, HDPE)} \\ \text{PP (Polypropylene)} \\ \text{PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)} \\ \text{PS (Polystyrene)} \\ \text{EVOH, PVDC (Barriers)} \end{cases} \\[4pt]
\text{Composite / Laminated Materials} \\[2pt]
\text{Bio-based / Edible Films}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.3 Detailed Material Properties — Cheat Sheet for KTU Exams

| Material | Key Properties | Barrier to O₂ | Barrier to H₂O | Typical Food Use | Recyclability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Glass** | Rigid, inert, transparent, heat-sealable | Excellent | Excellent | Jams, sauces, beer, wine | **100% recyclable, infinitely** |
| **Tinplate Steel** | Strong, magnetic, hermetically sealable | Excellent | Excellent | Canned vegetables, soups, milk powder | Recyclable (ferrous) |
| **Aluminium (foil)** | Lightweight, ductile, opaque | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | Chocolate, foil pouches, aseptic cartons | Highly recyclable |
| **Kraft Paper** | Porous, biodegradable, printable | Poor | Poor | Flour bags, sugar bags, outer wraps | Compostable |
| **Corrugated Fiberboard** | High stiffness-to-weight ratio | Poor | Poor | Shipping cartons, pizza boxes | Recyclable |
| **PET** | Clear, strong, lightweight | Moderate | Good | Beverage bottles, salad trays | Widely recycled (#1) |
| **LDPE** | Flexible, transparent, heat-sealable | Poor | Good | Bread bags, frozen food bags, squeeze bottles | Recyclable (#4) |
| **HDPE** | Rigid, opaque, chemical resistant | Moderate | Excellent | Milk jugs, detergent, cereal liners | Recyclable (#2) |
| **PP** | High heat resistance (melting ~165°C) | Moderate | Excellent | Yogurt cups, microwaveable trays, bottle caps | Recyclable (#5) |
| **PVC** | Clear, tough, oil-resistant | Good | Good | Cling film, blister packs | Limited (#3) |
| **PS (Foamed)** | Insulating, lightweight | Poor | Poor | Egg cartons, cups, fast food clamshells | Rarely recycled (#6) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students confuse *PET* with *PVC*. PET is the standard water/soda bottle material; PVC is used in cling wrap and blister packs. PET is **safer for food contact** because PVC can release plasticizers (e.g., phthalates) into fatty foods.

---

## 2.4 Permeability — The Core Engineering Concept

The performance of a packaging material is quantified by its **Permeability (P)**, defined by the equation:

$$
P = \frac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Quantity of gas/vapour transmitted (cm³)
- $L$ = Thickness of the film (m)
- $A$ = Area of the film (m²)
- $\Delta p$ = Partial pressure difference across the film (Pa)
- $t$ = Time (s)
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient (units: cm³·m / m²·s·Pa)

**Real-World Utility:** This equation is used by packaging engineers to calculate the **shelf life** of oxygen-sensitive products (e.g., roasted coffee, potato chips). A high-permeability plastic (e.g., LDPE) is unsuitable for vacuum-packed coffee; instead, a multi-layer laminate with **EVOH (Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol)** is used as the oxygen barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU-Expected):** Permeability is also commonly expressed in **cm³·mm/m²·day·atm**. Always cite the units given in the question, or convert.

---

## 2.5 Migration Concept

**Migration** is the transfer of substances from packaging into food. The **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** set by FSSAI and EU regulations is:

$$
\text{OML} \leq 10 \text{ mg/dm}^2 \text{ of food contact surface}
$$

Any individual substance must not exceed **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** values, e.g., vinyl chloride monomer in PVC ≤ **1 mg/kg** of food.

---

## 2.6 Active & Intelligent Packaging Mechanisms

- **Oxygen Scavengers:** Use iron powder or ascorbic acid → absorb residual O₂ inside the package.
- **Ethylene Absorbers:** Use potassium permanganate → slow ripening of climacteric fruits (banana, mango).
- **Moisture Absorbers:** Silica gel or calcium chloride → keep dried foods crisp.
- **Time-Temperature Indicators (TTIs):** Color-changing labels based on enzymatic or diffusion reactions.
- **Freshness Sensors:** pH-sensitive dyes that change color when amines (from spoilage) accumulate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shelf Life Estimation Using Permeability

A common KTU problem: *Estimate the shelf life of a packaged product using the permeability of the packaging material.*

### Setup

A snack food (e.g., potato chips) is packed in a flexible pouch. Critical quality limit: oxygen ingress of **$V_{O_2, crit}$ = 10 cm³** above which oxidative rancidity sets in.

**Given:**
- Pouch dimensions: 0.20 m × 0.15 m → Area $A = 0.03 \text{ m}^2$
- Film thickness: $L = 100 \mu m = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- Permeability coefficient of material: $P = 5 \times 10^{-15} \text{ cm}^3 \cdot m / m^2 \cdot s \cdot Pa$
- Partial pressure difference across film (outside air vs inside N₂): $\Delta p = 21,000 \text{ Pa}$ (≈ 21% of 101,325 Pa)

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

The amount of oxygen transmitted per unit time (transmission rate) is given by:

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}
$$

Where **OTR** = Oxygen Transmission Rate (cm³/s).

### Step 2 — Substitute values into the equation

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (0.03) \cdot (21{,}000)}{(100 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerator calculation

$$
(5 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (0.03) \cdot (21{,}000)
$$

Multiplying $5 \times 10^{-15}$ by $0.03$ gives:

$$
5 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{-2} = 15 \times 10^{-17}
$$

Multiplying by $21{,}000$ ($\approx 2.1 \times 10^{4}$):

$$
15 \times 10^{-17} \times 2.1 \times 10^{4} = 31.5 \times 10^{-13} = 3.15 \times 10^{-12}
$$

### Step 4 — Divide by film thickness

$$
\text{OTR} = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{-12}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-4}} = 3.15 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate shelf life

Shelf life = Critical volume of O₂ / OTR:

$$
t_{shelf} = \frac{V_{O_2, crit}}{\text{OTR}} = \frac{10 \text{ cm}^3}{3.15 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

$$
t_{shelf} = 3.17 \times 10^{8} \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to days

$$
t_{shelf} = \frac{3.17 \times 10^{8}}{86{,}400} \approx 3{,}670 \text{ days} \approx 10.05 \text{ years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** With this excellent barrier material, the theoretical shelf life of potato chips (oxygen-driven spoilage only) is ~10 years. In practice, moisture pickup and lipid oxidation kinetics reduce this to 6–9 months.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Packaging Material

| Food Category | Primary Spoilage Mode | Recommended Material | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbonated beverages** | CO₂ loss, oxidation | PET bottle + aluminium lid | Clear, strong, good CO₂ barrier |
| **UHT Milk (long life)** | Microbial, light damage | 6-layer Tetra Pak (Paper/PE/Al/PE) | Aseptic, opaque, sterile |
| **Canned tomato sauce** | Microbial | Tinplate steel can | Hermetic seal, retortable |
| **Fresh red meat** | Microbial, oxidation | Modified Atmosphere (O₂:CO₂:N₂ = 70:20:10) in PP/EVOH tray | High O₂ to maintain red color |
| **Fresh bread** | Staling (moisture loss) | LDPE bag with micro-perforations | Moisture balance, freshness |
| **Roasted coffee** | Oxidation, CO₂ release | Foil-laminate with one-way valve | Impermeable to O₂; valve releases CO₂ |
| **Crisps / Chips** | Oxidation, moisture gain | Metallized BOPP / PE laminate | O₂ + light + moisture barrier |
| **Frozen peas** | Freezer burn | HDPE/LDPE heavy-duty bag | Moisture-resistant at -18°C |
| **Edible oil** | Oxidation, light | Amber glass or PET + UV barrier | Blocks UV light |

---

## 3.3 Composite / Multi-Layer Packaging Structure

Modern food packaging often uses **laminated structures** to combine the strengths of multiple materials:

$$
\text{Typical "Tetra Brik" Layer Stack (outside to inside)}
$$

$$
\underbrace{\text{PE}}_{\text{outer print/heat-seal}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Print}}_{\text{ink layer}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Paperboard}}_{\text{structure, 70-80\%}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{PE}}_{\text{adhesion}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{Aluminium foil}}_{\text{barrier (6-9 μm)}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{PE}}_{\text{inner food contact}}
$$

**Why this works:**
- **Paperboard** = Mechanical rigidity.
- **Aluminium** = Oxygen + light barrier (≈ 0).
- **Polyethylene layers** = Heat-sealable, moisture-proof, food-safe.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why is aluminium only 6–9 μm thick?"* Because aluminium itself is expensive and rigid — making it thinner reduces cost and allows flexibility, while a paperboard carrier provides rigidity. At < 20 μm, aluminium becomes flexible and is called **aluminium foil**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Active vs Intelligent Packaging

```mermaid
graph LR
    modernA["Modern Smart Packaging"] --> actB["ACTIVE PACKAGING"]

    actB --> actF["Moisture Absorber Silica Gel"]
    actB --> actG["Antimicrobial Film Ag or Essential Oils"]

    intC --> intD["Time Temperature Indicator TTI"]
    intC --> intE["Freshness Sensor pH dye"]
    intC --> intF["RFID Tag for traceability"]
    intC --> intG["Gas Sensor for leak detection"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction for KTU:** *Active packaging* **changes** the internal environment of the package (e.g., removes oxygen). *Intelligent packaging* **monitors and reports** the state of the product without changing it (e.g., a color-changing freshness sensor).

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary packaging with suitable food industry examples.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Primary Packaging:** The innermost layer in **direct contact** with the food. Its primary role is to *contain* the product and protect it from immediate contamination. *Examples:* The plastic pouch around pasteurized milk, the glass jar of jam, the aluminium foil wrapping chocolate.
- **Secondary Packaging:** Wraps or holds together **multiple primary packages** for retail display and branding. *Examples:* The printed cardboard box containing six tetra packs of juice, the shrink-wrap sleeve over a 4-pack of yogurt.
- **Tertiary Packaging:** Used for **bulk handling, storage, and shipping**. It facilitates palletization, transport efficiency, and logistics tracking. *Examples:* The corrugated fiberboard shipping case, the wooden pallet, the stretch film wrapping the pallet.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "active packaging." Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Active packaging refers to packaging materials that **actively interact** with the food product or its internal headspace to **extend shelf life, improve safety, or enhance sensory properties**, beyond merely acting as a passive barrier.

**Examples:**
1. **Oxygen Scavenger Sachet** — An iron-based sachet placed inside a packet of processed meat absorbs residual oxygen, preventing oxidative rancidity and aerobic microbial growth.
2. **Ethylene Absorber Pad** — Potassium permanganate-based pad placed with fresh fruits (e.g., bananas, mangoes) absorbs the ripening gas ethylene, slowing senescence.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** Classify the different types of food packaging materials. Discuss the properties and applications of **glass** and **metals** (tinplate and aluminium) as food packaging materials. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**. Discuss its applications, gas compositions, and the typical plastic films used. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of packaging materials:**
Food packaging materials are classified into six broad families: glass, metals, paper and paperboard, plastics, composite/laminates, and bio-based/edible films. Each family has characteristic properties that suit different food types and supply chain conditions.

**Glass as a packaging material:**
- **Composition:** Silica (SiO₂) + soda ash (Na₂CO₃) + lime (CaO) + cullet (recycled glass).
- **Properties:** Inert (no chemical reaction with food), transparent, impermeable to gases and vapors, withstands high retort temperatures (~121°C), does not impart odor or taste, **infinitely recyclable** without quality loss.
- **Disadvantages:** Heavy (high transport cost), fragile, energy-intensive to manufacture.
- **Applications:** Carbonated beverages (beer, soft drinks), jams, sauces, baby food jars, wine bottles, pharmaceutical vials.

**Tinplate steel as packaging material:**
- **Composition:** Low-carbon steel sheet coated electrolytically with a thin layer of tin (~0.4–1.5 μm).
- **Properties:** High mechanical strength, magnetic (easy separation in recycling), excellent barrier to light, gas, and moisture when hermetically sealed, can withstand thermal processing (retorting at 121°C for canned food).
- **Applications:** Canned vegetables, canned soups, canned fish, edible oils, aerosol cans, crown caps for bottles.

**Aluminium as packaging material:**
- **Properties:** Lightweight (~1/3 the density of steel), excellent ductility (can be rolled to 6–9 μm foils), outstanding barrier against oxygen, moisture, light, and odors; non-toxic and corrosion-resistant when properly coated/lacquered.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot be used with highly acidic or saline foods without internal lacquer; more expensive than tinplate.
- **Applications:** Beverage cans (soda, beer), aluminium foil wraps for chocolate and butter, laminated pouches, aluminium trays for ready meals, aerosol cans, collapsible tubes for toothpaste and sauces.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP):**
MAP is a preservation technique in which the **gaseous atmosphere surrounding a food inside a package is altered** to a composition different from normal air (≈ 78% N₂, 21% O₂, 0.04% CO₂) in order to extend shelf life. The atmosphere is created by either **passive equilibration** (product and package interacting) or **active flushing** (replacing air with a specific gas mixture before sealing).

**Gas Compositions Used:**

| Food Product | O₂ (%) | CO₂ (%) | N₂ (%) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Red meat | 70 | 20 | 10 | High O₂ retains bright red oxymyoglobin color |
| White fish | 30 | 40 | 30 | Lower O₂ prevents rancidity |
| Fresh-cut salad | 0–3 | 10–20 | 80 | Inhibits browning |
| Cheese | 0 | 30–100 | 0–70 | Inhibits mold |
| Bakery | 0 | 50–70 | 30–50 | Inhibits mold growth |

**Functions of Gases:**
- **O₂:** Maintains color (red meat), inhibits anaerobic pathogens.
- **CO₂:** Inhibits bacterial and mold growth; dissolves in food to form carbonic acid.
- **N₂:** Inert filler, prevents pack collapse.

**Plastic Films Used:**
Multilayer structures like **PE/EVOH/PE**, **PA/PE (Nylon/PE)**, **PET/PE**, and **PP/EVOH/PP** are preferred due to high gas barrier, puncture resistance, and heat-sealability. EVOH (Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol) provides exceptional oxygen barrier when dry.

**Applications:**
- Fresh red meat, poultry, seafood, fresh-cut fruits and vegetables, bakery products, ready-to-eat meals, cheese, and pasta.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q. (a)** What are the different types of **plastics** used in food packaging? Discuss the properties and applications of **PET, PE (LDPE & HDPE), and PP**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **intelligent packaging** systems. Describe time-temperature indicators (TTI), freshness sensors, and RFID tags with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 Marks

Plastics dominate modern food packaging (> 40% by volume globally) due to their lightweight, moldability, low cost, and tunable properties. Key plastics include:

**1. PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) — Resin Code #1**
- **Properties:** Clear, glossy, strong, lightweight, good oxygen and moisture barrier, good chemical resistance, recyclable.
- **Applications:** Carbonated soft drink bottles, water bottles, salad trays, peanut butter jars, ready-meal trays (CPET for oven use).

**2. LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene) — Resin Code #4**
- **Properties:** Highly flexible, transparent, excellent moisture barrier, poor oxygen barrier, low melting point (~110°C), easily heat-sealable.
- **Applications:** Bread bags, frozen food bags, cling film, squeeze bottles for honey and ketchup, inner liners for cereal boxes.

**3. HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene) — Resin Code #2**
- **Properties:** More rigid and stronger than LDPE, opaque, excellent moisture barrier, better chemical resistance, higher melting point (~130°C).
- **Applications:** Milk jugs, juice bottles, detergent containers (food-grade HDPE), grocery bags, cereal box liners, caps and closures.

**4. PP (Polypropylene) — Resin Code #5**
- **Properties:** High melting point (~165°C) — microwaveable, good clarity, excellent moisture barrier, moderate oxygen barrier, good chemical resistance.
- **Applications:** Yogurt cups, single-serve butter tubs, bottle caps, microwaveable ready-meal trays, hot-fill beverage containers, BOPP films for chips.

---

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 Marks

**Intelligent Packaging:**
Intelligent packaging is a system that **monitors the condition of the packaged food** and **communicates information** about its quality, freshness, and supply chain history to the consumer or supply chain stakeholder, *without directly altering the product's internal environment*. (This contrasts with active packaging, which *does* alter the environment.)

**1. Time-Temperature Indicators (TTI):**
- **Principle:** TTIs are smart labels that record the **cumulative thermal history** a product has experienced, since spoilage kinetics are temperature-dependent.
- **Mechanism:** Based on enzymatic reaction, diffusion of a colored dye, or polymer melt. A visible color change indicates whether the cold chain has been broken.
- **Examples:** *Fresh-Check®* (used on dairy and meat products), *3M MonitorMark®*, *ColdSNAP* for ice cream.
- **Working:** A central colored dot diffuses outward; if the outer ring darkens before the expiry date, the product was exposed to abuse temperatures.

**2. Freshness Sensors:**
- **Principle:** Detect **volatile compounds** released during spoilage (e.g., amines, sulfides, organic acids) and produce a visible color change.
- **Examples:** *SensorQ®* for fish spoilage (amine-sensitive dye). *RipeSense®* for pear ripeness (responds to aroma compounds).
- **Working:** A pH-sensitive dye reacts with biogenic amines (TVB-N) released by microbial spoilage, changing from yellow → blue.

**3. RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) Tags:**
- **Principle:** Contain a microchip and antenna; communicate wirelessly to a reader, storing data such as batch number, origin, expiry, and temperature history.
- **Types:** Passive (no battery, powered by reader's signal) and Active (battery-powered, can log temperature throughout cold chain).
- **Examples:** Walmart's pallet-level RFID for traceability, dairy farms tracking cheese wheels, tuna export from Maldives to Europe.

**Real-World Utility:**
Intelligent packaging reduces food waste by giving consumers and retailers accurate, *real-time* information about whether a product is still safe and of high quality — a key goal of the KTU food supply chain curriculum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Types of Packaging" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "type of packaging" with "level of packaging"** — A KTU answer on *types* expects discussion of materials (glass, plastic, metal, etc.), while a question on *levels* expects primary/secondary/tertiary. Read carefully.
> 2. **Writing only resin codes (#1–#7) without properties** — Always pair the code with **at least two properties and one application**. Code-only answers get 1 mark out of 3.
> 3. **Forgetting units in permeability problems** — Always state the permeability coefficient's units (cm³·m / m²·s·Pa) explicitly. Marks are deducted for "bare numbers."
> 4. **Mixing up MAP and vacuum packaging** — In MAP, gas composition is *altered* but **not removed**; in vacuum packaging, gases are *essentially removed*. They are not synonyms.
> 5. **Active ≠ Intelligent packaging** — Examiners mark this distinction strictly. *Active* changes the environment; *Intelligent* reports on it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Three Levels of Packaging:** Primary (touches food), Secondary (groups primary), Tertiary (bulk transport).
- 🔹 **Material Classification:** Glass, Metal, Paper, Plastics, Composites, Bio-based.
- 🔹 **Glass:** Inert, transparent, infinitely recyclable; **drawback** = heavy + fragile.
- 🔹 **Tinplate Steel:** Steel + tin coating; used for **retort cans**; magnetic (easy recycling).
- 🔹 **Aluminium:** Lightweight, outstanding barrier, foil at **6–9 μm**, used in cans + laminates.
- 🔹 **Plastics — PET (#1):** Beverage bottles, clear, strong. **LDPE (#4):** Bread bags, flexible. **HDPE (#2):** Milk jugs, rigid. **PP (#5):** Yogurt cups, microwave-safe.
- 🔹 **Permeability Equation:** $P = QL/(A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t)$ — **must memorize for numerical problems.**
- 🔹 **MAP** = gas composition altered; **Vacuum** = gases removed; **Aseptic** = sterile product + sterile package.
- 🔹 **Active Packaging** *changes* the environment; **Intelligent Packaging** *monitors and reports.*
- 🔹 **OML** = 10 mg/dm²; **PVC** SML for VCM = 1 mg/kg.
- 🔹 **Tetra Pak** = PE / Paperboard / PE / Al-foil / PE multilayer — paperboard for rigidity, aluminium for barrier, PE for sealing.
- 🔹 **Bio-based Films:** Starch, chitosan, PLA, whey protein — sustainable, biodegradable alternatives.
- 🔹 **Permeability** is inversely proportional to film thickness → thicker film = better barrier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Export and Import Packaging and Labelling Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Export and Import Packaging and Labelling Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Export Packaging** is the specialized system of containment, protection, preservation, and presentation applied to food commodities intended for cross-border shipment, engineered to withstand the rigours of multimodal transit (road, rail, sea, air) while complying with the harmonized regulatory frameworks of the exporting nation, importing nation, transit nations, and the applicable international standardization bodies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Import Packaging** refers to the regulatory and technical specifications governing the receiving, inspection, de-containerization, and re-packaging of food consignments entering a domestic market, ensuring conformity with local labelling statutes, phytosanitary requirements, and consumer protection laws.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is examined under **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Apply packaging and labelling standards for domestic and international food trade"* — a **Level 3 (Apply)** and **Level 4 (Analyze)** cognitive mapping under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "International Diplomatic Courier" Intuition

Imagine you are sending a fragile traditional sweet (say, *Kerala Banana Halwa*) to a relative in London. You do not just toss it in a plastic bag. You:

1. **Primary wrap** the halwa in food-grade cling film (Primary Packaging).
2. **Secondary box** it in a printed cardboard carton bearing the product name (Secondary Packaging).
3. **Tertiary** place that carton inside a corrugated shipping case with thermocol, silica gel, and an *AGMARK* label, packed onto a wooden pallet (Tertiary Packaging).
4. **Document** attach the airway bill, phytosanitary certificate, FSSAI license copy, and CODEX compliance certificate (Documentation Layer).
5. **Mark** the outer case with directional arrows, "THIS SIDE UP," a UN 4G mark, and a barcode (Regulatory Marking Layer).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why five layers?** Because export/import is not just logistics — it is a **legal contract enforced in physical form**. Each layer answers one question: *Will the food arrive safe, identifiable, traceable, legal, and saleable?*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Play

| Parameter | Standard Value | Governing Body |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sea freight transit humidity tolerance** | **95% RH (Relative Humidity)** | ASTM D4169 |
| **Cold chain temperature for frozen foods** | **−18 °C ± 2 °C** | Codex Alimentarius |
| **Ambient shelf-stable transit temp** | **+25 °C** | ICH/FSSAI |
| **Standard pallet dimension (ISO)** | **1200 mm × 1000 mm (Euro)** or **1219 mm × 1016 mm (US)** | ISO 6780 |
| **20-ft container internal volume** | **~33 CBM (Cubic Metres)** | ISO 668 |
| **40-ft HC container internal volume** | **~76 CBM** | ISO 668 |
| **Maximum permissible moisture in packaging material** | **≤ 12% (corrugated fibreboard)** | IS 2771 |
| **Standard atmosphere for BCT test** | **23 °C, 50% RH** | TAPPI T 804 |

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **CBM (Cubic Metre)** is the universal volumetric unit in international freight — calculated as **Length × Breadth × Height (in metres)**. Sea freight is charged on **W/M (Weight or Measurement)** basis — whichever is greater between **1000 kg = 1 CBM** or actual weight.

## 1.4 The Four Pillars of Export/Import Packaging Compliance

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures this topic around **four functional pillars**:

1. **Protection Pillar** — Mechanical, chemical, biological, and physical barriers.
2. **Preservation Pillar** — Shelf-life extension via modified atmosphere, vacuum, aseptic, or retort technologies.
3. **Information Pillar** — Mandatory and voluntary labelling disclosures.
4. **Identification & Traceability Pillar** — Barcodes, lot codes, date marks, and origin marks.

Each pillar is **statutorily enforced** in cross-border trade. A failure in one pillar can result in **consignment rejection at the destination port**, financial loss, and legal penalty under the Customs Act, 1962 and FSSAI Act, 2006.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of Food Packaging

The International Safe Transit Association (ISTA) and the European Federation of Corrugated Board Manufacturers (FEFCO) recognize a **three-tier hierarchy**. This is the most frequently tested sub-topic in KTU Module 4.

### Tier I — Primary Packaging
This is the **layer in direct contact** with the food. Its functions are containment, protection, and consumer information delivery.

- **Materials:** Glass bottles (Type III soda-lime), PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate), HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene), LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene), PP (Polypropylene), aluminium foil (laminates), Aseptic Tetra Pak cartons, vacuum pouches (EVOH/EVA co-extruded), tin-plated steel cans.
- **Critical Properties:** Migration limits (overall ≤ **10 mg/dm²** or **60 mg/kg** food as per EU Regulation 10/2011), oxygen transmission rate (OTR), water vapour transmission rate (WVTR), and seal integrity (≥ **2.0 N/15 mm** for pouch seals).

### Tier II — Secondary Packaging
This **groups primary units** into a saleable SKU (Stock Keeping Unit). It carries the retail-facing label.

- **Materials:** Printed folding cartons (SBS — Solid Bleached Sulphate, FBB — Folding Box Board), shrink-wrap, paperboard sleeves, multipack carriers.
- **Functions:** Brand communication, barcoding (EAN-13/UPC-A), nutritional panel display, MRP (Maximum Retail Price) compliance for Indian exports.

### Tier III — Tertiary Packaging
This is the **logistics and distribution layer** engineered for transit stresses defined by **ASTM D4169** and **ISTA 3A/3E** test profiles.

- **Materials:** Corrugated fibreboard boxes (single-wall, double-wall, triple-wall), wooden crates (fumigated to **ISPM-15** standard), plastic crates, stretch-wrapped pallet loads, IBC (Intermediate Bulk Container) totes, flexitanks (for bulk liquids like edible oil — capacity up to **24,000 litres**).
- **Functions:** Vibration dampening, compression resistance, handling ergonomics, containerization efficiency.

## 2.2 Packaging Material Classification — Engineer's Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bursting Strength (BS)** of corrugated board is measured in **kg/cm²** as per **IS 1060 (Part IV)**. For export-grade boxes, the minimum BS is **8–10 kg/cm²** for single-wall and **14–20 kg/cm²** for double-wall.

| Flute Designation | Flute Height (mm) | Compression Strength | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A-Flute | 4.7 – 5.0 | High | Heavy-duty fruit, glass bottles |
| B-Flute | 2.1 – 3.0 | Low | Cans, printed cartons |
| C-Flute | 3.5 – 4.0 | Medium | General export cases (most common) |
| E-Flute | 1.0 – 1.5 | Very Low | Retail display boxes |
| BC-Flute (Double-wall) | 6.0 – 7.0 | Very High | Export of spices, processed foods |
| AAA-Flute (Triple-wall) | 12.0+ | Extreme | Industrial, military, refrigerated bulk |

## 2.3 Labelling — The Statutory Backbone

Labelling in international food trade is governed by a **layered regulatory stack** that students must memorize for KTU exams.

### Layer A — National Statutory Labels (Mandatory)
1. **FSSAI Label (India)** — Logo + **14-digit FSSAI License Number**. Mandatory for all domestically sold and exported Indian food products as per **FSSAI Packaging and Labelling Regulations, 2011** (last amended 2022).
2. **AGMARK** — For graded agricultural products (honey, ghee, spices, vegetable oils). Certifies **AGMARK Grade Standard** compliance.
3. **FPO (Fruit Products Order, 1955)** — Mandatory for processed fruit/vegetable products.
4. **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) ISI Mark** — Mandatory under BIS Act, 2016 for notified products (e.g., infant food, milk powder, packaged drinking water).

### Layer B — International Codex / Destination Market Labels
1. **Codex Alimentarius** — Global reference for food additives, contaminants, hygienic practices, and labelling. Published jointly by **FAO + WHO**.
2. **FDA (USA)** — Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act compliance; nutrition facts panel in **English units (oz, lb, kcal)**; allergen declaration under **FALCPA, 2004**.
3. **EU Regulation 1169/2011** — FIC (Food Information to Consumers) — mandatory allergen highlighting in **bold**, minimum font size **1.2 mm** (or **0.9 mm** if principal display panel ≤ 80 cm²), and "Best Before" / "Use By" date.
4. **Health & Nutritional Claims** — Regulated under **Regulation 1924/2006** in EU; "low fat," "high fibre," "sugar-free" claims need prior authorization.
5. **Halal / Kosher Marks** — Required for shipment to **Middle East, Indonesia, Malaysia** (Halal — issued by JAIN, MUI, ESMA) and **Israel/USA** (Kosher — OU, Star-K).

### Layer C — Logistics & Safety Labels
1. **UN Number** (e.g., UN 1170 for Ethanol) — Required for dangerous goods under **IATA DGR** (air) and **IMDG Code** (sea).
2. **Hazard Pictograms** — GHS (Globally Harmonized System) 9-symbol red-bordered diamond pictograms.
3. **Recycling Symbols** — Resin Identification Code (RIC), Möbius Loop, "Green Dot" (European packaging recovery).
4. **"This Side Up" Arrows** — Two opposing arrows on opposite vertical sides.
5. **"Keep Refrigerated" / "Keep Frozen"** — Thermochromic ink optional.
6. **Country of Origin Mark** — Mandatory under **WTO Rules of Origin** and **EU Regulation 626/2011**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Engineering & Commercial Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **most-asked numerical problems** in KTU Module 4 university examinations. Memorize the formula and the unit convention.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Volumetric Weight** | $V_{wt} = \dfrac{L \times B \times H}{5000}$ | kg | Air freight billing |
| 2 | **Volumetric Weight (LCL Sea)** | $V_{wt} = \dfrac{L \times B \times H}{6000}$ | kg | LCL sea freight |
| 3 | **Chargeable Weight** | $C_{wt} = \max(G_{wt},\, V_{wt})$ | kg | Determines freight cost |
| 4 | **Box Compression strength (McKee)** | $BCT = k \cdot E \cdot (h)^{2/3} \cdot Z^{1/3}$ | N | Stacked carton design |
| 5 | **Stack Load** | $P = n \cdot W \cdot 9.81$ | N | Max load on bottom box |
| 6 | **Safety Factor** | $SF = \dfrac{BCT}{P}$ | dimensionless | Must be **≥ 3.0** for export |
| 7 | **CBM per Carton** | $CBM = \dfrac{L \times B \times H}{10^6}$ (mm) or $= L \times B \times H$ (m) | m³ | Container stuffing |
| 8 | **Container Utilization** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Total CBM Loaded}}{\text{Container Capacity}}$ | % | Ideal **≥ 80%** |
| 9 | **Shelf-life activation** | $t_{s} = \dfrac{\ln(N_0 / N_{end})}{k_{T}}$ | days | Arrhenius kinetics |
| 10 | **Moisture Permeation** | $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | g/m²/day | Film barrier validation |

> Where:
> - $E$ = Edge Crush Test (ECT) of corrugated board (N/m)
> - $h$ = Caliper / thickness of board (mm)
> - $Z$ = Box perimeter (mm)
> - $k$ = Empirical McKee constant (**0.0291** for single-wall, **0.0272** for double-wall)
> - $n$ = Number of boxes stacked vertically
> - $W$ = Gross weight per box (kg)
> - $N_0, N_{end}$ = Initial and final microbial counts (CFU/g)
> - $k_T$ = Temperature-dependent rate constant (day⁻¹)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic — they are operational daily in **EXIM (Export-Import) trade**. A freight forwarder in Cochin Port loading 18,000 kg of freeze-dried mango pulp to Hamburg will:

1. Compute CBM per carton → fit cartons in 40-ft HC reefer.
2. Compute stack load on bottom pallet → select **BC-flute double-wall** with BCT ≥ 800 kgf.
3. Compute volumetric weight for air freight if expedite.
4. Validate pallet pattern using **ISO 3394** standard (1200×1000 mm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McKee's Formula (BCT equation) was empirically derived by Herbert F. McKee Jr. in 1963 and remains the gold standard in packaging engineering.** The 2/3 exponent on thickness and 1/3 exponent on perimeter are **not arbitrary** — they reflect the structural mechanics of edge-supported fibreboard columns under Euler-type buckling loads.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Sample Problem 1 — Freight Charge Calculation (Sea vs Air)

> **Problem:** A consignment of **250 cartons** of basmati rice is to be exported from Mumbai to Jeddah. Each carton measures **45 cm × 30 cm × 25 cm** and has a gross weight of **12.5 kg**. Sea freight rate is **USD 95 per CBM** and air freight rate is **USD 3.20 per kg chargeable weight**. Determine the cost-optimal mode of transport. The customs duty in Saudi Arabia is **5% of CIF value** and the goods are valued at **USD 18 per kg** FOB.

### Step 1: Compute Volume per Carton

$$V_{carton} = 45 \times 30 \times 25 = 33750 \,\text{cm}^3$$

Converting to cubic metres:

$$V_{carton} = 33750 \times 10^{-6} = 0.03375 \,\text{m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Consignment Volume

$$V_{total} = 250 \times 0.03375 = 8.4375 \,\text{CBM}$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Gross Weight

$$G_{wt} = 250 \times 12.5 = 3125 \,\text{kg}$$

### Step 4: Compute Volumetric Weight (Sea — LCL Conversion Factor 6000)

$$V_{wt} = \frac{L \times B \times H \times n}{6000} = \frac{45 \times 30 \times 25 \times 250}{6000}$$

$$V_{wt} = \frac{8437500}{6000} = 1406.25 \,\text{kg}$$

### Step 5: Compute Volumetric Weight (Air — Conversion Factor 5000)

$$V_{wt,air} = \frac{45 \times 30 \times 25 \times 250}{5000} = \frac{8437500}{5000} = 1687.5 \,\text{kg}$$

### Step 6: Compute Chargeable Weight for Each Mode

$$\text{Sea Chargeable} = \max(3125, 1406.25) = 3125 \,\text{kg} \;(\text{Actual weight governs})$$

$$\text{Air Chargeable} = \max(3125, 1687.5) = 3125 \,\text{kg} \;(\text{Actual weight governs})$$

### Step 7: Compute Sea Freight Cost

For sea freight, billing is on **CBM basis** (since cargo is general — W/M rule applies per CBM vs per 1000 kg):

$$C_{sea} = V_{total} \times 95 = 8.4375 \times 95 = \text{USD } 801.56$$

### Step 8: Compute Air Freight Cost

$$C_{air} = 3125 \times 3.20 = \text{USD } 10000.00$$

### Step 9: Cost Differential

$$\Delta C = C_{air} - C_{sea} = 10000.00 - 801.56 = \text{USD } 9198.44$$

### Step 10: Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{Sea freight is cheaper by USD } 9198.44 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Sea transport selected.}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The 12.5 kg/carton gross weight vs 1.406 kg/carton volumetric weight shows the cargo is **weight-dense** (rice grain density ≈ 0.75 g/cm³). For such commodities, sea is **always preferred** unless transit time < 3 days is critical.

---

## 3.2 Sample Problem 2 — McKee Box Compression Test Design

> **Problem:** A coffee exporter in Cochin packs roasted & ground coffee in corrugated boxes. The stack height in the warehouse is **6 boxes high**. Each box has gross weight **8 kg**, dimensions **40 cm × 30 cm × 25 cm**. The box is **C-flute single-wall** with thickness **4 mm** and ECT value **5.5 kN/m**. Determine:
> (a) The required BCT value.
> (b) The McKee-predicted BCT.
> (c) Whether the selected board passes the safety threshold.

### Step 1: Identify the McKee constant for C-flute single-wall

$$k = 0.0291 \;(\text{from packaging engineering literature})$$

### Step 2: Compute Perimeter (Z)

$$Z = 2 \times (L + B) = 2 \times (400 + 300) = 1400 \,\text{mm}$$

### Step 3: Compute Stack Load (P) Acting on Bottom Box

$$P = n \times W \times g = 6 \times 8 \times 9.81 = 470.88 \,\text{N}$$

### Step 4: Apply Safety Factor (3.0 for export)

$$BCT_{required} = SF \times P = 3.0 \times 470.88 = 1412.64 \,\text{N}$$

### Step 5: Compute McKee-Predicted BCT

$$BCT_{mckee} = k \times ECT \times h^{2/3} \times Z^{1/3}$$

$$BCT_{mckee} = 0.0291 \times 5500 \times (4)^{2/3} \times (1400)^{1/3}$$

Computing the fractional powers:

$$4^{2/3} = (2^2)^{2/3} = 2^{4/3} = 2.5198$$

$$1400^{1/3} = 11.1187$$

Now:

$$BCT_{mckee} = 0.0291 \times 5500 \times 2.5198 \times 11.1187$$

$$BCT_{mckee} = 0.0291 \times 5500 \times 28.0224$$

$$BCT_{mckee} = 0.0291 \times 154123.2$$

$$\boxed{BCT_{mckee} \approx 4484.99 \,\text{N}}$$

### Step 6: Compare and Validate

$$\text{Actual Safety Factor} = \frac{BCT_{mckee}}{P} = \frac{4484.99}{470.88} = 9.524$$

### Step 7: Conclusion

$$\boxed{\text{Actual SF = 9.52 > 3.0 — The C-flute single-wall box is highly over-engineered.} }$$

> **Design Recommendation:** A **B-flute single-wall** (cheaper, lighter) would suffice, reducing fibreboard cost by ~25%. This is a classical *value-engineering* insight in food packaging.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Export Packaging" and list its four functional objectives. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Export packaging is the engineered system of containment, protection, preservation, and information delivery applied to food commodities intended for cross-border shipment, designed to withstand multimodal transit stresses and comply with both origin and destination market regulations.

The **four functional objectives** are:

1. **Protection** — Resistance to mechanical shock, vibration, compression, moisture, dust, and microbial contamination.
2. **Preservation** — Extension of shelf life through barrier properties, modified atmosphere, or thermal processing.
3. **Information** — Communication of mandatory product, safety, and traceability data via labels and marks.
4. **Identification & Traceability** — Lot coding, barcoding, and origin marking to enable supply chain visibility and recall management.

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of the FSSAI logo and the 14-digit license number on pre-packaged food in India? (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **FSSAI logo** is the official mark of the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India, mandated under the **FSSAI Packaging and Labelling Regulations, 2011** (as amended in 2022). Its presence on a pre-packaged food product certifies that the product is manufactured, packed, and distributed under a valid FSSAI license/registration, and conforms to the food safety standards prescribed under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**.

The **14-digit license number** is a unique numerical identifier issued to every Food Business Operator (FBO). The first digit indicates the state of operation, the next two digits indicate the year of issue, and the remaining digits form a unique FBO code. This number enables **traceability** in case of food safety incidents, allows consumers to **verify authenticity** via the FSSAI Food Safety Compliance System (FoSCoS) portal, and is **mandatory for all packaged food sold in India**, including imported products.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In McKee problems, students **commonly forget to convert ECT units** (kN/m vs N/m). 1 kN/m = 1000 N/m — wrong conversion = wrong BCT.
> 2. Students often use **1000 kg = 1 CBM** for sea LCL — this is wrong. Sea LCL uses **6000 divisor** for volumetric weight.
> 3. In labelling questions, students write "**FPO**" instead of "**FSSAI**" for current regulations — FPO was subsumed into FSSAI in 2011.
> 4. McKee constant is asked as a constant — do NOT derive it; cite value 0.0291.
> 5. In Halal/Kosher questions, students often forget that the certificate must be from an **accredited body**, not self-declared.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks): Packaging Engineering & Cost Optimization

**Question A:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

(a) Explain the **three-tier hierarchy of food packaging** with suitable examples for an export consignment of **spices from Kerala to the USA**. State the relevant Indian and international standards. (7 Marks)

(b) A shipment of **600 cartons of turmeric powder** is to be exported from Kochi to Los Angeles. Each carton is **50 cm × 40 cm × 30 cm** and weighs **15 kg gross**. The freight forwarder quotes **USD 110 per CBM** for sea freight and **USD 4.50 per kg** for air freight (chargeable weight basis). Compute:
- (i) Total consignment volume in CBM
- (ii) Total gross weight
- (iii) Volumetric weight (air)
- (iv) Total cost by sea and by air
- (v) Recommend the mode of transport. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **three-tier hierarchy** as per ISTA/FEFCO standards consists of:

**Tier I — Primary Packaging:** This is the packaging layer in direct contact with the food product. Its primary function is to **contain, protect, and preserve** the food, while ensuring no chemical migration occurs (migration limit ≤ **10 mg/dm²** per EU Regulation 10/2011). For spices, examples include:
- **LDPE pouches** (50–80 micron) for individual retail sale
- **Met-PET + LDPE laminates** for oxygen-barrier protection (OTR < 1 cm³/m²/day)
- **Glass jars** (Type III soda-lime) for premium retailed turmeric

Applicable standards: **IS 1797** (spices and condiments), **AGMARK Grade Standard** for turmeric (curcumin content ≥ 2% for export).

**Tier II — Secondary Packaging:** This layer **groups multiple primary units** into a saleable SKU. Examples include:
- **Printed Folding cartons (FBB grade, 350–400 GSM)**
- **Shrink-wrap collation** of pouches
- **Display-ready corrugated trays**

This tier carries the **FSSAI logo, AGMARK grade, nutrition facts, barcode (EAN-13)**, and **"Best Before"** date.

**Tier III — Tertiary Packaging:** This is the **logistics and distribution layer** engineered to withstand transit stresses defined by **ASTM D4169** and **ISTA 3A** test profiles. Examples include:
- **5-ply BC-flute corrugated shipping cases** (BCT ≥ 700 kgf)
- **ISPM-15 fumigated wooden pallets** (1200 × 1000 mm, heat-treated at 56 °C/30 min)
- **Stretch-wrapped pallet loads** with edge protectors
- **Silica gel desiccant** (200 g per case) to maintain moisture below critical water activity (a_w < 0.6 for turmeric)

International standards: **FDA 21 CFR 100-169**, **CODEX STAN 328-2014** (turmeric), **FSSAI Packaging & Labelling Regulations 2011 (amended 2022)**, **HACCP Codex 2020**, **ISO 22000:2018**, **BRCGS Global Standard for Packaging Materials Issue 6**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Total consignment volume in CBM:**

$$V_{carton} = 0.50 \times 0.40 \times 0.30 = 0.060 \,\text{m}^3$$

$$V_{total} = 600 \times 0.060 = 36.00 \,\text{CBM}$$

**(ii) Total gross weight:**

$$G_{wt} = 600 \times 15 = 9000 \,\text{kg}$$

**(iii) Volumetric weight (air, divisor 5000):**

$$V_{wt,air} = \frac{50 \times 40 \times 30 \times 600}{5000} = \frac{36000000}{5000} = 7200 \,\text{kg}$$

**(iv) Sea freight cost (per CBM, since cargo is weight-dense but quoted per CBM here):**

$$C_{sea} = 36.00 \times 110 = \text{USD } 3960.00$$

**Air freight cost (chargeable weight = max of 9000, 7200 = 9000 kg):**

$$C_{air} = 9000 \times 4.50 = \text{USD } 40500.00$$

**(v) Recommendation:**

$$\text{Saving} = C_{air} - C_{sea} = 40500 - 3960 = \text{USD } 36540.00$$

**Conclusion:** Sea freight is **cheaper by USD 36,540.00** — approximately **91% cost reduction**. For non-perishable spices with 6+ months shelf life, **sea freight is recommended**, with transit time of 18–25 days via Singapore transshipment. Air freight is justifiable only for **urgent replenishment orders** or for **high-value saffron/kesar** consignments.

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks): Labelling & Regulatory Compliance

**Question B:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

(a) Compare and contrast the **labelling requirements under FSSAI (India) and FDA (USA)** for a pre-packaged food product. Include in your answer the mandatory declarations, allergen disclosure rules, and font size specifications. (7 Marks)

(b) Discuss the **significance of AGMARK, FPO, and BIS certifications** for Indian food exports. What happens to a consignment if these certifications are missing or expired? (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | **FSSAI (India)** | **FDA (USA)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Governing Law** | Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006; FSSAI Packaging & Labelling Regulations 2011 (amended 2022) | Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FDCA), 21 CFR Part 101 |
| **Mandatory Mark** | FSSAI logo + 14-digit license number | Nutrition Facts panel; Allergen declaration |
| **Date Marking** | "Best Before" — DD/MM/YY format | "Best By" / "Use By" — MM/DD/YYYY format |
| **Allergen Rule** | 8 major allergens (Reg. 2.3.1) | 9 major allergens per FALCPA 2004 (added sesame in FASTER Act 2023) |
| **Font Size (Principal Display Panel)** | Minimum **1 mm** for declarations on PDP; **2 mm** for nutritional info on packs > 200 cm² PDP | Minimum **1/16 inch (≈ 1.6 mm)** based on PDP area |
| **Nutritional Info** | Mandatory per serving (g) and per 100 g/100 mL | Mandatory per serving in **oz & g** and per container |
| **Veg/Non-Veg Symbol** | Mandatory **green dot** (veg) or **brown triangle** (non-veg) — FSSAI Reg. 4.10 | No such symbol; relies on ingredient declaration |
| **Country of Origin** | Mandatory for imported food | Mandatory under 19 CFR 134 (Country of Origin Marking) |
| **Additive Disclosure** | Class name + specific name (e.g., INS 211 = Sodium Benzoate) | Functional class + specific name (e.g., "Preservative: Sodium Benzoate") |
| **Language** | English or Hindi (or both) | English mandatory |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Allergen emphasis:** FSSAI uses a **declarative list** in ingredients; FDA's FALCPA mandates **plain English disclosure** of 9 allergens ("Contains: Milk, Wheat, Soy") separate from the ingredient list, with emphasis (bold, underline, or contrasting color).
2. **Veg/Non-Veg:** This is uniquely Indian, driven by cultural and religious sensitivity; FDA does not have this mandate.
3. **Nutritional data:** FDA requires **"Daily Value %"** based on 2000-kcal reference; FSSAI uses **RDA %** based on ICMR 2020 guidelines.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. AGMARK (Agricultural Marketing):**
- Governed by **AGMARK Act, 1937**; administered by **Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)**, Ministry of Agriculture.
- Certifies **quality grade** (Special, Good, Fair) for agricultural products — **honey, ghee, butter, vegetable oils, whole spices (turmeric, chilli, coriander, cumin)**.
- For exports, AGMARK is **mandatory** for notified commodities under **Exim Policy** (Foreign Trade Policy 2015-2020, extended). For example, **export of turmeric** requires AGMARK certification verifying **curcumin content ≥ 2%**, **lead content < 10 ppm**, and **moisture < 10%**.

**2. FPO (Fruit Products Order, 1955):**
- Originally governed the manufacture and sale of processed fruit and vegetable products (jams, juices, squashes, pickles, preserves).
- **Subsumed under FSSAI in 2011.** However, certain **Schedule IV standards** of FPO are still referenced for **Brix levels, TSS (Total Soluble Solids), preservatives**, and **minimum fruit content** in beverages.
- For export of fruit pulp to EU/US, compliance with FPO-equivalent FSSAI standards is **mandatory**; FDA 21 CFR 145/146 also applies for canned fruit products.

**3. BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards):**
- Governed by **BIS Act, 2016**; mandatory for products under **Mandatory Certification Scheme** (ISI Mark) — e.g., **infant food, milk powder, packaged drinking water, biscuits**.
- For exports, **ISI Mark** is essential for accessing **tender-based** government procurements in some importing countries.
- BIS also issues **BIS Hallmark** for edible oils and ghee under **IS 13690** and **IS 2785** respectively.

**Consequences of Missing or Expired Certifications:**

| Scenario | Consequence |
| :--- | :--- |
| Expired AGMARK | **Consignment detention** at port; re-test mandatory; customs may reject |
| Missing AGMARK for notified spice | **100% examination** + rejection; re-export at exporter cost |
| Expired FSSAI license | **Shutout from manufacturing**; consignment deemed unsafe; recall issued |
| No BIS for infant food | **Automatic rejection** at port of destination; criminal prosecution under BIS Act |
| Expired Phytosanitary Certificate | **Re-issuance required**; cargo may be ordered destroyed or re-shipped |

> **Legal Recourse:** Under **Customs Act 1962 (Sec 111)**, improper certification makes the consignment **"liable for confiscation."** The exporter may face **penal action** under the **Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act, 1992** including **blacklisting by DGFT** and cancellation of **IEC (Import Export Code)**.

- [ ] **EU FIC 1169/2011** mandates allergen **14 substances in bold**, minimum font **1.2 mm**.
- [ ] **FALCPA 2004** lists **9 US allergens** (added sesame in 2023 FASTER Act).
- [ ] **Volumetric weight (air)** = L × B × H ÷ **5000**; **(sea LCL)** = L × B × H ÷ **6000**.
- [ ] **Chargeable weight** = **max** of gross and volumetric weight.
- [ ] **Sea freight** is charged per **CBM or per 1000 kg** — whichever is greater (W/M rule).
- [ ] **McKee formula**: BCT = **0.0291 × ECT × h^(2/3) × Z^(1/3)** (single-wall); constant changes for double-wall (**0.0272**).
- [ ] **Safety Factor** for export cartons must be **≥ 3.0**.
- [ ] **Standard atmosphere** for BCT test: **23 °C, 50% RH** (TAPPI T 804).
- [ ] **C-flute** is most common for export (height 3.5–4 mm); **BC-flute** for heavy duty.
- [ ] **ISPM-15** governs wood packaging material (56 °C for 30 min) — mandatory for all wooden pallets/crates in international trade.
- [ ] **Phytosanitary Certificate** is issued by **Plant Quarantine**; **Health Certificate** by **FSSAI/MPEDA**.
- [ ] **Halal cert** for Middle East/Indonesia/Malaysia; **Kosher** for Israel/USA.
- [ ] **GHS 9 pictograms** for dangerous goods (e.g., flammable solvents in food flavorings).
- [ ] **Cold chain**: Frozen foods maintained at **−18 °C ± 2 °C**; chilled at **0–4 °C**.
- [ ] **Migration limit** for food contact materials: **10 mg/dm²** or **60 mg/kg** food.
- [ ] **EU 1924/2006** regulates nutritional claims ("low fat," "high fibre"); unauthorized use is illegal.
- [ ] **Country of Origin mark** mandatory for all cross-border consignments per **WTO Rules**.
- [ ] **Reefer container** = refrigerated ISO container; **Flexitank** = 24,000 L bulk liquid bag.
- [ ] **Bursting strength (BS)** in kg/cm²; **Edge Crush Test (ECT)** in kN/m; **BCT** in N or kgf.
- [ ] **McKee's 2/3 exponent on thickness, 1/3 exponent on perimeter** — non-negotiable, memorize the formula, not the derivation.
- [ ] **Container capacities**: 20-ft = 33 CBM; 40-ft HC = 76 CBM; utilization target ≥ 80%.
- [ ] **Export documents** (must remember 8): Commercial Invoice, Packing List, B/L or AWB, Certificate of Origin, Phytosanitary Cert, Health Cert, Insurance Cert, Fumigation Cert.
- [ ] **Customs Act 1962, Sec 111** allows confiscation of non-compliant goods; **DGFT can blacklist** exporters.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Topic: Export and Import Packaging and Labelling Details**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PEFTT636 | NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containerization
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Containerization** is a standardized, intermodal freight transport system that uses large, standardized containers—commonly known as **Intermodal Containers (IMCs), ISO Containers, or Freight Containers**—to transport goods seamlessly across multiple modes of transportation (ships, trucks, rail, and air) without the need for intermediate unpacking, handling, or reloading of the cargo. In the context of **Food Supply Chain Management (FSCM)**, containerization refers to the use of temperature-controlled, hermetically sealed, and standardized containers (especially **Reefer Containers / Refrigerated Containers**) that maintain the cold chain integrity of perishable food commodities from farm (or processing plant) to fork (or retail shelf). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** "Containerization is a system of intermodal freight transport using standard-sized containers, that can be loaded and unloaded, transferred between modes of transport (ship, rail, truck, and air), and stored at terminals without opening their contents." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lego Block of Global Trade" Imagine a child building a massive structure with **LEGO blocks**. Each LEGO brick is a standard size and shape. The child can stack, unstack, and rearrange the blocks endlessly, because every block is **uniform**, **interchangeable**, and **stackable**. Containerization works exactly the same way for the global food trade: - The **container** is the LEGO brick — a uniform steel box. - The **truck, ship, train, and warehouse crane** are the hands that move the brick. - The **food (frozen shrimp, fresh mangoes, dairy, meat)** is the payload inside the brick. > [!NOTE] > Before containerization, dock workers manually unloaded individual sacks, crates, and barrels of food — a process that took **weeks** per ship. Today, a single modern vessel can be unloaded in **less than 24 hours** using standardized containers and automated gantry cranes. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis) The following **ISO 668** standard metrics are critical for KTU 2024 examinations: | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | **Standard Container Width** | **8 feet (2.438 m)** | | **Standard Container Height** | **8 feet 6 inches (2.591 m) — "1C" type** | | **High Cube Height** | **9 feet 6 inches (2.896 m) — "1A" type** | | **Standard Container Lengths** | **20 ft (TEU)** and **40 ft (FEU)** | | **TEU Definition** | **Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit (1 TEU = 1 × 20 ft container)** | | **FEU Definition** | **Forty-foot Equivalent Unit (1 FEU = 2 TEU)** | | **Reefer Set Point Range** | **−30°C to +30°C** | | **Max Gross Weight (20 ft)** | **30,480 kg (67,200 lbs)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** is the industry-standard "currency" of containerization. Whenever KTU asks you to calculate fleet capacity, port throughput, or vessel capacity — always convert container counts into **TEU** first. ## 1.4 Containerization in the Food Supply Chain — Big Picture ``` FARM / PROCESSING PLANT ↓ PACKHOUSE (Pre-cooling + Sorting) ↓ COLD STORE (Reefer Warehouse) ↓ REEFER CONTAINER LOADING AT PORT ↓ INTERMODAL TRANSPORT (Ship + Rail + Truck) ↓ IMPORT TERMINAL CUSTOMS CLEARANCE ↓ DISTRIBUTION CENTER ↓ RETAIL SHELF / CONSUMER ``` > [!NOTE] > Containerization is the **physical backbone** of modern cold chain logistics. It is the vessel that carries food from one end of the supply chain to the other while maintaining temperature, hygiene, and traceability. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Containerization Containerization as a system rests on **five inter-locking engineering and logistical principles**. KTU 2024 examiners frequently test these: ### Pillar 1 — Standardization (ISO 668 & ISO 1496) - Every container must conform to **ISO 668** (dimensions/ratings) and **ISO 1496** (specifications). - This guarantees that a container loaded in **Kochi (India)** can be transferred seamlessly to a truck in **Rotterdam (Netherlands)** without ever being opened. ### Pillar 2 — Intermodalism - A single container can move on: - **Ocean vessel** (Cellular container ship) - **Rail wagon** (Container flatcar) - **Road truck** (Semi-trailer chassis) - **Inland barge** (Container barge) - The cargo is **never unpacked** at any transfer point. ### Pillar 3 — Mechanization - **Ship-to-shore gantry cranes (STS)** unload vessels. - **Rubber-tyred gantry cranes (RTG)** and **reach stackers** move containers in yards. - This replaces the historical gangs of 50+ longshoremen per ship hatch. ### Pillar 4 — Concentration & Hub-and-Spoke - Food containers are **concentrated at major hub ports** (e.g., Cochin Port, Singapore, Jebel Ali). - From hubs, smaller feeder vessels or rail lines distribute to inland destinations. ### Pillar 5 — Documentation & Digitalization - Every container has a unique **BIC Code (Container Identification Number)** — e.g., `MSCU 123456-7`. - The **Bill of Lading (B/L)**, **Container Freight Station (CFS)** receipt, and **Customs declarations** are now digital. - **IoT-enabled reefer containers** transmit real-time temperature, GPS location, and humidity data. ## 2.2 Types of Containers Used in Food Supply Chain | Container Type | Application in Food SCM | Temperature Range | |---|---|---| | **Dry Van / General Purpose** | Shelf-stable foods (rice, wheat, sugar, packaged snacks) | Ambient | | **Reefer (Refrigerated)** | Fresh produce, dairy, meat, seafood, frozen foods | **−30°C to +30°C** | | **Insulated (Non-Mechanical)** | Short-haul transport of mildly perishable goods | Maintains ±5°C of loading temp | | **Open Top** | Oversized machinery for food processing plants | Ambient | | **Flat Rack** | Heavy food processing equipment | Ambient | | **Tank Container (ISO Tank)** | Liquid food: edible oils, fruit juices, milk, wine, beer | Often insulated + heated | | **Ventilated** | Coffee, cocoa beans, onions, garlic (need airflow) | Ambient but ventilated | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Tip:** The single most important container type for the FSCM paper is the **Reefer Container**. Memorize its components, working principle, and set point management. ## 2.3 Anatomy of a Reefer Container (Engineering Detail) A modern reefer container is a **self-contained refrigerated unit**. Its critical subsystems are: 1. **Outer Shell** — Corrugated steel (CORTEN) for marine durability. 2. **Inner Lining** — Stainless steel or GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) — food-grade, easy to sanitize. 3. **Insulation Layer** — **Polyurethane foam (PUF)**, ~50–100 mm thick, K-value ≤ 0.4 W/m·K. 4. **Refrigeration Unit** — Hermetic compressor using **HFC refrigerant R-134a** (or R-404A in older units). 5. **Air Circulation System** — Bottom T-rail floor forces cold air through the cargo and returns it via the top. 6. **Control Panel** — Digital controller (e.g., Carrier DataCorder, Thermo King CASC) for set point, defrost, and logging. 7. **Fresh Air Exchange (FAE) Vent** — Allows respiration gases (CO₂, ethylene) to vent for fresh produce. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why bottom T-rail floor?** Cold air is denser than warm air, so it is forced UPWARD through the cargo.
