topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Export and Import Procedure and Documentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: EXPORT AND IMPORT PROCEDURE AND DOCUMENTATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Export Procedure** refers to the systematic, legally compliant sequence of activities undertaken by an exporter to dispatch goods (in this case, food products) from the domestic (Indian) market to a foreign buyer across international borders, in conformity with the **Foreign Trade Policy (FTP)** of **DGFT**, the **Customs Act, 1962**, **FSSAI** regulations (for food items), and the **International Commercial Terms (Incoterms® 2020)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Import Procedure** refers to the structured, regulatory-compliant sequence of operations performed by an importer to bring goods from a foreign supplier into the domestic (Indian) territory, involving compliance with the **Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA, 1999)**, the **Customs Tariff Act, 1975**, the **Indian Foreign Trade Policy**, and applicable food safety/sanitary and phytosanitary (SPS) norms of the destination country.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Documentation** in international food trade constitutes the **legal, commercial, transport, regulatory, insurance, and financial paper trail** that authenticates ownership, ensures regulatory compliance, facilitates cargo movement, enables payment settlement, and protects both exporter and importer against commercial, operational, and statutory risks.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🌍

Imagine you are a **Kerala-based spices exporter** sending 20 metric tonnes of premium **black pepper** to a buyer in **Germany**.

| Real-World Analogy | Export-Import Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Packing your suitcase before a foreign trip | Pre-shipment packaging, marking, and labeling |
| Showing your passport at immigration | Customs clearance & Bill of Export |
| The airline ticket you carry | Bill of Lading / Airway Bill |
| The travel insurance paper | Insurance Certificate / Policy |
| Money exchanged at airport forex | Foreign Exchange remittance via AD Bank |
| Visa application with Embassy | IEC, RCMC, FSSAI Certificate, Phytosanitary Certificate |
| Boarding pass stamping | Let Export Order (Customs stamp) |

> [!TIP]
> Think of export-import documentation as a **"passport system for cargo"** — every document is a "visa stamp" that gives the goods legal permission to move, stay, or be released in a foreign country.

---

### 1.3 Key Regulatory & Statutory Bodies (Bolded Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following authorities are **statutory pillars** of Indian food trade:

- **DGFT** — Director General of Foreign Trade (Ministry of Commerce)
- **CBIC** — Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs
- **FSSAI** — Food Safety and Standards Authority of India
- **EIC** — Export Inspection Council of India
- **APEDA** — Agricultural & Processed Food Products Export Development Authority
- **MPEDA** — Marine Products Export Development Authority
- **EIC** / **PPQS** — Plant Protection & Quarantine Services
- **NABL** — National Accreditation Board for Testing and Calibration Laboratories
- **ECGC** — Export Credit Guarantee Corporation
- **AD Bank** — Authorized Dealer Bank (RBI-empanelled)
- **CHA / CUSTOMS BROKER** — Custom House Agent licensed under Section 141 of Customs Act, 1962
- **CFS / ICD** — Container Freight Station / Inland Container Depot
- **EPC** — Export Promotion Council (sector-specific)

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in International Food Trade

| Metric | Standard Value / Reference |
|---|---|
| Standard **FOB** value declaration | Mandatory in shipping bill |
| **IEC (Import Export Code)** | 10-digit, lifetime validity, issued by DGFT |
| **RCMC** (Registration Cum Membership Certificate) | 5-year validity (with EPC) |
| **Free on Board (FOB)** vs **Cost, Insurance & Freight (CIF)** | Incoterms® 2020 definitions |
| **Letter of Credit (L/C)** validity | Typically 30/60/90/120 days from shipment |
| **Bill of Lading** — original sets | Typically issued in **3 originals + 3 non-negotiable copies** |
| **Cargo Insurance** | Min **110% of CIF value** (UCP 600 standard) |
| **HSN Code** | 8-digit Harmonized System of Nomenclature (India) |
| **Phytosanitary Certificate** | Issued by PPQS under IPPC |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The EXPORT Procedure — 10-Stage Logical Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Module-4 learning outcome, students must be able to **"describe the sequential steps and documentation involved in export and import of food products."**

### Stage 1: Market Research & Buyer Identification
- Identify target market (demand for spice, seafood, organic, processed foods).
- Study **WTO-SPS** requirements of destination country.
- Study **EU RASFF**, **US FDA FSVP**, **FSSAI** dual compliance for food items.

### Stage 2: Registration & Statutory Approvals
- Obtain **IEC** (10-digit) from DGFT.
- Register with relevant **EPC** (e.g., APEDA for agri-products, MPEDA for seafood).
- Obtain **RCMC** (Registration Cum Membership Certificate).
- **FSSAI License** (Central or State) for food safety.
- **MSME / Udyam Registration** (optional, for benefits).

### Stage 3: Product Compliance & Pre-Shipment Quality
- **Lab testing** of food product from **NABL-accredited lab**.
- Obtain **Health Certificate**, **Phytosanitary Certificate (PC)**, **Certificate of Origin (CoO)**.
- **EIC** inspection (for items in mandatory EIC list — fish, meat, milk products).
- **APEDA** registration for scheduled products (fruits, vegetables, processed foods, cereals, etc.).

### Stage 4: Price Quotation & Order Confirmation
- Quote in **Incoterms® 2020** (e.g., **FOB Mumbai**, **CIF Hamburg**).
- Receive **Purchase Order (PO)** from foreign buyer.
- Sign **Export Contract** (with HS code, quality specs, payment terms, shipment date, packing, port of loading/discharge).

### Stage 5: Finance & Payment Mechanism Setup
- Open **Letter of Credit (L/C)** or agree on **Advance Payment / DA / DP terms**.
- Apply for **Export Finance / Packing Credit** from AD Bank.
- **ECGC** policy for credit risk coverage.

### Stage 6: Procurement & Manufacturing
- Procure raw material.
- Manufacture, process, pack as per **destination-country labelling** (e.g., EU allergen declaration, FDA nutritional facts).
- Pre-shipment inspection.

### Stage 7: Packaging, Marking & Labelling
- **ISPM-15** compliance for wooden packaging (heat treatment / fumigation mark).
- Marking: **Shipper's mark, Consignee mark, Port of discharge, Package number, Net/Gross weight, Country of Origin "PRODUCT OF INDIA"**.
- Special food labelling: **Best Before, Batch/Lot No., FSSAI Logo, Ingredients, Veg/Non-Veg symbol, Nutritional info**.

### Stage 8: Pre-Shipment Customs Procedures
- File **Shipping Bill** on **ICEGATE** (Electronic customs portal).
- Classification under **ITC-HS Code (8-digit)**.
- Submit documents to **CHA** (Customs House Agent).
- **Let Export Order (LEO)** is issued by Customs after examination.

### Stage 9: Cargo Handling & Shipment
- Goods moved to **CFS / Port / Airport**.
- **Container stuffing** (FCL) or **LCL consolidation**.
- **Mate's Receipt** issued by carrier.
- Mate's Receipt exchanged for **Bill of Lading (B/L) / Airway Bill (AWB)**.

### Stage 10: Post-Shipment Documentation & Realization
- Send **shipping documents** to importer / bank for negotiation.
- File **EGM (Export General Manifest)** by carrier.
- Claim **Drawback / RoDTEP / RoSCTL** benefits.
- Receive foreign exchange, file **Foreign Exchange Realization (e-FIRC / BRC)**.
- File **GST refund** for zero-rated supplies.

---

## 2.2 The IMPORT Procedure — 8-Stage Logical Flow

### Stage 1: IEC & AD Code Registration
- **IEC (Import Export Code)** is the primary prerequisite — issued by DGFT.
- Register with **AD Bank** (Authorized Dealer) for foreign exchange.

### Stage 2: Sourcing & Contract Finalization
- Identify foreign supplier (exhibitions, B2B portals, embassies).
- Sign **Import Contract** with **Incoterms® 2020** (e.g., **FOB, CIF, CFR, EXW**).
- Open **Letter of Credit (L/C)** through AD Bank in favour of foreign supplier.
- Apply for **Import License** (only for restricted/prohibited items under ITC-HS).

### Stage 3: Foreign Exchange & Remittance
- AD Bank sanctions **foreign exchange** under **FEMA, 1999**.
- Remit payment through banking channels.

### Stage 4: Cargo Arrival & Customs Filing
- File **Bill of Entry (BOE)** on **ICEGATE** before cargo arrival.
- Submit **Import Manifest** (filed by carrier).
- Pay applicable **BCD, IGST, Social Welfare Surcharge, Custom Duty** as per **Customs Tariff Act, 1975**.

### Stage 5: Customs Examination & Assessment
- **First Check (FC)** or **Second Appraisement** by Customs Officer.
- **Sampling** if needed — sent to NABL lab.
- Risk Management System (RMS) based assessment.

### Stage 6: Duty Payment & Customs Clearance
- Pay **Customs Duty + IGST** through ICEGATE e-payment.
- **Out of Charge (OOC)** order issued by Customs.

### Stage 7: Food Safety Clearance (Special for Food Imports)
- **FSSAI** clearance under **FSS (Import) Regulations, 2017**.
- **Visual inspection, labeling compliance, lab analysis**.
- **NOC** by FSSAI authorized officer at port.
- **Referral lab testing** for non-compliant samples.

### Stage 8: Post-Clearance Compliance
- **Warehousing / Distribution**.
- Maintain **import records** for **5 years** (FSSAI requirement).
- File **Bill of Entry (Final)** and reconcile with banks (FIRC).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master this table — these are the most testable document names in KTU exams.**

| # | Document | Purpose | Issuing Authority | Stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IEC** | Authorisation to do foreign trade | DGFT | Pre-trade |
| 2 | **RCMC** | EPC membership (benefits + DBK) | EPC (APEDA/MPEDA) | Pre-trade |
| 3 | **FSSAI License** | Food safety compliance | FSSAI | Pre-shipment |
| 4 | **Purchase Order / Contract** | Legal basis of trade | Buyer & Seller | Pre-trade |
| 5 | **Letter of Credit (L/C)** | Bank-guaranteed payment | Issuing Bank | Pre-shipment |
| 6 | **Proforma Invoice** | Quotation by exporter | Exporter | Pre-shipment |
| 7 | **Commercial Invoice** | Final sale invoice | Exporter | Shipping |
| 8 | **Packing List** | Item-wise weight & packing details | Exporter | Shipping |
| 9 | **Shipping Bill** | Customs export declaration | Exporter/CHA | Customs |
| 10 | **Bill of Lading (B/L)** | Title document, receipt of cargo | Shipping Line | Shipping |
| 11 | **Airway Bill (AWB)** | Title document for air cargo | Airline | Shipping |
| 12 | **Certificate of Origin (CoO)** | Confirms country of manufacture | Chamber of Commerce / EPC | Pre-shipment |
| 13 | **Phytosanitary Certificate** | Plant health for agricultural goods | PPQS / IPPC | Pre-shipment |
| 14 | **Health Certificate** | Food safety attestation | EIC / FSSAI | Pre-shipment |
| 15 | **Insurance Certificate** | Covers transit risk | Insurance Co. | Pre-shipment |
| 16 | **Bill of Entry (BOE)** | Customs import declaration | Importer/CHA | Customs |
| 17 | **Let Export Order (LEO)** | Customs permission to export | Customs | Customs |
| 18 | **EGM** | Export General Manifest | Carrier | Post-shipment |
| 19 | **FIRC / BRC** | Foreign exchange realization proof | AD Bank | Post-shipment |
| 20 | **ARE-1** | Removal of goods for export (excise) | Central Excise | Pre-shipment |

### 2.4 Incoterms® 2020 — The 11 Terms (Examiner Favourite!)

| Group | Term | Risk Transfer Point | Cost Burden on Seller |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E** | EXW (Ex Works) | Buyer's premises | Minimum |
| **F** | FCA (Free Carrier) | At named place | Low |
| **F** | FAS (Free Alongside Ship) | Alongside vessel at port | Low |
| **F** | FOB (Free On Board) | On board vessel at port of shipment | Medium |
| **C** | CFR (Cost & Freight) | On board (freight paid) | Medium |
| **C** | CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight) | On board (freight + insurance paid) | Medium-High |
| **C** | CPT (Carriage Paid To) | At named destination (carriage paid) | Medium |
| **C** | CIP (Carriage & Insurance Paid) | At named destination (carriage + insurance paid) | Medium-High |
| **D** | DAP (Delivered At Place) | At named place of destination | High |
| **D** | DPU (Delivered at Place Unloaded) | Unloaded at named place | High |
| **D** | DDP (Delivered Duty Paid) | At named place, duty paid | Maximum |

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU 2024, focus on:** FOB, CIF, CFR, EXW, DDP — these are most frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

For Food Technology / Food Engineering students, mastering export-import procedure is critical because:
- **Cold chain integrity** (reefer containers, $-21°C$ for frozen seafood) must be declared correctly in B/L.
- **Shelf-life** calculations (e.g., $T_{remaining} = T_{total} - T_{transit} - T_{inspection}$) directly affect B/L dating.
- **Moisture content, water activity ($a_w$), and HACCP** documentation are now mandatory for EU/US imports.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Documentary Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Linking Documents to Trade Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides an exhaustive **logical mapping** of documents to the trade lifecycle, as required for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

### 3.1.1 Letter of Credit (L/C) Mathematical Settlement Model

Consider a Kerala exporter shipping **Cardamom** to Saudi Arabia under L/C.

Let:
- $C$ = CIF value of cargo in USD
- $F$ = Freight cost in USD
- $I$ = Insurance premium in USD
- $E$ = Exchange rate (INR per USD)
- $D_t$ = Customs duty rate applicable (BCD + IGST)
- $M$ = Margin money blocked with bank (\% of L/C value)

The **CIF value breakdown** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{FOB} &= C - F - I \\
\text{Insurance (UCP 600 minimum)} &= 1.10 \times C_{FOB} \times 0.005 \;\;\text{(typical 0.5\% rate)} \\
\text{Margin blocked with AD Bank} &= M \times C \times E \\
\text{Realized INR revenue} &= C \times E \times (1 - M) \\
\text{Eligible Drawback (approx.)} &= C \times E \times R_{DBK} \;\;\text{where } R_{DBK} \in [0.3\%, 2\%] \\
\text{Net effective realization} &= C \times E \times (1 - M) + C \times E \times R_{DBK} + C \times E \times R_{RoDTEP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Explanation of each step (text-row logic):**
> - **Step 1:** FOB value is derived by subtracting freight and insurance from CIF.
> - **Step 2:** UCP 600 mandates minimum **110%** of CIF value under CIF terms; here we use 0.5% as the typical marine cargo rate.
> - **Step 3:** AD Bank blocks margin money (typically 10–25% of L/C value) as security.
> - **Step 4:** Net realization to exporter = full CIF value minus blocked margin.
> - **Step 5:** Drawback + RoDTEP = government incentives that effectively **boost exporter realization by 2–5%**.

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A food exporter from Kerala ships **basmati rice** to a buyer in Dubai.  
> CIF value = USD 50,000 | Freight = USD 3,000 | Insurance = USD 500 | Exchange rate = ₹83/USD | Margin = 10% | DBK rate = 1% | RoDTEP = 1.5%  
> **Calculate the net effective realization in INR.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{FOB} &= C - F - I = 50000 - 3000 - 500 = \text{USD } 46{,}500 \\
\text{Margin blocked (INR)} &= 0.10 \times 50000 \times 83 = \text{₹ } 4{,}15{,}000 \\
\text{Net CIF in INR} &= 50000 \times 83 = \text{₹ } 41{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Realized revenue (INR)} &= 50000 \times 83 \times (1 - 0.10) = \text{₹ } 37{,}35{,}000 \\
\text{DBK benefit} &= 50000 \times 83 \times 0.01 = \text{₹ } 41{,}500 \\
\text{RoDTEP benefit} &= 50000 \times 83 \times 0.015 = \text{₹ } 62{,}250 \\
\text{Total Effective Realization} &= 37{,}35{,}000 + 41{,}500 + 62{,}250 = \text{₹ } 38{,}38{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's verification step:** The effective realization is **$38{,}38{,}750 \div 50000 = \text{₹ } 76.775/\text{USD}$** — which is **less than the spot rate of ₹83/USD** because the bank blocked 10% margin that is released only after export realization confirmation.

### 3.1.3 Cost-Build-Up Analytical Matrix (Importer Side)

For an **importer** receiving frozen shrimp from Vietnam under **CIF Cochin**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Landed Cost (LC)} &= \text{CIF Value (INR)} + \text{BCD} + \text{IGST} + \text{SWS} + \text{ACD} \\
\text{BCD (Basic Customs Duty)} &= CIF \times d_{BCD} \;\;\text{(e.g., 30\% for shrimp)} \\
\text{IGST} &= (CIF + BCD) \times 18\% \;\;\text{(typical for food items)} \\
\text{Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS)} &= BCD \times 10\% \\
\text{Anti-dumping / Safeguard} &= CIF \times d_{AD} \;\;\text{(if applicable)} \\
\text{FSSAI Clearance Cost} &= \text{Fixed} + \text{Lab Test Fee} \\
\text{Clearing, Handling, CHA} &= \text{Fixed or \% of CIF}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Logic text:** Each customs levy is **stacked additively**, but IGST is calculated on a **compounded base** (CIF + BCD), which makes imports of high-duty items disproportionately expensive. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark analytical question pattern**.

### 3.1.4 Documentary Flow — Mapped Timeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Document = "Time-stamped proof"** — the following mapping shows which document is produced at which trade milestone.

| Trade Milestone | Document Generated | Custodian |
|---|---|---|
| Buyer-Seller negotiation | Proforma Invoice | Exporter |
| Contract signing | Export Contract | Both parties |
| Bank guarantee | Letter of Credit | Issuing Bank → Advising Bank → Beneficiary |
| Pre-shipment finance | Packing Credit Loan Sanction | AD Bank |
| Manufacturing complete | Pre-Shipment Inspection Certificate | Inspection agency (e.g., SGS) |
| Cargo ready for customs | **Shipping Bill** filed on ICEGATE | CHA |
| Examination by Customs | Examination Report + **LEO** | Customs Officer |
| Cargo loaded on vessel | Mate's Receipt → **Bill of Lading** | Shipping Line |
| Cargo in transit | Insurance Certificate, CoO, PC | Insurer, Chamber, PPQS |
| Cargo arrives at destination | **Bill of Entry** filed by importer | CHA at destination |
| Payment settlement | **Foreign Exchange Remittance** | AD Bank (both ends) |
| Export incentives claimed | **DBK / RoDTEP** application | DGFT / Customs |
| GST zero-rating | **LUT / Refund application** | GST portal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Export Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Market Research & Buyer Identification] --> B[Obtain IEC from DGFT]
    B --> C[Register with APEDA MPEDA or other EPC and obtain RCMC]
    C --> D[Obtain FSSAI License and EIC Approval if applicable]
    D --> E[Receive Purchase Order and sign Export Contract with Incoterms 2020]
    E --> F[Open Letter of Credit or finalize payment terms with AD Bank]
    F --> G[Procurement Manufacturing and Quality Lab Testing]
    G --> H[ISPM 15 Packaging Marking and Destination Labelling]
    H --> I[File Shipping Bill on ICEGATE and submit to CHA]
    I --> J{Customs Examination}
    J -- Conforms --> K[Issue of Let Export Order LEO]
    J -- Non Conformance --> L[Rejection or Re inspection]
    L --> J
    K --> M[Cargo Movement to Port CFS or Airport]
    M --> N[Container Stuffing and Mate Receipt]
    N --> O[Issuance of Bill of Lading or Airway Bill]
    O --> P[Goods in Transit with Insurance and Certificates]
    P --> Q[Document Negotiation via Bank and Payment Realization]
    Q --> R[Claim DBK RoDTEP and GST Refund]
    R --> S[End: Export Process Complete]
```

## 4.2 End-to-End Import Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: IEC and AD Code Registration] --> B1[Identify Foreign Supplier and Sign Import Contract]
    B1 --> C1[Open Letter of Credit with AD Bank]
    C1 --> D1[Foreign Exchange Remittance under FEMA 1999]
    D1 --> E1[Shipment by Exporter and Receipt of Documents]
    E1 --> F1[Cargo Arrival at Indian Port]
    F1 --> G1[File Bill of Entry on ICEGATE before cargo arrival]
    G1 --> H1{Customs Examination and Assessment}
    H1 -- RMS Green --> I1[Out of Charge Order]
    H1 -- RMS Yellow or Red --> J1[Physical Examination and Sampling]
    J1 --> K1{NABL Lab Test Result}
    K1 -- Pass --> I1
    K1 -- Fail --> L1[Re export or Destruction Order]
    I1 --> M1[FSSAI Clearance under FSS Import Regulations 2017]
    M1 --> N1[Payment of BCD IGST SWS and Other Duties]
    N1 --> O1[Out of Charge and Delivery from Port]
    O1 --> P1[Warehousing Distribution and Record Keeping for 5 Years]
    P1 --> Q1[End: Import Process Complete]
```

## 4.3 Document Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PreTrade[Pre Trade Documents]
        IEC1[IEC Code]
        RCMC1[RCMC]
        FSSAI1[FSSAI License]
    end

    subgraph Trade[Trade Documents]
        PO1[Purchase Order]
        LC1[Letter of Credit]
        CI1[Commercial Invoice]
        PL1[Packing List]
    end

    subgraph Customs[Customs Documents]
        SB1[Shipping Bill]
        BOE1[Bill of Entry]
        LEO1[LEO]
        OOC1[Out of Charge]
    end

    subgraph Transport[Transport Documents]
        BL1[Bill of Lading]
        AWB1[Airway Bill]
        INS1[Insurance Certificate]
    end

    subgraph Statutory[Statutory Documents]
        COO1[Certificate of Origin]
        PHY1[Phytosanitary Certificate]
        HC1[Health Certificate]
    end

    subgraph PostShip[Post Shipment]
        EGM1[Export General Manifest]
        FIRC1[FIRC and BRC]
        DBK1[Drawback and RoDTEP]
    end

    PreTrade --> Trade
    Trade --> Customs
    Trade --> Transport
    Trade --> Statutory
    Customs --> PostShip
    Transport --> PostShip
    Statutory --> PostShip
```

## 4.4 Payment Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PM[International Payment Mechanism Selection] --> ADV[Advance Payment T T]
    PM --> LC[Letter of Credit L C]
    PM --> DA[Documents against Acceptance D A]
    PM --> DP[Documents against Payment D P]
    PM --> OA[Open Account O A]
    PM --> CONS[Consignment Basis]
    LC --> BANK1[Issuing Bank in Importer Country]
    BANK1 --> BANK2[Advising Bank in Exporter Country]
    BANK2 --> EXP[Exporter Ships Goods and Submits Documents]
    EXP --> BANK3[Negotiating Bank Checks Documents]
    BANK3 --> BANK4[Reimbursing Bank Settles Funds]
    BANK4 --> EXP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** Payment mechanism selection is governed by **trust, trade volume, country risk, and credit history**. L/C is the most secure, while Open Account is the least secure but most buyer-friendly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**List any six documents required for the export of food products from India. State the issuing authority for each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks)

| # | Document | Issuing Authority |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **IEC (Import Export Code)** | DGFT |
| 2 | **RCMC (Registration Cum Membership Certificate)** | Respective EPC (e.g., APEDA) |
| 3 | **FSSAI License / Certificate** | FSSAI |
| 4 | **Commercial Invoice & Packing List** | Exporter |
| 5 | **Bill of Lading / Airway Bill** | Carrier (Shipping Line / Airline) |
| 6 | **Certificate of Origin** | Chamber of Commerce / EPC |
| 7 | **Phytosanitary Certificate** | Plant Protection & Quarantine Services (PPQS) |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation tip:** Listing 6 documents with correct issuing authority = **3 full marks**. Pairing wrong authority with a document = **−0.5 mark** per pair (as per KTU marking scheme).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]`
**What is a Letter of Credit (L/C)? Mention any two advantages of L/C for an Indian food exporter.**

#### ✅ Model Answer

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** A **Letter of Credit (L/C)** is a **conditional, bank-guaranteed payment undertaking** issued by the importer's bank (Issuing Bank) in favour of the exporter (Beneficiary), guaranteeing payment upon presentation of **complying shipping documents** as per **UCP 600** (Uniform Customs & Practice for Documentary Credits, ICC Publication No. 600).

**Two advantages (1 mark = 0.5 each):**
1. **Payment security** — Exporter is assured of payment by the bank, not the foreign buyer (reduces commercial risk).
2. **Financing facility** — Exporter can obtain **pre-shipment packing credit** and **post-shipment negotiation** from AD Bank using L/C as collateral.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU Model Question – Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step export procedure** for a food product (e.g., basmati rice) from India with reference to the **statutory authorities and documents** involved at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of FSSAI, APEDA, EIC, and PPQS** in ensuring compliance of food exports from India. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Pre-Trade Registrations (1 mark)**
- Obtain **IEC (10-digit)** from DGFT.
- Register with **APEDA** (since basmati rice is an APEDA-scheduled product) → obtain **RCMC**.
- Obtain **FSSAI License** (Central, since export-grade processed food).

**Step 2: Buyer & Contract (1 mark)**
- Identify foreign buyer (e.g., Saudi Arabia importer).
- Sign **Export Contract** with **Incoterms® 2020** (typically **FOB Mundra** or **CIF Jeddah**).
- Open **Letter of Credit** with AD Bank.

**Step 3: Pre-Shipment Quality (1 mark)**
- Quality testing from **NABL-accredited lab** (pesticide residue, moisture, foreign matter).
- **Phytosanitary Certificate** from **PPQS** (no live pests, ISPM-15 compliance for pallets).
- **Certificate of Origin** from APEDA / Chamber of Commerce.

**Step 4: Packing, Marking & Labelling (1 mark)**
- Food-grade packaging, vacuum or nitrogen flush.
- Marking: "PRODUCT OF INDIA", Lot No., Net/Gross weight, "Best Before" date, FSSAI logo, Veg/Non-Veg symbol, allergen declaration (mandatory for EU).

**Step 5: Customs Clearance (1 mark)**
- File **Shipping Bill** on **ICEGATE** with correct **ITC-HS Code** (e.g., **1006.30** for Basmati rice).
- **CHA** submits documents → Customs examination → **LEO** issued.

**Step 6: Shipment (1 mark)**
- Container stuffing at **CFS** → **Mate's Receipt** → **Bill of Lading** issued by shipping line.
- **Insurance Certificate** (110% of CIF as per UCP 600).

**Step 7: Post-Shipment (1 mark)**
- Submit documents to negotiating bank for **L/C negotiation**.
- Receive foreign exchange → **FIRC/BRC** from AD Bank.
- Claim **RoDTEP, DBK, and GST refund** for zero-rated supply.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Authority | Role in Food Export Compliance |
|---|---|
| **FSSAI** (1.75 marks) | Issues **FSSAI License** for manufacturing, processing, and exporting food. Mandates **FSS (Licensing & Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011** compliance. Verifies **labelling, shelf-life, additives, contaminants (FSSAI manual on food additives)**. |
| **APEDA** (1.75 marks) | **Export Promotion Council** for scheduled products (fruits, vegetables, processed foods, cereals, basmati, organic). Provides **Market Development Assistance, RCMC, financial assistance for infrastructure, quality certification, transport assistance**. |
| **EIC** (Export Inspection Council) (1.75 marks) | Mandates **Pre-Shipment Inspection (PSI)** for products in the **mandatory EIC list** (fish, meat, egg products, milk, honey). Issues **Health Certificates** accepted by importing countries. |
| **PPQS** (Plant Protection & Quarantine Services) (1.75 marks) | Issues **Phytosanitary Certificate** under **International Plant Protection Convention (IPPC)**. Enforces **quarantine regulations** to prevent pest entry. Issues **ISPM-15** treatment certification for wood packaging. |

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Confusing APEDA and MPEDA:** APEDA = Agricultural & Processed Food. **MPEDA = Marine Products**. Rice falls under APEDA, **NOT MPEDA**.
> - **Writing FSSAI as "Food Safety Act":** The correct statutory body is the **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India**, established under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** (FSS Act, 2006).
> - **Skipping statutory backing:** Always mention the **Act/Regulation year** (e.g., FSSAI under FSS Act 2006, Customs under Customs Act 1962, DGFT under Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992).
> - **Omitting "Let Export Order"** in customs procedure: This is the **single most important customs approval** and examiners specifically look for it.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU Choice Alternative – Module 4]`

**(a)** Describe the **import procedure for a food product** entering India, with emphasis on **Customs, FSSAI clearance, and duty calculation**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Incoterms® 2020** with a focus on **FOB, CIF, and DDP**, and state the responsibility matrix for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: IEC & AD Code (1 mark)**
- The **IEC (Import Export Code)** is the **primary prerequisite** for any importer in India, issued by **DGFT** under the **Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992**.
- Register with an **Authorized Dealer (AD) Bank** for foreign exchange remittance.

**Step 2: Contract & L/C (1 mark)**
- Sign **Import Contract** with foreign supplier under **Incoterms® 2020**.
- Open **Letter of Credit** in favour of foreign supplier.

**Step 3: Cargo Arrival & Bill of Entry (1.5 marks)**
- File **Bill of Entry (BOE)** electronically on **ICEGATE** before cargo arrival at the Indian port.
- **Import Manifest** is filed by the carrier.
- **Risk Management System (RMS)** — based on importer's compliance history.

**Step 4: Customs Examination & Assessment (1.5 marks)**
- Customs assesses **BCD (Basic Customs Duty)**, **IGST**, **Social Welfare Surcharge (10% of BCD)**, and any **Anti-dumping / Safeguard Duty**.
- For **food products**: **FSSAI** clearance under **FSS (Import) Regulations, 2017** is mandatory.
- Visual inspection, label compliance, sampling for lab analysis.

**Step 5: Duty Payment & Out of Charge (1 mark)**
- Pay all duties via **ICEGATE e-payment**.
- Receive **Out of Charge (OOC) Order** from Customs → cargo can be removed from port.
- Maintain **import records for 5 years** as per FSSAI.

**Step 6: Special Food-Import Compliance (1 mark)**
- **NABL lab testing** for non-conforming samples.
- **Recalls / Re-export / Destruction** for failed samples.
- **Labeling compliance** as per FSSAI labelling regulations (2011 / 2020 amendments).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incoterms® 2020** are **11 internationally recognized trade terms** published by the **International Chamber of Commerce (ICC)**. They define the **transfer of risk, cost, and responsibility** between buyer and seller.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Element | **FOB** (Free On Board) | **CIF** (Cost, Insurance, Freight) | **DDP** (Delivered Duty Paid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Transfer** | When goods are **on board the vessel** at port of shipment | Same as FOB (on board), but insurance also paid by seller | When goods delivered to **named destination, cleared for import** |
| **Cost Bearer (Freight)** | Buyer | **Seller** | Seller |
| **Insurance** | Buyer (optional) | **Seller (min 110% of CIF)** | Seller |
| **Customs Export Clearance** | Seller | Seller | Seller |
| **Customs Import Clearance & Duty** | Buyer | Buyer | **Seller** |
| **Unloading at Destination** | Buyer | Buyer | Buyer (but DPU variant includes seller) |
| **Best Suited For** | Sea freight, experienced buyers | Sea freight, first-time exporters selling CIF | Door-to-door, when seller knows import rules |

**Key Rule (2 marks):**
- **FOB** = Seller's risk ends **once goods cross ship's rail**.
- **CIF** = Seller pays **cost, insurance, and freight** but risk still transfers **at port of loading** (not at destination).
- **DDP** = Seller bears **maximum obligation**, including import duties — used when seller has local presence at destination.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B:**
> - **Confusing "risk transfer" with "cost transfer":** In CIF, cost transfers at destination, but **risk transfers at port of loading**. Many students write both at destination — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU exams.
> - **Omitting "UCP 600 110% insurance"** rule under CIF: Examiners specifically check for the **minimum 110% of CIF value** rule.
> - **Confusing DDP with DAP:** DDP = seller pays **all duties**; DAP = buyer pays duties. This is frequently tested.
> - **Not naming the ICC** (International Chamber of Commerce) as the publisher of Incoterms — examiners award 0.5 mark for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 KTU 2024 — Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **IEC (10-digit)** issued by **DGFT** is the **first and foremost** requirement for both export and import in India — valid **lifetime**, no renewal.
- ✅ **RCMC** is obtained from the **sector-specific EPC** (APEDA, MPEDA, FIEO, TEXPROCIL, etc.) — validity **5 years**, mandatory for **Drawback** and **RoDTEP** benefits.
- ✅ **FSSAI License** is mandatory for all food businesses — **Central FSSAI** for turnover > ₹20 crore or export-grade; **State FSSAI** otherwise.
- ✅ **APEDA** = Agricultural & Processed Food Products (rice, spices, fruits, vegetables, processed food). **MPEDA** = Marine (shrimp, fish, crustaceans).
- ✅ **EIC** does **pre-shipment inspection** for **fish, meat, egg, milk, honey** (mandatory list).
- ✅ **Phytosanitary Certificate** is issued by **PPQS** under **IPPC** — required for **all plant-origin food products**.
- ✅ **Bill of Lading (B/L)** is the **document of title** for sea cargo — 3 originals + 3 copies is the global standard.
- ✅ **Airway Bill (AWB)** is for **air cargo** — **non-negotiable**, unlike B/L.
- ✅ **Insurance Certificate** = minimum **110% of CIF value** (UCP 600 standard).
- ✅ **Shipping Bill** is filed on **ICEGATE** for exports; **Bill of Entry** is filed for imports.
- ✅ **LEO (Let Export Order)** is the customs permission to export — without it, cargo cannot be loaded on vessel.
- ✅ **FSSAI (Import) Regulations, 2017** govern food import clearance — **NOC from FSSAI** is required at the port.
- ✅ **Incoterms® 2020** = **11 terms** published by **ICC** (Paris) — effective from **1 January 2020** — replaced **DAT with DPU**.
- ✅ **FOB, CIF, CFR** are used for **sea/inland waterway** transport only; **CPT, CIP, DAP, DDP, FCA, EXW** work for **all modes**.
- ✅ **DDP** = seller bears **maximum cost & risk**, including import duties.
- ✅ **L/C (UCP 600)** is governed by **ICC Publication No. 600** — most secure international payment method.
- ✅ **FEMA, 1999** governs all foreign exchange transactions in India — handled through **AD Banks** (Authorized Dealers).
- ✅ **EGM (Export General Manifest)** is filed by the **carrier** within **7 days of sailing** — a critical post-shipment compliance.
- ✅ **RoDTEP** (Remission of Duties and Taxes on Exported Products) and **RoSCTL** (Rebate of State & Central Taxes and Levies) are the **current export incentive schemes** replacing the older **MEIS** scheme (since 1 Jan 2021).
- ✅ **GST on exports** is **zero-rated** via either **LUT (Letter of Undertaking)** for export without payment of tax, or **payment of IGST and claiming refund**.
- ✅ **ISPM-15** governs **wooden packaging material** — must be **heat-treated (HT)** or **methyl bromide fumigated (MB)** and bear the **IPPC mark**.
- ✅ **Labelling of pre-packaged food** must follow **FSSAI Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, 2020** — mandatory declaration of **country of origin for imported food**.
- ✅ **Reefer (refrigerated) containers** carry **frozen/perishable food** at controlled temperatures (typically $-18°C$ to $-25°C$ for frozen, $0°C$ to $4°C$ for chilled).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Management in Global Logistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Risk Management in Global Logistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Management in Global Logistics** is the systematic, proactive, and continuous process of identifying, assessing, prioritizing, mitigating, and monitoring uncertainties that can disrupt the cross-border flow of food products — from farm/factory origin to the end consumer — while preserving product safety, quality, regulatory compliance, and economic value.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PEFTT636)**, Module 4 specifically addresses how food organizations recognize hazards (biological, chemical, physical, logistical, geopolitical, financial, and environmental), evaluate their likelihood and consequence, and deploy control measures (preventive, detective, and corrective) across the international supply chain.

### 1.1.1 Core Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Rigorous Definition | Food Logistics Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | A biological, chemical, physical, or economic agent with the potential to cause harm | *Salmonella* in poultry, pesticide residue in spices, microplastics in seafood |
| **Risk** | The combination of the probability of an event occurring and the severity of its consequence | Cold-chain breakdown leading to spoilage of frozen fish export |
| **Vulnerability** | The susceptibility of a supply chain node to disruption | Single-sourcing of a critical ingredient from one country |
| **Resilience** | The capacity of the supply chain to absorb disruption and recover functionality | Backup suppliers, redundant cold storage |
| **Disruption** | An unplanned event that materially alters expected supply chain performance | Port congestion, container shortage, trade embargo |
| **Risk Appetite** | The amount and type of risk an organization is willing to accept to meet strategic objectives | Tolerance for 2% shipment loss vs. 0.5% under FSSC 22000 |
| **EMV** | Expected Monetary Value = Probability × Monetary Impact | Forecasting the rupee cost of a delayed mango consignment |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Risk Management in Global Logistics like insuring and navigating a sailing ship carrying perishable mangoes across the Indian Ocean.**

Imagine you are a ship captain transporting fresh Alphonso mangoes from Mumbai to Dubai. Before sailing, you cannot remove every possible danger — storms, pirates, port delays, refrigeration failure, sudden bans, currency fluctuations. But you CAN do five things:

1. **Spot the dangers** (identify risks: storms, spoilage, customs rejection).
2. **Estimate how likely each danger is and how bad it would be** (assess probability × impact).
3. **Rank them in order of "biggest headache"** (prioritize — a storm is more urgent than a minor paperwork issue).
4. **Take preventive action** — install GPS, buy insurance, pack ice, carry backup documents (mitigation).
5. **Keep watching the horizon while sailing** — monitor temperature logs, weather updates, customs bulletins (continuous monitoring).

That is exactly what **Risk Management in Global Logistics** does at a corporate, scientific, and operational level for food supply chains.

## 1.3 The Food Industry Stakes — Why This Module Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The global food logistics market carries an estimated **8–12% annual loss** due to spoilage, contamination, and supply-chain disruptions. For a **5,000-tonne annual export** of seafood valued at ₹250 crore, a 10% loss equates to **₹25 crore** in shrinkage per year. Effective risk management can reclaim 60–70% of this.

The three pillars of food logistics risk are:

- **Food Safety Risk** → consumer health (microbial, chemical, allergen)
- **Food Defense Risk** → intentional adulteration, fraud, bioterrorism
- **Food Quality Risk** → sensory, nutritional, shelf-life degradation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students frequently confuse *food safety risk* with *logistics risk*. They are *interconnected but not identical*. A port strike (logistics risk) can CAUSE a food safety risk (cold-chain break → microbial growth). In exam answers, always trace the **causal chain**.

## 1.4 Categories of Risk in Global Food Logistics (High-Yield List)

The KTU 2024 syllabus segments risk into **five core categories**:

1. **Supply-side Risk** — raw material scarcity, supplier failure, crop disease
2. **Demand-side Risk** — volume forecasting errors, sudden bans, consumer trends
3. **Operational Risk** — equipment failure, cold-chain break, labor strikes
4. **Geopolitical / Macro Risk** — trade wars, tariffs, sanctions, war, pandemics
5. **Environmental / Climate Risk** — floods, cyclones, temperature anomalies, emissions rules
6. **Financial / Currency Risk** — forex volatility, inflation, payment default
7. **Regulatory / Compliance Risk** — new food laws, labeling changes, pesticide MRL revisions
8. **Reputational / Brand Risk** — social media crisis, recall events

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Management Process (ISO 31000 Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 examiners expect students to describe this process using **ISO 31000:2018** terminology because global food companies (Nestlé, ITC, Fonterra, Cargill) adopt it. Memorize the **7-stage cycle**.

1. **Establish Context** — define scope, stakeholders, regulatory environment, risk criteria.
2. **Risk Identification** — enumerate hazards via tools (FMEA, HACCP, Bowtie, brainstorming, Delphi).
3. **Risk Analysis** — assess probability, consequence, and exposure qualitatively and/or quantitatively.
4. **Risk Evaluation** — compare estimated risk against pre-defined risk criteria (likelihood × severity matrix).
5. **Risk Treatment (Mitigation)** — apply the **4T strategy**: Terminate, Treat, Transfer, Tolerate.
6. **Monitoring & Review** — KPIs, audits, IoT sensors, control charts.
7. **Communication & Consultation** — internal + external stakeholder reporting.

## 2.2 Risk Assessment Mathematics — The Quantitative Backbone

### 2.2.1 Risk Score (Qualitative/Semi-Quantitative)

$$ \text{Risk Score (R)} = P \times I $$

where $P$ = Probability (1–5 scale), $I$ = Impact (1–5 scale). Risk Score ranges from **1 to 25**.

### 2.2.2 Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

$$ \text{EMV} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i $$

where $P_i$ = probability of risk scenario $i$, $C_i$ = monetary consequence of scenario $i$, $n$ = number of scenarios. **Units: ₹ / USD.**

### 2.2.3 Risk Priority Number (RPN) — FMEA Specific

$$ \text{RPN} = S \times O \times D $$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection Difficulty (1–10). Range: **1 to 1000**. RPN > 200 = critical action zone.

### 2.2.4 Value at Risk (VaR) — Financial/Logistics Risk

$$ \text{VaR}_{\alpha} = \inf\{x \mid P(L > x) \le 1 - \alpha\} $$

Used for forecasting maximum monetary loss at confidence level $\alpha$ (e.g., 95%, 99%).

### 2.2.5 Total Cost of Risk (TCoR)

$$ \text{TCoR} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}} $$

This is the **P-A-I-E model** borrowed from quality cost management.

### 2.2.6 Safety Stock Buffer (Statistical Risk Buffer)

$$ \text{Safety Stock} = z \times \sigma_{LT} $$

where $z$ = service-level Z-score (e.g., 1.65 for 95%), $\sigma_{LT}$ = standard deviation of lead time.

### 2.2.7 Cold Chain Risk — Time-Temperature Tolerance

$$ Q_{10} = \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}} $$

where $R_1, R_2$ are spoilage reaction rates at temperatures $T_1, T_2$ (°C). The **Q₁₀ rule** says microbial spoilage rate **doubles to triples** per 10 °C rise.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Risk Score | $R = P \times I$ | dimensionless (1–25) | Risk matrix plotting |
| 2 | EMV | $\text{EMV} = \sum P_i C_i$ | ₹ / USD | Investment decisions in mitigation |
| 3 | RPN (FMEA) | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | dimensionless (1–1000) | Hazard prioritization in HACCP |
| 4 | TCoR (P-A-I-E) | $\text{TCoR} = P + A + IF + EF$ | ₹ / year | Total logistics risk costing |
| 5 | Safety Stock | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | days or units | Buffer against supply disruption |
| 6 | Q₁₀ Rule | $Q_{10} = (R_2/R_1)^{10/\Delta T}$ | dimensionless | Cold-chain shelf-life risk |
| 7 | Value at Risk | $\text{VaR}_\alpha$ | ₹ at confidence $\alpha$ | Financial hedging |
| 8 | Probability of Stockout | $P_s = 1 - \text{CSL}$ | dimensionless | Service level planning |
| 9 | Lead-time Variance | $\sigma_{LT}^2 = \sigma_d^2 \bar{L} + \bar{d}^2 \sigma_L^2$ | days² | Disruption forecasting |
| 10 | Mitigation ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{C_{\text{avoided}} - C_{\text{mitigation}}}{C_{\text{mitigation}}}$ | ratio | Justifying HACCP/QMS investments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question says *"calculate the risk priority"*, default to **RPN = S × O × D**. When it says *"expected loss"*, use **EMV**. When it says *"maximum probable loss"*, use **VaR**.

## 2.4 The 4T Mitigation Framework (KTU High-Yield)

| Strategy | Meaning | Food Logistics Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Terminate** | Eliminate the risk source | Drop an unreliable supplier entirely |
| **Treat** | Reduce likelihood or impact | Install IoT temperature sensors in reefer containers |
| **Transfer** | Shift risk to third party | Marine cargo insurance, Incoterms CIP/DAP |
| **Tolerate** | Accept within appetite | Absorb minor documentation delays |

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering / Industry

- **Export Houses** use RPN to decide which pre-shipment tests to perform.
- **3PL Cold Chain Operators** rely on Q₁₀ calculations to size refrigeration capacity.
- **Banks & Insurers** use VaR and EMV to price marine cargo policies for spices, seafood, and dairy.
- **Food Regulators (FSSAI, USFDA, EFSA)** mandate HACCP, which is fundamentally a risk-management framework.
- **Industry 4.0**: Blockchain + IoT enable real-time risk monitoring across borders.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Risk Score using P × I Matrix

### Problem
A Kerala-based spice exporter ships cardamom to Germany. Risk analyst identifies **four risks** with the following ratings:

| Risk | Probability (P, 1–5) | Impact (I, 1–5) |
|---|---|---|
| R1: Customs delay | 4 | 3 |
| R2: Cold-chain break | 2 | 5 |
| R3: Currency fluctuation | 3 | 4 |
| R4: Pest infestation | 1 | 5 |

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute Risk Score for each.

$$R_1 = 4 \times 3 = 12$$

$$R_2 = 2 \times 5 = 10$$

$$R_3 = 3 \times 4 = 12$$

$$R_4 = 1 \times 5 = 5$$

**Step 2:** Classify into zones using standard 5×5 matrix.

| Zone | Score Range | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Low | 1–4 | Monitor |
| Medium | 5–9 | Treat / Schedule |
| High | 10–15 | Immediate action |
| Critical | 16–25 | Terminate / Transfer |

**Step 3:** Apply classification.

| Risk | Score | Zone | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| R1 Customs delay | 12 | High | Treat — pre-clear docs digitally |
| R2 Cold-chain break | 10 | High | Treat — install IoT loggers |
| R3 Currency fluctuation | 12 | High | Transfer — forward forex contract |
| R4 Pest infestation | 5 | Medium | Treat — fumigation certificate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU:** R1 and R3 have *equal* scores but require *different* strategies because R1 is operational and R3 is financial. **Always justify mitigation by risk type, not just score.**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

### Problem
An Indian basmati rice exporter faces three disruption scenarios for a ₹10 crore shipment to Saudi Arabia:

| Scenario | Probability $P_i$ | Monetary Loss $C_i$ (₹ crore) |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth delivery | 0.70 | 0.00 |
| Port strike (7-day delay) | 0.20 | 0.80 |
| Container rejection (quality) | 0.10 | 2.50 |

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the EMV formula.

$$\text{EMV} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times C_i$$

**Step 2:** Compute each term.

$$P_1 C_1 = 0.70 \times 0.00 = 0.00 \text{ ₹ crore}$$

$$P_2 C_2 = 0.20 \times 0.80 = 0.16 \text{ ₹ crore}$$

$$P_3 C_3 = 0.10 \times 2.50 = 0.25 \text{ ₹ crore}$$

**Step 3:** Sum.

$$\text{EMV} = 0.00 + 0.16 + 0.25 = 0.41 \text{ ₹ crore}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.

$$\text{EMV} = ₹41 \text{ lakh expected loss per shipment}$$

If a marine insurance policy costs ₹25 lakh and covers 100% of the loss, the net benefit is:

$$\text{Net Benefit} = 41 - 25 = +₹16 \text{ lakh}$$

> **Therefore:** **Transfer (insurance)** is justified by positive net benefit.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — RPN Calculation (FMEA-Style)

### Problem
In a shrimp export pre-processing line, three Critical Control Points (CCPs) are identified:

| CCP | Severity (S, 1–10) | Occurrence (O, 1–10) | Detection (D, 1–10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CCP1: Cooking temperature | 9 | 3 | 4 |
| CCP2: Metal detection | 8 | 5 | 2 |
| CCP3: Cold storage entry | 7 | 6 | 5 |

### Solution

$$\text{RPN}_1 = 9 \times 3 \times 4 = 108$$

$$\text{RPN}_2 = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80$$

$$\text{RPN}_3 = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210$$

**Ranking & Decision:**
- CCP3 (RPN = 210) → **Critical**, immediate CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action).
- CCP1 (RPN = 108) → **High**, schedule improvement.
- CCP2 (RPN = 80) → **Medium**, routine monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Answer Format:** Always present RPN in **descending order** with a brief justification sentence. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if reasoning is absent.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Risk × Mitigation Mapping

| Risk Category | Probability Driver | Impact Driver | Best Mitigation Strategy | Quantitative Tool |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold-chain break | Equipment age | Distance, ambient temp | IoT monitoring, redundancy | Q₁₀, RPN |
| Supplier failure | Geopolitical | Single-sourcing | Dual sourcing, audits | VaR, EMV |
| Currency volatility | Macro-economy | Contract value | Forward contracts, options | VaR |
| Regulatory change | Trade policy shifts | Market access | Compliance team, horizon scan | EMV |
| Microbial contamination | Hygiene, time-temp | Public health, recall | HACCP, pasteurization | RPN |
| Climate disruption | Region, season | Volume, perishability | Diversified sourcing | VaR |
| Documentation error | Manual process | Port demurrage | Blockchain, EDI | EMV |
| Cyber/data breach | IT maturity | Reputational | ISO 27001, encryption | TCoR |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO 31000 Risk Management Cycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Establish Context"] --> nodeB["Risk Identification"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Risk Analysis"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Risk Evaluation"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Risk Treatment"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Monitoring and Review"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Communication and Consultation"]
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeH["4T Framework"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Terminate"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Treat"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Transfer"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Tolerate"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Risk management is a *closed-loop* system — outputs of monitoring feed back into context-setting, ensuring continuous improvement (Plan-Do-Check-Act synergy with ISO 22000).

## 4.2 Global Food Logistics Risk Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG["ORIGIN LAYER"]
        farmA["Farm and Primary Processing"]
        suppA["Supplier Network"]
    end
    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        procA["Manufacturing Plant"]
        qcA["Quality Control Lab"]
    end
    subgraph LOG["LOGISTICS LAYER"]
        coldA["Cold Storage Hub"]
        portA["Port and Customs"]
        shipA["Shipping and Reefer"]
    end
    subgraph MKT["MARKET LAYER"]
        distA["Distributor"]
        retA["Retailer"]
        consA["Consumer"]
    end
    farmA --> procA
    suppA --> procA
    procA --> qcA
    qcA --> coldA
    coldA --> portA
    portA --> shipA
    shipA --> distA
    distA --> retA
    retA --> consA
    riskA["SUPPLY RISK"] -.-> farmA
    riskB["OPERATIONAL RISK"] -.-> coldA
    riskC["GEOPOLITICAL RISK"] -.-> portA
    riskD["COLD CHAIN RISK"] -.-> shipA
    riskE["DEMAND RISK"] -.-> consA
    riskF["REGULATORY RISK"] -.-> procA
```

**Interpretation:** Every node is a *risk anchor*. The dotted lines indicate where each risk category *typically* attacks the chain. In exam answers, students should annotate at least 2 risks per layer.

## 4.3 Bowtie Diagram — Cold Chain Spoilage (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    threatA["Threats: Reefer Failure, Port Delay, Power Cut"] --> hazardA["HAZARD: Temperature Excursion"]
    hazardA --> topA["TOP EVENT: Microbial Spoilage"]
    topA --> consA["Consequence 1: Consumer Illness"]
    topA --> consB["Consequence 2: Product Recall"]
    topA --> consC["Consequence 3: Brand Loss"]
    pC1["Preventive Controls: Pre-cooling, IoT sensors"] -.-> threatA
    pC2["Mitigation Controls: Cold backup, insurance"] -.-> consA
    pC3["Recovery Controls: Recall plan, PR"] -.-> consB
```

**Reading:** Bowtie separates *left-side prevention* (stop the threat) from *right-side mitigation* (reduce consequence). This is the standard tool used by FSSC 22000 / BRC auditors.

## 4.4 Risk Heat Map (Schematic — Mermaid Block Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    h1["LIKELIHOOD 5"] --> h2["M"] --> h3["H"] --> h4["H"] --> h5["C"]
    h6["LIKELIHOOD 4"] --> h7["L"] --> h8["M"] --> h9["H"] --> h10["H"]
    h11["LIKELIHOOD 3"] --> h12["L"] --> h13["L"] --> h14["M"] --> h15["H"]
    h16["LIKELIHOOD 2"] --> h17["L"] --> h18["L"] --> h19["M"] --> h20["M"]
    h21["LIKELIHOOD 1"] --> h22["L"] --> h23["L"] --> h24["L"] --> h25["M"]
    h26["IMPACT 1"] --> h27["IMPACT 2"] --> h28["IMPACT 3"] --> h29["IMPACT 4"] --> h30["IMPACT 5"]
    legendA["L = Low Risk"] --- legendB["M = Medium Risk"]
    legendB --- legendC["H = High Risk"]
    legendC --- legendD["C = Critical Risk"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Practice Tip:** In 14-mark answers, always place the heat map at the start to set context, then place risks on it (R1 at H, R2 at H, etc.). This earns the "structured presentation" mark.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer in 4–5 sentences. Define, list, or briefly explain.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]`

**Define risk management in global food logistics. List any four categories of supply chain risk with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Risk management in global food logistics is the systematic process of identifying, assessing, mitigating, and monitoring uncertainties that can disrupt cross-border food flows while ensuring safety, quality, and compliance (1 mark).

Four categories with examples (2 marks — ½ mark each):

1. **Supply-side risk** — failure of a single shrimp supplier in Andhra Pradesh.
2. **Operational risk** — breakdown of reefer container during transshipment at Colombo.
3. **Geopolitical risk** — sudden export ban on basmati rice by government.
4. **Regulatory risk** — change in EU Maximum Residue Limits (MRL) for pesticides.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students write only *names* of risks without examples. Each example must be **food-specific** (not generic logistics). Marks are deducted for vague examples like "delay in transport."

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]`

**Distinguish between Hazard and Risk in the context of food logistics. Provide one numerical illustration using Risk Score = Probability × Impact.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A potential source of harm | Probability × Consequence of harm |
| Nature | Qualitative (what can hurt) | Quantitative (how likely and how severe) |
| Example | *Salmonella* in frozen chicken | Cold-chain break → 40% probability × ₹50 lakh impact |

(1.5 marks for table)

Numerical illustration (1.5 marks):

$$R = P \times I = 0.40 \times 50 = ₹20 \text{ lakh}$$

This indicates a **High Zone** risk, requiring immediate mitigation such as IoT temperature monitoring and HACCP enforcement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Provide diagrams, formulas, and structured points. KTU valuation key requires: *Introduction (2M), Body (10M), Conclusion/Diagram (2M)*.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model, Q-A]`

**"Global food logistics faces unprecedented risks in the post-pandemic era." Critically analyze the categories of risk in global food supply chains with a suitable risk assessment framework. Evaluate mitigation strategies using the 4T model with food-industry examples. (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — Categories of Risk and Risk Assessment Framework (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Introduction (2 marks):* Global food logistics spans multi-modal, multi-country movement of perishable and non-perishable food products. The interconnected nature exposes the chain to compound risks.

*Body — Risk Categories (3 marks):*

| Category | Description | Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| Supply risk | Raw material failure | Cocoa bean shortage due to West African drought |
| Demand risk | Volume forecasting errors | Sudden drop in restaurant demand post-pandemic |
| Operational risk | Process / equipment failure | Reefer container breakdown on Reefer ship |
| Geopolitical risk | Trade policy, war, sanctions | Russia-Ukraine wheat export disruption |
| Environmental risk | Climate, natural disasters | Kerala floods damaging cardamom crop |
| Financial risk | Forex, inflation, default | Rupee depreciation raising import costs |
| Regulatory risk | New food laws, MRL changes | EU banning certain pesticide residues |

*Body — Risk Assessment Framework (2 marks):* Use **ISO 31000:2018** seven-stage cycle: Establish Context → Identify → Analyze → Evaluate → Treat → Monitor → Communicate. Quantify using **Risk Score (P × I)** and **EMV**.

**Part (b) — 4T Mitigation Model with Food Examples (7 Marks)**

*Body — 4T Model (4 marks):*

| Strategy | Meaning | Food Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Terminate** | Eliminate risk source | Drop an unreliable pesticide-tested supplier |
| **Treat** | Reduce likelihood/impact | Install IoT temperature sensors in reefer fleet |
| **Transfer** | Shift risk to third party | Marine cargo insurance (Incoterm CIP) |
| **Tolerate** | Accept within appetite | Allow minor documentation delays ≤ 2 days |

*Body — Quantitative Justification (2 marks):*

$$\text{EMV} = \sum P_i C_i$$

For a ₹5 crore spice shipment, EMV = ₹18 lakh. Insurance premium = ₹12 lakh. Net benefit = ₹6 lakh → Transfer justified.

*Conclusion (1 mark):* A blended 4T strategy, aligned with ISO 31000 and HACCP, builds resilience in global food logistics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often describe 4T without **food-specific examples**. Generic examples like "buy insurance" lose marks. Use *marine cargo insurance for frozen shrimp*, *blockchain for traceability*, etc.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - 2024 Scheme Model, Q-B]`

**A Kerala-based marine food exporter ships frozen tuna worth ₹8 crore to Japan monthly. Apply the FMEA-based Risk Priority Number (RPN) technique to identify the top three hazards across the cold-chain process. Develop a quantitative mitigation plan using Expected Monetary Value (EMV) and Safety Stock formulas. (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — FMEA and RPN Calculation for Three Hazards (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Introduction (2 marks):* FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is a structured hazard-assessment tool used in HACCP-aligned food safety systems. RPN = S × O × D ranks hazards for prioritization.

*Body — Three Hazards Identified (3 marks):*

| ID | Hazard | Failure Mode | S | O | D | RPN |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Pre-cooling delay | Tuna enters blast freezer > 4°C | 9 | 6 | 5 | **270** |
| H2 | Reefer breakdown mid-voyage | Container temp > -18°C | 10 | 4 | 6 | **240** |
| H3 | Customs documentation error | Detention > 5 days at Tokyo port | 6 | 5 | 7 | **210** |

*Body — Ranking & Justification (2 marks):* H1 (RPN 270) is the *critical priority* because S × O × D is highest. Corrective action: install calibrated IoT loggers + SOP for blast-freezer entry.

**Part (b) — Quantitative Mitigation Plan using EMV & Safety Stock (7 Marks)**

*Body — EMV (3 marks):*

$$\text{EMV} = \sum P_i C_i$$

| Scenario | $P_i$ | $C_i$ (₹ crore) | $P_i C_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smooth delivery | 0.75 | 0 | 0.000 |
| Reefer break | 0.15 | 0.80 | 0.120 |
| Customs rejection | 0.10 | 2.00 | 0.200 |

$$\text{EMV} = 0.000 + 0.120 + 0.200 = ₹32 \text{ lakh/month}$$

**Mitigation Decision:** Install IoT sensors (cost ₹8 lakh/month) + insurance (₹10 lakh/month). Total mitigation cost = ₹18 lakh. Avoided loss ≈ ₹32 lakh. Net benefit = ₹14 lakh.

*Body — Safety Stock (2 marks):*

$$SS = z \times \sigma_{LT}$$

For 97.5% service level, $z = 1.96$. Lead-time SD = 2 days. Demand/day = 10 tonnes.

$$SS = 1.96 \times 2 \times 10 = 39.2 \text{ tonnes}$$

This buffer offsets supply disruption risk.

*Conclusion (1 mark):* RPN-driven hazard control combined with EMV-justified mitigation and statistical safety stock creates a *resilient, cost-efficient* global tuna export operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** In numerical answers, students forget to **state units** (₹, tonnes, days). KTU valuation key deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit. Always append units to every number.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- [ ] **Risk = Probability × Impact** — the foundational equation; memorize the 5×5 matrix zones.
- [ ] **ISO 31000:2018** is the global standard; remember the **7 stages** in cyclical order.
- [ ] **RPN = S × O × D** — used in FMEA / HACCP; threshold for action is usually **> 200**.
- [ ] **EMV = Σ PᵢCᵢ** — used to justify mitigation spend vs. expected loss.
- [ ] **Safety Stock = z·σ_LT** — service-level buffer; z = 1.65 (95%), 1.96 (97.5%), 2.33 (99%).
- [ ] **Q₁₀ Rule** — spoilage rate doubles every 10 °C rise; critical for cold chain design.
- [ ] **4T Model** — Terminate, Treat, Transfer, Tolerate — must use **food-specific examples**.
- [ ] **Five+ risk categories** — Supply, Demand, Operational, Geopolitical, Environmental, Financial, Regulatory — list at least **five** with **examples**.
- [ ] **Bowtie diagram** — separates *prevention* (left) from *mitigation* (right); useful for 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Heat Map** — always present at the start of risk assessment answers for visual structure.
- [ ] **VaR** — used in financial / monetary exposure analysis at 95% / 99% confidence.
- [ ] **TCoR (P-A-I-E)** — total cost of risk = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.
- [ ] **Always quantify** — qualitative answers alone are insufficient for KTU 14-mark questions; include at least one numeric or formula-based sub-answer.
- [ ] **Use Indian food examples** — Kerala spices, Alphonso mango, basmati rice, frozen shrimp, cardamom — examiners reward contextualization.
- [ ] **Connect logistics risk to food safety** — show the causal chain (e.g., port delay → cold-chain break → microbial growth → consumer hazard).
- [ ] **Standards to remember**: ISO 31000 (Risk), ISO 22000 (Food Safety Mgmt), FSSC 22000, HACCP, BRC, SQF, GDP (Good Distribution Practice).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Name the risk, score it with RPN/EMV, plot it on a heat map, mitigate it with the 4T, monitor it with IoT."* — this single sentence covers 80% of long-answer marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Relationship Management in LSCM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT636 - FOOD SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Customer Relationship Management in LSCM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** in the context of **Logistics and Supply Chain Management (LSCM)** is defined as the integrated strategic framework, supported by information technology and data analytics, that an organization employs to manage, analyse, and coordinate all interactions with its current and prospective customers across the entire supply chain — from raw material sourcing to last-mile delivery — with the objective of enhancing customer retention, lifetime value, and overall satisfaction.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme perspective on LSCM, CRM extends beyond marketing; it is a **cross-functional discipline** that synchronises sales, customer service, order fulfilment, distribution, and post-sale support into a unified customer-centric ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standardised):** *CRM in LSCM is the holistic management of customer-facing logistics processes — including order capture, delivery scheduling, reverse logistics, complaint resolution, and demand sensing — to build long-term, mutually beneficial relationships that translate into sustainable competitive advantage.*

### 1.1.1 Sub-Definitions in LSCM Context

| Sub-Construct | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Customer Value (CV)** | The perceived worth of a product/service relative to its total cost of ownership (TCO). |
| **Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** | A psychological measure reflecting the gap between customer expectation and perceived performance. |
| **Customer Loyalty** | A behavioural commitment to repurchase a brand despite competitive offerings. |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV/CLTV)** | The total revenue a business can expect from a single customer account throughout the business relationship. |
| **Churn Rate** | The percentage of customers who stop purchasing from a firm during a specific time period. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Favourite Neighbourhood Tea Stall" Analogy** 🍵

Imagine a small tea stall in Kerala run by *Appukuttan*. He knows that *Ramesh uncle* likes his tea with less sugar and a hint of ginger, that *Priya chechi* takes hers in a steel tumbler, and that *Aravind* comes only on Saturday evenings after tuition. He even remembers when his regulars are sick and offers them a lighter *kashayam* instead.

That is **CRM at its purest**:
- **Knowing** the customer (Data Collection)
- **Remembering** preferences (Data Storage/Analysis)
- **Personalising** service (Customisation)
- **Predicting** needs (Demand Sensing)
- **Following up** post-service (Feedback Loop)

Now scale this up to a multinational food company like *Nestlé* or a dairy giant like *Amul*. CRM is the **digital, automated, scalable version of Appukuttan's memory** — applied across thousands of distributors, retailers, and end consumers to ensure the right product reaches the right customer at the right time, in the right condition.

---

## 1.3 The 3 Pillars of CRM in LSCM (Standard KTU Framework)

CRM in LSCM rests on **three foundational pillars** which examiners frequently test:

1. **Operational CRM** — Streamlines and automates customer-facing business processes (sales force automation, order processing, customer service).
2. **Analytical CRM** — Uses data warehousing, OLAP, and data mining to extract actionable insights about customer behaviour.
3. **Collaborative CRM** — Integrates all departments (sales, marketing, technical support, logistics) and external stakeholders (suppliers, distributors, retailers) to share customer information seamlessly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Term for KTU 2024 Board Exam:** *Operational CRM* handles **processes**, *Analytical CRM* handles **intelligence**, and *Collaborative CRM* handles **communication channels** — remember the mnemonic **PIC** (Process–Intelligence–Communication).

---

## 1.4 Why CRM is Critical in Food Supply Chains (Unique to PEFTT636)

Food supply chains are uniquely **perishable, regulated, and trust-sensitive**. Therefore, CRM in food LSCM has additional dimensions:

- **Traceability & Recall Management** — If a batch is contaminated, the firm must instantly identify and notify affected customers.
- **Cold Chain Integrity** — CRM must communicate real-time temperature data to assure customers of food safety.
- **Sensory Quality Assurance** — Beyond price, customers expect consistent taste, freshness, and appearance.
- **Sustainability Transparency** — Modern consumers demand visibility into sourcing ethics, carbon footprint, and fair-trade practices.
- **Hyper-Local Demand Patterns** — Festivals (Onam, Vishu, Ramadan) cause demand spikes that CRM systems must predict.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of CRM in LSCM

### 2.1.1 The CRM Value Chain (Strategic Framework)

CRM transforms a linear supply chain into a **closed-loop, feedback-driven value network**. The following logic chain represents the core theoretical process:

- **Step 1 — Customer Acquisition:** Identify and attract potential customers through marketing, referrals, and outreach campaigns. *Why?* A supply chain cannot be customer-centric without first having customers.
- **Step 2 — Data Integration:** Consolidate customer data from POS systems, loyalty programmes, e-commerce platforms, social media, and ERP systems into a **Customer Data Warehouse (CDW)**. *How?* Using middleware like APIs and EDI standards.
- **Step 3 — Customer Segmentation:** Cluster customers using models like **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** or **LTV-based segmentation** to enable differentiated service.
- **Step 4 — Personalised Offerings:** Tailor products, pricing, promotions, and delivery modes to each segment. *Example:* Premium organic produce delivered within 2 hours for high-LTV urban customers vs. weekly subscription for suburban families.
- **Step 5 — Order Fulfilment & Logistics Execution:** Trigger warehouse picking, route optimisation, and last-mile delivery — all informed by customer preference data (e.g., preferred delivery time window).
- **Step 6 — Post-Sale Service & Reverse Logistics:** Handle returns, complaints, and feedback. *Why critical in food?* Spoilage complaints must be processed within hours, not days.
- **Step 7 — Analytics & Continuous Improvement:** Feed post-sale data back into the CDW to refine future interactions. *How?* Using churn prediction models, NPS (Net Promoter Score) tracking, and sentiment analysis.

---

### 2.1.2 Theoretical Models Used in CRM-LSCM

| Model / Framework | Origin / Author | Application in Food LSCM |
|---|---|---|
| **IDIC Model** (Identify–Differentiate–Interact–Customise) | Peppers & Rogers (1993) | Foundation for building customer strategy in B2B food distribution. |
| **Payne's Five Process Model** | Adrian Payne (2000) | Strategic → Operational → Analytical CRM transition. |
| **CRM Value Chain** | Francis Buttle (2004) | Maps primary and support activities of CRM in supply chain. |
| **SERVQUAL Model** | Parasuraman et al. (1988) | Measures service quality gaps — heavily used in food delivery logistics. |
| **RFM Analysis** | Industry Standard | Customer segmentation based on purchasing behaviour. |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) Model** | Various | Prioritises high-value customers for premium logistics services. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *"Explain the IDIC model with relevance to LSCM"*. Memorise the four stages in order: **I-D-I-C**.

---

### 2.1.3 Types of CRM Architecture (KTU Frequently Asked)

- **Operational CRM Architecture** — Front-office systems: Sales Force Automation (SFA), Customer Service & Support (CSS), Enterprise Marketing Automation (EMA). *In food LSCM:* Used to manage distributor orders, retailer complaints, and field sales visits.
- **Analytical CRM Architecture** — Back-office systems: Data warehouse, OLAP cubes, data mining engines. *In food LSCM:* Predicts festival demand, identifies at-risk retail accounts.
- **Collaborative CRM Architecture** — Cross-channel communication portals, partner relationship modules, social CRM. *In food LSCM:* Connects farmers, processors, distributors, retailers, and end consumers on a single visibility platform.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Management Metrics)

While CRM is qualitative-heavy, the **2024 Scheme** does test quantitative metrics. Below is the **cheat sheet** every KTU student must memorise.

| Metric | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** | $CAC = \dfrac{\text{Total Marketing \& Sales Spend}}{\text{Number of New Customers Acquired}}$ | Currency per customer; **lower is better**. |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \times r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | Where $r_t$ is retention probability, $d$ is discount rate, $T$ is horizon. |
| **Churn Rate** | $\text{Churn} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost in Period}}{\text{Customers at Start of Period}} \times 100$ | Expressed in **%**; **lower is better**. |
| **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $CRR = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Churn Rate}}{100}\right) \times 100$ | Inverse of churn; **higher is better**. |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | Scale: $-100$ to $+100$. |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $CSAT = \dfrac{\text{Sum of Satisfaction Ratings}}{\text{Number of Responses}}$ | Usually on a 1–5 scale. |
| **RFM Score (Recency-Frequency-Monetary)** | $RFM = R_{score} + F_{score} + M_{score}$ | Each scored 1–5; max score = **15**. |
| **CLV : CAC Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{CLV}{CAC}$ | Ideal ratio $\geq 3:1$ for sustainable growth. |
| **Customer Equity (CE)** | $CE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CLV_i$ | Total value of all customers in the database. |
| **Service Quality Gap (SERVQUAL)** | $\text{Gap}_5 = \text{Perception} - \text{Expectation}$ | Negative gap = service failure. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The **CLV : CAC ratio** is the single most-tested metric. If the ratio is below 3:1, the firm is over-spending on acquisition. If above 5:1, the firm is **under-investing** in growth — both are inefficiencies. Students must state this interpretation in board answers.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility & Industry Applications

CRM in food LSCM is operationalised across multiple touchpoints:

- **FMCG Giants (HUL, Nestlé, ITC Foods):** Use CRM analytics to design route-to-market strategies, optimise distributor margins, and design retail merchandising.
- **Quick Commerce (Blinkit, Zepto, Swiggy Instamart):** Real-time CRM predicts basket-level demand per micro-PIN code, enabling sub-15-minute delivery.
- **Dairy Industry (Amul, Hatsun):** CRM-based subscription models ensure daily door-step delivery with churn prediction for dormant customers.
- **Export-Oriented Seafood/Meat (Kerala-based MPEDA-linked firms):** CRM integrates with traceability platforms (e.g., GS1 standards) to provide EU/US importers with farm-to-fork data.
- **Organised Retail (Reliance Smart, Lulu Hypermarket):** Loyalty programmes convert POS data into personalised offers, driving repeat visits.

> [!NOTE]
> The convergence of **CRM + IoT + Blockchain** in food LSCM is a **2024 Scheme NEP-aligned** topic. Students should be able to articulate how blockchain-enabled traceability enhances customer trust — a key UPSC/KTU interview point as well.
