# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of CRM Types

Since CRM is a qualitative management discipline, the "derivations" in this section are **logical frameworks, decision matrices, and step-by-step analytical constructs** that the KTU board tests frequently.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Construction of an RFM Segmentation Matrix

**RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary)** is the most-tested segmentation model in CRM-LSCM. Below is the explicit construction logic:

- **Step 1 — Data Extraction:** Pull transactional data fields: *Customer ID, Last Purchase Date, Purchase Count, Total Spend*.
  - *Logic:* RFM requires only **3 numerical variables** per customer — making it computationally lightweight and ideal for ERP integration.
- **Step 2 — Recency Calculation ($R$):**
  - $R = \text{Today} - \text{Last Purchase Date}$, measured in **days**.
  - Lower $R$ = more engaged customer.
- **Step 3 — Frequency Calculation ($F$):**
  - $F = \text{Total Number of Transactions in Defined Period}$ (typically 6 or 12 months).
- **Step 4 — Monetary Calculation ($M$):**
  - $M = \text{Total Revenue Generated by Customer}$ in the same period.
- **Step 5 — Score Assignment:** Assign quintile scores (1–5) for each dimension.
  - For $R$: Lowest $R$ value → Score 5 (best).
  - For $F$ and $M$: Highest value → Score 5 (best).
- **Step 6 — Composite RFM Score:**
  - $RFM_{score} = R_{score} + F_{score} + M_{score}$, range: 3 to 15.
- **Step 7 — Customer Bucketing:**

| RFM Score Range | Segment | Strategic Action |
|---|---|---|
| 13 – 15 | **Champions** | Reward with VIP offers, early access to new products. |
| 10 – 12 | **Loyal Customers** | Cross-sell, upsell, referral incentives. |
| 7 – 9 | **At-Risk / Potential Loyalists** | Re-engagement campaigns, personalised discounts. |
| 4 – 6 | **Hibernating / Lost** | Win-back campaigns or remove from active targeting. |
| 3 | **Lost Customers** | Minimal spend — survey to understand churn reason. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RFM works in food LSCM:** A customer who buys *Amul butter* weekly, recently, and spends ₹500/month is far more valuable than a one-time bulk buyer. RFM quantifies this difference and routes it into the logistics system — the Champions segment gets *priority delivery slots* and *freshest stock*.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step CLV Derivation (Annual Retention Model)

The simplest CLV model — **favoured in KTU numerical questions** — is derived as follows:

- **Step 1 — Define Variables:**
  - Let $A$ = Average Order Value (₹)
  - Let $f$ = Purchase Frequency per year
  - Let $g$ = Gross Margin (as a decimal)
  - Let $r$ = Customer Retention Rate (as a decimal)
  - Let $d$ = Discount Rate (as a decimal)
  - Let $T$ = Time horizon (years)
- **Step 2 — Compute Annual Customer Revenue:**
  - $\text{Annual Revenue} = A \times f$
- **Step 3 — Compute Annual Profit Contribution:**
  - $\text{Annual Profit} = A \times f \times g$
- **Step 4 — Apply Retention Decay Over Time:**
  - In year $t$, the probability that the customer is still active is $r^t$.
- **Step 5 — Discount Future Cash Flows to Present Value:**
  - The present value factor is $\dfrac{1}{(1+d)^t}$.
- **Step 6 — Sum the Discounted Cash Flows Over Horizon $T$:**
  - 
  $$CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{A \times f \times g \times r^{t}}{(1+d)^{t}}$$
- **Step 7 — Simplify Using Geometric Series:**

  Since the term inside the sum is a constant $C = A \times f \times g$ multiplied by $\left(\dfrac{r}{1+d}\right)^{t}$, and for an infinite horizon ($T \to \infty$), the geometric series converges to:

  $$CLV_{\infty} = \dfrac{A \times f \times g}{1 - \dfrac{r}{1+d}} = \dfrac{A \times f \times g \times (1+d)}{1+d-r}$$

  - *Logic Check:* If retention rate $r$ approaches 0, CLV approaches the single-period profit (correct). If $r$ approaches $(1+d)$, CLV → ∞ (the customer is "forever loyal" in present value terms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Convention:** When the question states *"compute CLV for a food retail customer with AOV = ₹500, frequency = 12 orders/year, gross margin = 20%, retention = 80%, discount rate = 10%"*, students must use the **infinite horizon** formula unless otherwise specified.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (CLV)

**Problem (Modelled on KTU 2024 Pattern):**

A premium organic food brand has the following customer profile:
- Average Order Value ($A$) = ₹800
- Purchase Frequency ($f$) = 6 orders/year
- Gross Margin ($g$) = 25% = 0.25
- Retention Rate ($r$) = 85% = 0.85
- Discount Rate ($d$) = 10% = 0.10

**Compute the Customer Lifetime Value using the infinite horizon formula.**

**Step 1:** Identify the constant profit contribution per year.

$$A \times f \times g = 800 \times 6 \times 0.25 = \text{₹}1200$$

**Step 2:** Apply the infinite horizon CLV formula.

$$CLV = \dfrac{A \times f \times g \times (1+d)}{1+d-r}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$CLV = \dfrac{1200 \times (1 + 0.10)}{1 + 0.10 - 0.85}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately.

$$\text{Numerator} = 1200 \times 1.10 = 1320$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 1.10 - 0.85 = 0.25$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$CLV = \dfrac{1320}{0.25} = \text{₹}5280$$

**Interpretation:** Each customer in this segment is worth **₹5,280** over the indefinite future. The brand can justify spending **up to ₹1,760** (one-third of CLV) on acquisition and still remain profitable per the **3:1 CLV:CAC rule**.

---

### 3.1.4 Analytical Matrix: CRM vs. Traditional Customer Service

| Dimension | Traditional Customer Service | CRM-Enabled LSCM |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Storage** | Siloed, manual registers | Unified Customer Data Warehouse |
| **Customer View** | Transactional (one-off) | 360° holistic view |
| **Response Time** | Hours to days | Real-time / minutes |
| **Personalisation** | Generic, mass-oriented | Segment-level / individual |
| **Channel Integration** | Single channel (phone/in-person) | Omni-channel (web, app, social, voice) |
| **Predictive Capability** | Reactive | Proactive (churn prediction, demand sensing) |
| **Feedback Loop** | Ad-hoc complaints | Systematic NPS / CSAT tracking |
| **Strategic Role** | Cost Centre | Profit Centre / Value Generator |
| **Technology Stack** | Minimal (CRM software absent) | AI, ML, IoT, Blockchain integration |
| **Food Industry Fit** | Poor (cannot handle perishability) | Strong (cold chain + traceability) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

> **Q1.** Define Customer Relationship Management (CRM) in the context of Logistics and Supply Chain Management. List the three types of CRM architecture.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Customer Relationship Management (CRM) in LSCM is the integrated strategic framework that uses information technology, data analytics, and process automation to manage all customer-facing supply chain activities — from order capture and fulfilment to post-sale service and reverse logistics — with the objective of building long-term, profitable customer relationships and delivering superior customer value.

**Three Types of CRM (1 Mark — 0.33 × 3):**
- **Operational CRM** — Automates and streamlines customer-facing business processes such as sales force automation, customer service, and order management.
- **Analytical CRM** — Uses data warehousing, OLAP, and data mining to analyse customer behaviour and generate actionable insights.
- **Collaborative CRM** — Integrates communication and information sharing across all departments and external partners (suppliers, distributors, retailers, customers).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **Q2.** What is Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)? State the ideal CLV:CAC ratio for a sustainable food retail business.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the total net profit a business can expect to generate from a customer over the entire duration of their relationship with the company. Mathematically, it is the present value of future cash flows attributed to the customer, discounted at an appropriate rate and adjusted for retention probability. The simplified formula is:

$$CLV = \dfrac{A \times f \times g \times (1+d)}{1+d-r}$$

where $A$ = Average Order Value, $f$ = Purchase Frequency, $g$ = Gross Margin, $d$ = Discount Rate, $r$ = Retention Rate.

**Ideal CLV:CAC Ratio (1 Mark):** The ideal **CLV:CAC ratio is ≥ 3:1** for sustainable growth. A ratio below 3:1 indicates over-spending on customer acquisition, while a ratio above 5:1 suggests under-investment in growth.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing generic marketing definitions of CRM without linking to **LSCM functions** (order fulfilment, distribution, reverse logistics). *Loss: Up to 3 marks.*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Omitting **quantitative metrics** like CLV, NPS, Churn Rate. *Loss: Up to 2 marks.*
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to mention **food-industry-specific CRM challenges** (perishability, cold chain, traceability, festival demand). *Loss: Up to 2 marks.*
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not structuring the answer with **headings and sub-points**. KTU examiners reward structured presentation with up to 1 extra mark.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **CRM with ERP or SCM** — they are distinct but interlinked. Always clarify the relationship in the first paragraph.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

> **Q.A)** *(a)* Explain the **IDIC model** of CRM in detail. Discuss its application in a food supply chain context. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* Discuss the role of **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** and **RFM segmentation** in designing customer-centric logistics strategies for a perishable food company. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — IDIC Model Explained (7 Marks)

The **IDIC Model**, proposed by **Don Peppers and Martha Rogers** in 1993, is one of the foundational frameworks for operationalising CRM. The acronym stands for **Identify, Differentiate, Interact, Customise**. Each stage is detailed below.

**1. IDENTIFY (1.5 Marks):**
The first step is to identify all current and potential customers and build a comprehensive customer database. In a food supply chain context, this means consolidating data from:
- Point-of-Sale (POS) systems at retail outlets.
- Loyalty card registrations at supermarkets.
- E-commerce purchase histories (e.g., BigBasket, Blinkit).
- B2B distributor orders.
- HoReCa (Hotel/Restaurant/Café) institutional purchase records.

**2. DIFFERENTIATE (1.5 Marks):**
Customers are differentiated based on their **value to the firm** and their **needs**. Two customers with similar purchase volumes may have vastly different profitability and strategic importance. In a food LSCM, differentiation could be based on:
- **CLV** (high-value vs. low-value customers).
- **Product preferences** (organic vs. conventional, vegetarian vs. non-vegetarian).
- **Service expectations** (express delivery tolerance, packaging preferences).
- **Geographic and climatic needs** (hill-region vs. coastal-distribution cold chain).

**3. INTERACT (1.5 Marks):**
The company interacts with each customer segment through their **preferred channels** — SMS, WhatsApp, email, in-app notifications, telephonic customer care, or in-person field visits. In food LSCM, interaction must be:
- **Timely** (recall alerts, expiry notifications).
- **Relevant** (festival-specific offers, region-specific recipes).
- **Two-way** (feedback collection, complaint redressal).

**4. CUSTOMISE (1.5 Marks):**
Based on the differentiated understanding, the company customises its offerings — products, pricing, delivery modes, and post-sale service. In a food supply chain, customisation can mean:
- Personalised subscription boxes for organic customers.
- Priority cold-chain slots for premium buyers.
- Tailored credit terms for small retailers.
- Co-branded packaging for institutional HoReCa clients.

**Closing the Loop (1 Mark):** The IDIC model is iterative — customised interactions generate new data, which feeds back into the Identify stage, continuously refining the CRM system.

---

#### Part (b) — CLV and RFM in Perishable Food Logistics (7 Marks)

**Role of CLV (3.5 Marks):**

CLV enables a perishable food company to **allocate logistics resources intelligently** across heterogeneous customer segments. For example, a dairy company using CLV analysis can:
- **Identify high-CLV customers** (urban, daily-milk subscribers) and assign them **express cold-chain delivery** with sub-2-hour windows, even if the per-unit delivery cost is higher.
- **Identify low-CLV occasional buyers** and serve them through **shared delivery routes** on alternate days, optimising cost-to-serve.
- **Justify investments** in cold-chain infrastructure (refrigerated vans, IoT temperature sensors) by mapping the **CLV uplift** to **service-level investments**.

**Role of RFM Segmentation (3.5 Marks):**

RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) provides a **behavioural segmentation framework** that is especially powerful in perishable food chains:
- **Champions (RFM 13–15):** Get first pick of freshest stock, exclusive access to limited-edition products, and priority delivery windows.
- **At-Risk (RFM 7–9):** Triggered for **win-back campaigns** — e.g., "We miss you! Enjoy 20% off your next order with free insulated packaging."
- **Hibernating (RFM 4–6):** Routed to **low-cost re-engagement channels** (email, SMS) with sample-size products to test reactivation.
- **Logistics Implication:** RFM data can be integrated with **TMS (Transport Management Systems)** to dynamically adjust delivery frequency, packaging type, and route priority — ensuring that the **freshest produce** reaches the **most valuable customers** within the **shortest time window**.

**Synthesis:** When CLV (a forward-looking value metric) is combined with RFM (a backward-looking behavioural metric), a food company achieves **value-based logistics orchestration** — a hallmark of mature CRM-LSCM integration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

> **Q.B)** *(a)* Discuss the **three types of CRM architecture** (Operational, Analytical, Collaborative) with suitable examples from the food industry. **(7 Marks)**
>
> *(b)* *"In the food supply chain, Customer Relationship Management is not a marketing function — it is a logistics imperative."* Critically analyse this statement with reference to **perishability, traceability, and cold chain integrity**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Three Types of CRM Architecture (7 Marks)

**1. Operational CRM (2.5 Marks):**
Operational CRM refers to the **automation and streamlining of customer-facing business processes**. It includes three sub-modules:
- **Sales Force Automation (SFA):** Automates lead tracking, order booking, and field sales visit scheduling. *Food LSCM Example:* A biscuit company's field sales representative uses a tablet-based SFA app to book orders from retail outlets during a visit, auto-updating the inventory and delivery schedule.
- **Customer Service and Support (CSS):** Manages customer queries, complaints, and service requests. *Example:* A 24×7 multilingual helpline for an organic food brand, integrated with a ticketing system that routes complaints to the nearest depot.
- **Enterprise Marketing Automation (EMA):** Automates campaign management, email marketing, and promotional offers. *Example:* An automated email campaign offering a discount on cheese during the Christmas season, triggered by customer purchase history.

**2. Analytical CRM (2.5 Marks):**
Analytical CRM leverages **data warehousing, OLAP, and data mining** to extract insights from customer data. *Food LSCM Examples:*
- **Demand Forecasting:** Analysing two years of festival sales (Onam, Vishu, Ramadan, Christmas) to predict next year's demand at the SKU-store level.
- **Churn Prediction:** Using logistic regression to identify small retailers likely to switch to a competitor distributor, enabling proactive intervention.
- **Market Basket Analysis:** Discovering that customers who buy basmati rice also buy ghee and dal within 7 days, enabling cross-sell bundling.
- **Customer Lifetime Value Modelling:** As derived in Section 3.1.2, quantifying long-term value to guide investment.

**3. Collaborative CRM (2 Marks):**
Collaborative CRM enables **information sharing across all internal departments and external stakeholders** through unified communication channels. *Food LSCM Examples:*
- **Distributor Portals:** Real-time order booking, inventory visibility, and promotional updates for distributors and retailers.
- **Social CRM:** Monitoring Twitter, Instagram, and Facebook for customer complaints and engaging publicly to build trust.
- **Omnichannel Integration:** A customer can start an order on the mobile app, modify it on the website, and collect it from a physical store — all with a single, consistent interaction history.

---

#### Part (b) — Critical Analysis of the Statement (7 Marks)

The statement *"In the food supply chain, CRM is not a marketing function — it is a logistics imperative"* captures a fundamental shift in modern food industry thinking. The analysis proceeds along three dimensions:

**1. Perishability (2.5 Marks):**
Food products have a **finite shelf life** — fresh dairy lasts days, leafy vegetables last a week, frozen foods last months, and shelf-stable packaged foods last longer. CRM in this context is *not* merely about sending offers; it is about ensuring that the **right product reaches the right customer in the right condition, within the right time window**. A delayed delivery of strawberries renders them unsellable, transforming a CRM-driven sale into a **logistics failure and a customer experience disaster**. Thus, CRM must dictate **delivery slot planning, dispatch prioritisation, and route optimisation** — all of which are core logistics functions. A "marketing-only" CRM that offers a discount on a perishable product but cannot ensure timely delivery actively destroys customer trust.

**2. Traceability (2.5 Marks):**
Modern food safety regulations (FSSAI in India, FSMA in the US, EU Regulation 178/2002) require **end-to-end traceability** — from farm to fork. When a contamination incident occurs (e.g., the 2013 horsemeat scandal in Europe, or recurring foodborne illness outbreaks globally), the CRM system must:
- **Instantly identify** all customers who purchased the affected batch.
- **Notify them** via SMS, email, and push notifications.
- **Coordinate reverse logistics** for product recall and refund.
- **Document every interaction** for regulatory compliance.

This is a **logistics-led, not marketing-led, activity**. Marketing-driven CRM focuses on segmentation and offers; traceability-driven CRM focuses on **batch tracking, rapid recall, and crisis communication** — all deeply embedded in supply chain operations.

**3. Cold Chain Integrity (2 Marks):**
For temperature-sensitive products (dairy, meat, seafood, frozen foods, pharmaceuticals-adjacent items), CRM must communicate **real-time cold chain status** to customers. Modern CRM platforms integrate with **IoT temperature sensors** in refrigerated trucks and warehouses, allowing customers to verify that their product was maintained at the correct temperature throughout transit. A customer who receives a "Your Amul ice cream was maintained at -22°C throughout delivery" notification develops **deep, data-backed trust** in the brand — a trust that pure marketing communications cannot replicate. This trust directly translates into **higher retention, lower churn, and increased CLV**.

**Conclusion:** The statement is **largely valid** in the modern food industry context. While marketing remains a CRM stakeholder, the **operational backbone of CRM in food LSCM is undeniably logistics** — the function that physically delivers on the brand promise. Firms that treat CRM as purely a marketing tool often suffer from a "promise-delivery gap," while firms that integrate CRM with logistics operations achieve **sustainable competitive advantage** and **superior customer lifetime value**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CRM in LSCM** is the integrated management of customer interactions across the entire supply chain — from procurement to last-mile delivery and reverse logistics.
- The **three pillars** are: **Operational** (process automation), **Analytical** (data-driven intelligence), **Collaborative** (cross-channel communication). Mnemonic: **PIC**.
- The **IDIC model** (Peppers & Rogers) comprises: **Identify → Differentiate → Interact → Customise**.
- **Key Metrics to Memorise:**
  - **CLV** = $\dfrac{A \times f \times g \times (1+d)}{1+d-r}$
  - **CAC** = Total Marketing Spend ÷ New Customers
  - **CLV:CAC Ratio** ≥ 3:1 is healthy.
  - **NPS** = % Promoters − % Detractors (range −100 to +100).
  - **Churn Rate** = (Customers Lost ÷ Customers at Start) × 100.
  - **RFM Score** ranges 3–15; ≥13 = Champions.
- **Food-Specific CRM Imperatives:** Perishability, Traceability, Cold Chain Integrity, Festival Demand Spikes, Sensory Quality Consistency.
- **RFM Buckets (in order of priority):** Champions (13–15) → Loyal (10–12) → Potential Loyalists (7–9) → At-Risk (4–6) → Lost (3).
- **SERVQUAL Gap 5:** Perception − Expectation. Negative = service failure.
- **Payne's Five Process Model:** Strategy → Value → Process → Technology → People.
- **Customer Equity (CE):** Sum of all customers' CLV in the database.
- **Reverse Logistics in Food CRM:** Mandatory for product recall, expiry management, and sustainability goals.
- **Technology Stack Layers (bottom-up):** Logistics (WMS/TMS/IoT) → Integration (APIs/EDI/Blockchain) → Data (CDW) → Analytics (ML/OLAP) → Application (SFA/CSS/EMA) → Presentation (Web/App/Social).
- **NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Alignment:** Always integrate **sustainability, ethics, and digital transformation** (AI, IoT, Blockchain) into your CRM-LSCM answers.
- **Common Exam Triggers:** "Explain CRM in LSCM," "Discuss IDIC model," "Differentiate Operational/Analytical/Collaborative CRM," "Compute CLV," "Design a CRM strategy for [a food company]," "Critically analyse CRM in perishable food chains."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always **end your 14-mark answer with a concluding paragraph** that synthesises logistics and customer-relationship perspectives. Examiners explicitly award **1 mark** for synthesis and forward-looking insight. Phrases like *"In the era of hyper-personalisation, CRM in food LSCM is evolving from a cost centre to a strategic value generator"* earn bonus impression marks.
